Note that the masked-softmax σ𝑴​(⋅)subscript𝜎𝑴⋅\sigma_{\bm{M}}(\cdot) operator only computes unmasked terms, ensuring that each (i,j)𝑖𝑗(i,j)-th attention score survives as nonzero if and only if 𝑴i​j=1subscript𝑴𝑖𝑗1\bm{M}_{ij}=1. This is thus equivalent to filling in the (i,j)𝑖𝑗(i,j)-th attention score with −∞-\infty if 𝑴i​j=0subscript𝑴𝑖𝑗0\bm{M}_{ij}=0, then applying the standard softmax operator. Most sparsity-based efficient Transformers fall under this formulation, while using different methods to either manually fix or learn the mask 𝑴𝑴\bm{M}. For instance, local attention (9, 3, 51) with a sliding window sets 𝑴i​j=1subscript𝑴𝑖𝑗1\bm{M}_{ij}=1 if |i−j|<c𝑖𝑗𝑐|i-j|<c for some context window size c𝑐c while Reformer  sets 𝑴i​j=1subscript𝑴𝑖𝑗1\bm{M}_{ij}=1 if 𝑸isubscript𝑸𝑖\bm{Q}_{i} and 𝑲jsubscript𝑲𝑗\bm{K}_{j} are hashed into the same bucket.

answer: Local attention is a Transformer model that uses a sliding window of some fixed context window size [11].

input: Will these embeddings be based on measuring similarities between features of new faces and features extracted from faces which the model was trained on ?
question: Will these embeddings be based on measuring similarities between features of new faces and features extracted from faces which the model was trained on ?
output:
Yes, the embeddings will be based on measuring similarities between features of new faces and features extracted from faces the model was trained on.

The core of the method involves using a deep convolutional network to learn a "Euclidean embedding per image" [1]. This network is trained so that the squared L2 distances in this embedding space directly represent face similarity: faces belonging to the same person will have small distances, while faces belonging to different people will have large distances [1].

When using this system for tasks like verification or recognition, a new face's embedding will be compared against the embeddings of known faces (those from the training set) using these distance metrics [1].

ctxs.title: FaceNet: A Unified Embedding for Face Recognition and Clustering
ctxs.text: In this paper we present a unified system for face verification (is this the same person), recognition (who is this person) and clustering (find common people among these faces). Our method is based on learning a Euclidean embedding per image using a deep convolutional network. The network is trained such that the squared L2 distances in the embedding space directly correspond to face similarity: faces of the same person have small distances and faces of distinct people have large distances.

answer: FaceNet embeddings can be used to measure similarity between new faces and trained faces [0].

input: How much the computational complexity was reduced when using depthwise separable convolution?
question: How much the computational complexity was reduced when using depthwise separable convolution?
output: The computational complexity was reduced by **between 8 to 9 times** when using depthwise separable convolutions in MobileNet compared to standard convolutions, while only incurring a small reduction in accuracy [1].

ctxs.title: MobileNets: Efficient Convolutional Neural Networks for Mobile Vision Applications
ctxs.text: MobileNet uses 3×3333\times 3 depthwise separable convolutions which uses between 8 to 9 times less computation than standard convolutions at only a small reduction in accuracy as seen in Section 4.

answer: 3\times 3 depthwise separable convolutions use 8–9 times less computation than standard convolutions [25].

input: RoBERTa is based on BERT-large or BERT base?
question: RoBERTa is based on BERT-large or BERT base?
output:
RoBERTa, as described in this excerpt, is based on the **BERT-large** architecture [1].

The excerpt specifies that RoBERTa is trained following the BERT-large architecture, which has the following configuration:
*   $L=24$ (Layers)

*   $A=16$ (Attention heads)

ctxs.title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach
ctxs.text: To help disentangle the importance of these factors from other modeling choices (e.g., the pretraining objective), we begin by training RoBERTa following the BERTlargelarge{}_{\textsc{large}} architecture (L=24𝐿24L=24, H=1024𝐻1024H=1024, A=16𝐴16A=16, 355M parameters). We pretrain for 100K steps over a comparable BookCorpus plus Wikipedia dataset as was used in Devlin et al. (2019). We pretrain our model using 1024 V100 GPUs for approximately one day.

answer: RoBERTa is based on BERT-large [49].

input: How should positional information be interpreted or captured with sines and cosines?
question: How should positional information be interpreted or captured with sines and cosines?
output:
Positional information should be captured using sinusoidal functions, where each dimension of the positional encoding corresponds to a sinusoid [1].

The key aspects of this sinusoidal encoding are:

*   **Wavelength Progression:** The wavelengths of these sinusoids form a geometric progression, ranging from $2\pi$ to $10000 \cdot 2\pi$ [1].
*   **Relative Position Encoding:** This sinusoidal choice was made because it is hypothesized to enable the model to easily learn to attend based on relative positions. Specifically, for any fixed offset $k$, the positional encoding at position $pos+k$ ($\text{PE}_{pos+k}$) can be represented as a linear function of the positional encoding at position $pos$ ($\text{PE}_{pos}$) [1].
*   **Extrapolation Capability:** Compared to using learned positional embeddings, the sinusoidal version offers the potential advantage of allowing the model to extrapolate to sequence lengths that are longer than those seen during training [2].

ctxs.title: Attention Is All You Need
ctxs.text: where p​o​s𝑝𝑜𝑠pos is the position and i𝑖i is the dimension. That is, each dimension of the positional encoding corresponds to a sinusoid. The wavelengths form a geometric progression from 2​π2𝜋2\pi to 10000⋅2​π⋅100002𝜋10000\cdot 2\pi. We chose this function because we hypothesized it would allow the model to easily learn to attend by relative positions, since for any fixed offset k𝑘k, P​Ep​o​s+k𝑃subscript𝐸𝑝𝑜𝑠𝑘PE_{pos+k} can be represented as a linear function of P​Ep​o​s𝑃subscript𝐸𝑝𝑜𝑠PE_{pos}.

ctxs.title: Attention Is All You Need
ctxs.text: We also experimented with using learned positional embeddings  instead, and found that the two versions produced nearly identical results (see Table 3 row (E)). We chose the sinusoidal version because it may allow the model to extrapolate to sequence lengths longer than the ones encountered during training.

answer: Positional encoding can be generated using sinusoidal function whose wavelengths form a geometric progression which can encode relative positions [31]. Advantage of the sinusoidal positional encoding is that it may allow the model to extrapolate to sequence lengths longer than the ones encountered during training [32].

input: Why doesn't character-level embeddings degrade performance compared to word-level embeddings?
question: Why doesn't character-level embeddings degrade performance compared to word-level embeddings?
output:
The provided excerpt does not explicitly state why character-level embeddings do not degrade performance compared to word-level embeddings.

However, the text does describe the *benefits* of using character-level features, which implies an advantage over traditional word embeddings:

*   **Smoother and Compact Parametrization:** Character-level features allow for a "smoother and compact parametrization of the word embeddings" [1].
*   **Parameter Reduction (in the Softmax Layer):** The proposed extension aims to "reduce the number of parameters of the Softmax layer" by generating the word embedding ($\mathbf{e}_w$) using a CNN over the characters of the word ($\mathbf{e}_w = \text{CNN}(\text{chars}_w)$) instead of using a large matrix of $|V| \times |h|$ parameters [1].

While the excerpt focuses on the *mechanism* of using CNNs over characters to improve parameter efficiency, it does not offer a direct comparison explaining why this approach avoids performance degradation compared to word-level embeddings.

ctxs.title: Exploring the Limits of Language Modeling
ctxs.text: The character-level features allow for a smoother and compact parametrization of the word embeddings. Recent efforts on small scale language modeling have used CNN character embeddings for the input embeddings (Kim et al., 2015). Although not as straightforward, we propose an extension to this idea to also reduce the number of parameters of the Softmax layer. Recall from Section 2.3 that the Softmax computes a logit as zw=hT​ewsubscript𝑧𝑤superscriptℎ𝑇subscript𝑒𝑤z_{w}=h^{T}e_{w} where hℎh is a context vector and ewsubscript𝑒𝑤e_{w} the word embedding. Instead of building a matrix of |V|×|h|𝑉ℎ|V|\times|h| (whose rows correspond to ewsubscript𝑒𝑤e_{w}), we produce ewsubscript𝑒𝑤e_{w} with a CNN over the characters of w𝑤w as ew=C​N​N​(c​h​a​r​sw)subscript𝑒𝑤𝐶𝑁𝑁𝑐ℎ𝑎𝑟subscript𝑠𝑤e_{w}=CNN(chars_{w}) – we call this a CNN Softmax. We used the same network architecture to dynamically generate the Softmax word embeddings without sharing the parameters with the input word-embedding sub-network. For inference, the vectors ewsubscript𝑒𝑤e_{w} can be precomputed, so there is no computational complexity increase w.r.t. the regular Softmax.

answer: The paper discusses one advantage of character-level embeddings over word-level embeddings [28].

input: Define how fANOVA is performed?
question: Define how fANOVA is performed?
output:
fANOVA is a framework designed to assess hyperparameter importance [1]. It operates on the principle that marginalizing over different dimensions of the hyperparameter space can be performed efficiently using regression trees [1].

Specifically, this efficiency allows the framework to:

*   Predict the marginal error for a single hyperparameter while simultaneously averaging over all the other hyperparameters [1].

This approach offers a significant advantage over traditional methods, as it allows the hyperparameter space to be sampled randomly, rather than requiring a complete hyperparameter grid search [1].

ctxs.title: LSTM: A Search Space Odyssey
ctxs.text: The fANOVA framework for assessing hyperparameter importance by Hutter et al.  is based on the observation that marginalizing over dimensions can be done efficiently in regression trees. This allows predicting the marginal error for one hyperparameter while averaging over all the others. Traditionally this would require a full hyperparameter grid search, whereas here the hyperparameter space can be sampled at random.

answer: fANOVA marginalize over hyperparameter dimensions using regression trees to predict the marginal error for single parameter while averaging over all other parameters [47].

input: Which part of the proposed model helps to solve the sparsity problem?
question: Which part of the proposed model helps to solve the sparsity problem?
output:
Several parts of the proposed model contribute to solving the sparsity problem:

1. **The Autoencoder's Encoding of Retention:** The autoencoder is specifically designed to handle retention data, which is described as being in the form of "high-dimensional sparse features" [1]. This autoencoder encodes this retention into a low-dimensional dense encoding, $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model}}$, which is then fed into the transformer encoder [1].

2. **App Embeddings Based on App ID and Category ID:** The design of the app embeddings helps overcome sparsity. The embeddings are built using both the specific **app ID** and its corresponding **category ID** [2]. This allows the model to retain valid information even if the usage of a specific app is "gravely sparse," because the category information can still provide context [2].

3. **Weight Matrix Tying:** The model addresses sparsity by tying the weight matrices across multiple components: the input and output of the retention autoencoder, the input of the transformer encoder, the output for masked app prediction, the output of the transformer decoder, and the reconstruction part of retention from the user embeddings (the bottleneck) [3]. By setting $\mathbf{W}^{\Omega}=\mathbf{W}^{\Theta}=\mathbf{W}^{\Phi}=\mathbf{W}^{(4)}=\mathbf{W}^{a\mathrm{T}}$, the model reduces the total number of parameters, which helps in overcoming the problem of sparsity [3].

ctxs.title: general-purpose user embeddings based on mobile app usage
ctxs.text: Secondly, this autoencoder provides effective representations of user retention for the transformer part. The transformer encoder part needs to be fed with the retention for compressing long-term interests into user embeddings. However, retention is originally in the form of high-dimensional sparse features. This autoencoder encodes retention into the first hidden layer 𝒙(1)∈ℝdm​o​d​e​lsuperscript𝒙1superscriptℝsubscript𝑑𝑚𝑜𝑑𝑒𝑙\bm{x}^{(1)}\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{model}}. As a low-dimensional dense encoding, 𝒙(1)superscript𝒙1\bm{x}^{(1)} plays an important role in the transformer encoder part.

ctxs.title: general-purpose user embeddings based on mobile app usage
ctxs.text: We carefully design our weight matrices for several parts of the model, which helps to solve the sparsity problem and tightly couple the autoencoder part and the transformer parts. As shown in Figure 5, the app embeddings are built based on both the app ID and its corresponding category ID. Even if the usage of some app is gravely sparse, its category can still provide valid information. This setting helps to overcome the problem of sparsity.

ctxs.title: general-purpose user embeddings based on mobile app usage
ctxs.text: As introduced previously, we repeatedly use the M×dm​o​d​e​l𝑀subscript𝑑𝑚𝑜𝑑𝑒𝑙M\times d_{model} embedding matrix for apps, i.e., at the input and output of the retention autoencoder, the input of the transformer encoder, the output for the masked app prediction, the output of the transformer decoder, as well as the reconstruction part for retention from the user embeddings (bottleneck). We tie the weight matrices of all these parts together, i.e.,   (3)  𝐖Ω=𝐖Θ=𝐖Φ=𝐖(4)=𝐖aT.superscript𝐖Ωsuperscript𝐖Θsuperscript𝐖Φsuperscript𝐖4superscriptsuperscript𝐖𝑎T\displaystyle\mathbf{W}^{\Omega}=\mathbf{W}^{\Theta}=\mathbf{W}^{\Phi}=\mathbf{W}^{(4)}={\mathbf{W}^{a}}^{\mathrm{T}}.    We reduce the total number of parameters by tying weight matrices of the above layers, which benefits of overcoming the problem of sparsity. Moreover, weight tying benefits the backpropagation of the gradient and speeds the convergence.

answer: Both transformers and retention autoencoder parts try to solve the sparsity problem by using tied weight matrices [27]. The reason of this is that \mathbf{W}^{\Omega}=\mathbf{W}^{\Theta}=\mathbf{W}^{\Phi}=\mathbf{W}^{(4)}={\mathbf{W}^{a}}^{\mathrm{T}} [38]. Since \mathbf{W}^{\Omega} is used in transformer part and \mathbf{W}^{(4)} is used in autoencoder part, both of them try to solve the sparsity problem [39].

input: Why the authors experiment on an NPU simulator, not the real hardware chip?
question: Why the authors experiment on an NPU simulator, not the real hardware chip?
output:
The authors experimented on an NPU simulator rather than a real hardware chip because, at the time of the writing, the MIDAP architecture had not yet been implemented as a physical hardware chip [1].

However, the use of the cycle-accurate simulator allowed them to achieve several key objectives:

*   **Accurate Latency Estimation:** The simulator allowed them to build an accurate analytical model for estimating the end-to-end latency [1].
*   **Modeling Complex Interactions:** Specifically, the cycle-accurate simulator considered factors like DRAM access contention and parameterized DRAM access delay, which is crucial for accurate performance modeling [1].

In summary, the simulator served as a necessary tool to enable detailed performance analysis and latency estimation before the physical hardware implementation was complete [1].

ctxs.title: S3NAS: Fast NPU-aware Neural Architecture Search Methodology
ctxs.text: Another good feature of MIDAP is that it efficiently supports the following operations that would lower the MAC (multiply-accumulate) utilization in other NPUs that have many MAC units: pooling, DWConv, and squeeze-and-excitation (SE). For DWConv operation, it does not use an adder tree but an alternative hardware logic that consists of a set of individual accumulators connected to the multiply units. For pooling and SE operations, reduction logic is included in the pipeline. Note that MIDAP has not been implemented as a real hardware chip yet but as a virtual prototype with a cycle-accurate simulator. Thanks to the cycle-accurate simulator that considers the DRAM access contention and parametrized DRAM access delay, we could build an accurate analytical model for end-to-end latency estimation, based on the profiling result with the simulator.

ctxs.title: S3NAS: Fast NPU-aware Neural Architecture Search Methodology
ctxs.text: We evaluate the proposed NAS technique for image classification with the ImageNet dataset. The current implementation is made for MIDAP (Kang et al., 2019) that can perform DWConv and SE operations efficiently so that MBConv is preferred to full 3-D convolution as the basic building block, as explained above. Latencies on the target NPU are obtained with the cycle-accurate simulator222https://github.com/cap-lab/MidapSim.

answer: MIDAP can support DWConv, SE more efficiently than other NPUs [27]. However, MIDAP is not implemented as a real hardware chip yet, but the cycle-accurate simulator is open-sourced [59].

input: How many terms are used for the loss function of a generator?
question: How many terms are used for the loss function of a generator?
output:
The loss function for the generator ($\mathcal{L}_G$) uses **three** terms [1].

The generator's objective function is defined as:
$$\mathcal{L}_{G}=\mathcal{L}_{adv}+{\lambda}_{cls}\thinspace\mathcal{L}_{cls}^{f}+{\lambda}_{rec}\thinspace\mathcal{L}_{rec}$$ [1]

These three terms are:
1. $\mathcal{L}_{adv}$ (the adversarial loss) [1]
2. ${\lambda}_{cls}\thinspace\mathcal{L}_{cls}^{f}$ (the domain classification loss, weighted by $\lambda_{cls}$) [1]
3. ${\lambda}_{rec}\thinspace\mathcal{L}_{rec}$ (the reconstruction loss, weighted by $\lambda_{rec}$) [1]

input: Why is it necessary to maximize the coverage of anchor points?
question: Why is it necessary to maximize the coverage of anchor points?
output:
It is necessary to maximize the coverage of anchor points to allow for **diverse transformations** [2].

This is achieved by using the **Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) algorithm** to select the anchor points ($\mathcal{P}^{\mathcal{A}}\subset\mathcal{P}$) [2]. FPS works by randomly selecting the first point and then subsequently choosing the points that are farthest away from the previously selected points, which maximizes the overall coverage of the anchor points [2].

ctxs.title: Point cloud augmentation with weighted local transformations
ctxs.text: We present a simple yet effective point cloud augmentation with weighted local transformations (PointWOLF). Our method generates deformation for point clouds by a convex combination of multiple transformations with smoothly varying weights. PointWOLF first selects several anchor points and locates random local transformations (e.g., similarity transformations) at the anchor points. Based on the distance from a point in the input to the anchor points, our method differentially applies the local transformations. The smoothly varying weights based on the distance to the anchor points allow spatially continuous augmentation and generate realistic samples. Our framework can be viewed as a kernel regression with transformations.

ctxs.title: Point cloud augmentation with weighted local transformations
ctxs.text: Sampling anchor points is the first step of our framework to locate multiple local transformations. To minimize the redundancy between local transformations, the anchor points 𝒫𝒜⊂𝒫superscript𝒫𝒜𝒫\mathcal{P}^{\mathcal{A}}\subset\mathcal{P} are selected by the Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) algorithm. FPS randomly chooses the first point and then sequentially chooses the farthest points from previous points. This maximizes the coverage of anchor points and allows diverse transformations.

answer: Maximizing the coverage of anchor points is necessary in order to ensure that the local transformations are being applied evenly across the entire input space [10]. This allows for a more diverse set of augmented samples to be generated, which can help to improve the robustness and generalization of a model trained on the augmented data [11].

input: What is the impact of number of training videos on the performance of supervised and unsupervised tasks?
question: What is the impact of number of training videos on the performance of supervised and unsupervised tasks?
output:
The provided excerpt focuses on comparing the performance of different models—specifically, a single frame classifier (logistic regression), a baseline LSTM classifier, and an LSTM classifier initialized with weights from a "Composite Model"—when varying the number of labeled videos used for training. The comparison highlights the impact of the labeled dataset size on the performance improvement gained by using unsupervised learning.

Here is a detailed breakdown of the impact:

* **Impact of Few Training Examples (Small Labeled Dataset):**
    * When the number of labeled videos is very small, unsupervised learning provides a **substantial improvement** in performance.
    * **Example (UCF-101):** Training on only one labeled video resulted in a performance improvement from 29.6% to 34.3% [1].
    * **Example (HMDB-51):** Training on only one video per class resulted in an improvement from 14.4% to 19.1% [1].

* **Impact as the Labeled Dataset Grows (Larger Labeled Dataset):**
    * As the size of the labeled dataset increases, the magnitude of the performance improvement gained from the unsupervised learning approach becomes **smaller**.
    * **Example (UCF-101 - Full Dataset):** Even when using the full UCF-101 dataset, there was still a considerable improvement, moving from 74.5% to 75.8% [1].
    * **Example (HMDB-51 - Full Dataset):** For the full HMDB-51 dataset (70 videos per class), the improvement was from 42.8% to 44.0% [1].

In summary, the unsupervised learning technique offers the most significant relative gains when the amount of labeled training data is scarce, but these gains tend to diminish as the labeled dataset becomes larger.

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Video Representations using LSTMs
ctxs.text: Fig. 12 compares three models - single frame classifier (logistic regression), baseline LSTM classifier and the LSTM classifier initialized with weights from the Composite Model as the number of labelled videos per class is varied. Note that having one labelled video means having many labelled 16 frame blocks. We can see that for the case of very few training examples, unsupervised learning gives a substantial improvement. For example, for UCF-101, the performance improves from 29.6% to 34.3% when training on only one labelled video. As the size of the labelled dataset grows, the improvement becomes smaller. Even for the full UCF-101 dataset we still get a considerable improvement from 74.5% to 75.8%. On HMDB-51, the improvement is from 42.8% to 44.0% for the full dataset (70 videos per class) and 14.4% to 19.1% for one video per class. Although, the improvement in classification by using unsupervised learning was not as big as we expected, we still managed to yield an additional improvement over a strong baseline. We discuss some avenues for improvements later.

answer: As the number of training videos increases the performance of supervised and unsupervised tasks increases [37].

input: What is used for measure the quantities of non-English data?
question: What is used for measure the quantities of non-English data?
output:
The provided excerpts do not specify a single, definitive metric used to "measure the quantities" of non-English data in a quantitative sense across all aspects of the research. However, they do provide several ways in which the presence and proportion of non-English data are described:

* **Percentage of Total Data:** The excerpts quantify the presence of non-English text by stating the percentage of the total training data that belongs to specific languages. For instance, the largest individual languages after English accounted for **0.01%, 0.15%, and 0.05%** of the BERT, RoBERTa, and T5 training data, respectively [1].
* **Presence of Non-English Lines:** In a closer analysis, researchers examined a **random subset (200 per corpus)** of lines predicted as non-English by a language classifier [2].
* **Categorization of Non-English Content:** The non-English lines were then categorized into six types based on their content:
    * **NE:** Containing only non-English language text [2].
    * **BiL:** Containing both English and non-English text (bilingual) [2].
    * **Trans.:** Containing English and non-English data that are translations of each other [2].
    * **Ent.:** Primarily English but containing non-English entities [2].

In summary, the quantity is measured both by the **proportion of the entire dataset** belonging to certain languages [1] and by the **subset analysis** of lines classified as non-English [2].

ctxs.title: Language Contamination Helps Explain the Cross-lingual Capabilities of English Pretrained Models
ctxs.text: We also see that non-English text makes up small percentages of the overall data, though this still leads to millions of tokens in large datasets. The largest individual languages after English only make up 0.01%, 0.15%, and 0.05% of the BERT, RoBERTa, and T5 training data, respectively. Multilingual pretraining work has shown that models generalize to new languages from varying amounts of data Delvin (2019); Lample and Conneau (2019); Conneau et al. (2020); however, these approaches intentionally select data across languages, and most upsample low-resource languages during training. Without these considerations, it is an open question how well the models trained on these relatively small amounts of non-English data generalize.

ctxs.title: Language Contamination Helps Explain the Cross-lingual Capabilities of English Pretrained Models
ctxs.text: We also perform a closer analysis on a random subset (200 per corpus) of non-English lines predicted by the language classifier (Table 2.1). Each example is manually coded into one of six categories. The first set covers various kinds of foreign language data: NE, where the line contains only non-English language text; BiL, or bilingual, where the line contains both English and non-English text; Trans., in which the English and non-English data that are translations of each other; and Ent., where the line is primarily English but contains non-English entities. The last two codes pertain to errors made by the language classifier: En., where the line only contains English text, and XX, which refers to lines that contain no natural language.

answer: Automatic language identification and manual qualitative analysis measure non-English data [10]. They are denominated in lines, tokens, and percentages across the paper [11].

input: In models inserting token expression, ([CLS],x1,...,xN,[SEP],y1,...,yM,[EOS]) calculate maximum value of N + M in RoBERTa case.
question: In models inserting token expression, ([CLS],x1,...,xN,[SEP],y1,...,yM,[EOS]) calculate maximum value of N + M in RoBERTa case.
output:
Based on the provided excerpts, the maximum value of $N+M$ is constrained by the maximum sequence length parameter $T$.

In the general BERT input format described in [1], the constraint is:
$$M+N < T$$
where $T$ is the parameter controlling the maximum sequence length during training [1].

However, when applying the specific modifications described for RoBERTa in [2], the context changes:
1. The input sequence is formed by concatenating a **question**, an **answer**, and a **passage**.
2. The total length of these sequences is truncated so that the total length is at most **512 tokens** [2].

While the notation $(\text{CLS}), x_1, \ldots, x_N, (\text{SEP}), y_1, \ldots, y_M, (\text{EOS})$ from [1] is used to describe the structure, the specific task described in [2] (question-answer-passage) implies a different structure than the two-segment input described in [1] (where $x$ and $y$ are two distinct segments).

If we strictly interpret the question as asking for the maximum value of $N+M$ based on the constraints mentioned in the excerpts:

* **General BERT Constraint:** $N+M < T$ [1].
* **RoBERTa Task Constraint:** The total length is limited to 512 tokens [2].

Since the RoBERTa task described in [2] imposes a hard limit of 512 tokens on the total sequence length, and this limit is more specific than the general $T$ parameter mentioned in [1], the maximum value of the combined lengths ($N+M$ if they map directly to the token counts in the final sequence) is constrained by this limit.

**Conclusion:**

The maximum value of $N+M$ is constrained by the maximum sequence length parameter, which is specified as **512 tokens** in the RoBERTa task description [2].

ctxs.title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach
ctxs.text: BERT takes as input a concatenation of two segments (sequences of tokens), x1,…,xNsubscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑁x_{1},\ldots,x_{N} and y1,…,yMsubscript𝑦1…subscript𝑦𝑀y_{1},\ldots,y_{M}. Segments usually consist of more than one natural sentence. The two segments are presented as a single input sequence to BERT with special tokens delimiting them: (𝐶𝐿𝑆),x1,…,xN,(𝑆𝐸𝑃),y1,…,yM,(𝐸𝑂𝑆)delimited-()𝐶𝐿𝑆subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑁delimited-()𝑆𝐸𝑃subscript𝑦1…subscript𝑦𝑀delimited-()𝐸𝑂𝑆(\mathit{CLS}),x_{1},\ldots,x_{N},(\mathit{SEP}),y_{1},\ldots,y_{M},(\mathit{EOS}). M𝑀M and N𝑁N are constrained such that M+N<T𝑀𝑁𝑇M+N<T, where T𝑇T is a parameter that controls the maximum sequence length during training.

ctxs.title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach
ctxs.text: We modify RoBERTa for this task by concatenating each candidate answer with the corresponding question and passage. We then encode each of these four sequences and pass the resulting (CLS) representations through a fully-connected layer, which is used to predict the correct answer. We truncate question-answer pairs that are longer than 128 tokens and, if needed, the passage so that the total length is at most 512 tokens.

answer: It is not true [5]. BERT takes concatenated two sequences as input like [\mathit{CLS}],x_{1},\ldots,x_{N},[\mathit{SEP}],y_{1},\ldots,y_{M}, They calculate N+M to control maximum sequence length [67].

input: Why there is no need to label objects in videos for the encoder-decoder model.
question: Why there is no need to label objects in videos for the encoder-decoder model.
output:
The provided text excerpt does not explicitly state why there is no need to label objects in videos for the encoder-decoder model. However, it describes the model as being part of an **unsupervised learning** framework [1].

The core idea of the model, as described, is that the learning signal is driven by the structure of the task and the model's architecture, not by human-provided labels. The text emphasizes the importance of choosing the right objective function and inductive biases to guide the learning process [1].

The two primary ways the model is trained (and thus, why labels aren't needed) are:

1. **Predicting the input sequence (Autoencoder-like):** The model attempts to reproduce the input sequence using the learned representation, similar to an autoencoder [1].
2. **Predicting future frames:** The model attempts to extrapolate motion and appearance beyond the observed frames using the learned representation [1].

Since the model is designed to learn representations based on inherent temporal relationships (like predicting the next state based on the current state, which enforces a consistent "physics of the world" [1]), it does not require pre-labeled objects to function or learn useful features [1].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Video Representations using LSTMs
ctxs.text: When designing any unsupervised learning model, it is crucial to have the right inductive biases and choose the right objective function so that the learning signal points the model towards learning useful features. In this paper, we use the LSTM Encoder-Decoder framework to learn video representations. The key inductive bias here is that the same operation must be applied at each time step to propagate information to the next step. This enforces the fact that the physics of the world remains the same, irrespective of input. The same physics acting on any state, at any time, must produce the next state. Our model works as follows. The Encoder LSTM runs through a sequence of frames to come up with a representation. This representation is then decoded through another LSTM to produce a target sequence. We consider different choices of the target sequence. One choice is to predict the same sequence as the input. The motivation is similar to that of autoencoders – we wish to capture all that is needed to reproduce the input but at the same time go through the inductive biases imposed by the model. Another option is to predict the future frames. Here the motivation is to learn a representation that extracts all that is needed to extrapolate the motion and appearance beyond what has been observed. These two natural choices can also be combined. In this case, there are two decoder LSTMs – one that decodes the representation into the input sequence and another that decodes the same representation to predict the future.

answer: Since representation is another form of input, that's why it doesn't need label for any purpose [3].

input: How has deep learning improved the accuracy of face recognition systems compared to traditional methods?
question: How has deep learning improved the accuracy of face recognition systems compared to traditional methods?
output:
Deep learning has led to a dramatic improvement in the accuracy of face recognition (FR) systems compared to traditional methods.

**Limitations of Traditional Methods:**
Traditional methods, which typically relied on one or two layer representations such as filtering responses, histogram of feature codes, or distribution of dictionary atoms, improved FR accuracy very slowly [1]. Furthermore, these older approaches tended to address only one aspect of unconstrained facial changes—like lighting, pose, expression, or disguise—without an integrated technique to handle these challenges holistically [1]. Despite continuous efforts spanning over a decade, these "shallow" methods only managed to bring the accuracy on the LFW benchmark up to about 95%, indicating they were insufficient for extracting stable identity features invariant to real-world variations [1]. Consequently, these systems often performed unreliably in real-world applications, leading to numerous false alarms [1].

**Advancements with Deep Learning:**
The adoption of deep learning architectures, combined with advanced training techniques like batch normalization (BN), allowed networks to become deeper and training to become more controllable [2]. This led to a rapid and significant boost in accuracy.

Key milestones demonstrating this improvement include:
*   **DeepFace (2014):** This method used a nine-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and achieved an accuracy of 97.35% on the LFW benchmark, which was noted as approaching human performance (97.53%) for the first time under unconstrained conditions [2, 3].
*   **Subsequent Rapid Gains:** Following this breakthrough, research focus shifted to deep-learning-based approaches, and accuracy was dramatically boosted to above 99.80% within just three years [3].
*   **FaceNet (2015):** This approach utilized a large private dataset to train a GoogleNet and achieved a good performance of 99.63% by employing a triplet loss function based on triplets of matching/nonmatching face patches [2].
*   **VGGface (2015):** This method trained a VGGNet on a large-scale internet dataset and fine-tuned it using a triplet loss function similar to FaceNet, obtaining an accuracy of 98.95% [2].
*   **SphereFace (2017):** Using a 64-layer ResNet architecture, SphereFace proposed the angular softmax (A-Softmax) loss to learn discriminative

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: In general, traditional methods attempted to recognize human face by one or two layer representations, such as filtering responses, histogram of the feature codes, or distribution of the dictionary atoms. The research community studied intensively to separately improve the preprocessing, local descriptors, and feature transformation, but these approaches improved FR accuracy slowly. What’s worse, most methods aimed to address one aspect of unconstrained facial changes only, such as lighting, pose, expression, or disguise. There was no any integrated technique to address these unconstrained challenges integrally. As a result, with continuous efforts of more than a decade, “shallow” methods only improved the accuracy of the LFW benchmark to about 95% , which indicates that “shallow” methods are insufficient to extract stable identity feature invariant to real-world changes. Due to the insufficiency of this technical, facial recognition systems were often reported with unstable performance or failures with countless false alarms in real-world applications.

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: With the evolved architectures and advanced training techniques, such as batch normalization (BN), the network becomes deeper and the training becomes more controllable. Following these architectures in object classification, the networks in deep FR are also developed step by step, and the performance of deep FR is continually improving. We present these mainstream architectures of deep FR in Fig. 9. In 2014, DeepFace  was the first to use a nine-layer CNN with several locally connected layers. With 3D alignment for face processing, it reaches an accuracy of 97.35% on LFW. In 2015, FaceNet  used a large private dataset to train a GoogleNet. It adopted a triplet loss function based on triplets of roughly aligned matching/nonmatching face patches generated by a novel online triplet mining method and achieved good performance of 99.63%. In the same year, VGGface  designed a procedure to collect a large-scale dataset from the Internet. It trained the VGGNet on this dataset and then fine-tuned the networks via a triplet loss function similar to FaceNet. VGGface obtains an accuracy of 98.95%. In 2017, SphereFace  used a 64-layer ResNet architecture and proposed the angular softmax (A-Softmax) loss to learn discriminative face features with angular margin. It boosts the achieves to 99.42% on LFW. In the end of 2017, a new large-scale face dataset, namely VGGface2 , was introduced, which consists of large variations in pose, age, illumination, ethnicity and profession. Cao et al. first trained a SENet with MS-celeb-1M dataset  and then fine-tuned the model with VGGface2 , and achieved the SOTA performance on the IJB-A  and IJB-B .

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: In 2014, DeepFace  achieved the SOTA accuracy on the famous LFW benchmark , approaching human performance on the unconstrained condition for the first time (DeepFace: 97.35% vs. Human: 97.53%), by training a 9-layer model on 4 million facial images. Inspired by this work, research focus has shifted to deep-learning-based approaches, and the accuracy was dramatically boosted to above 99.80% in just three years. Deep learning technique has reshaped the research landscape of FR in almost all aspects such as algorithm designs, training/test datasets, application scenarios and even the evaluation protocols. Therefore, it is of great significance to review the breakthrough and rapid development process in recent years. There have been several surveys on FR (24, 25, 26, 27, 28) and its subdomains, and they mostly summarized and compared a diverse set of techniques related to a specific FR scene, such as illumination-invariant FR , 3D FR , pose-invariant FR . Unfortunately, due to their earlier publication dates, none of them covered the deep learning methodology that is most successful nowadays. This survey focuses only on recognition problem, and one can refer to Ranjan et al.  for a brief review of a full deep FR pipeline with detection and alignment, or refer to Jin et al.  for a survey of face alignment. Specifically, the major contributions of this survey are as follows:

answer: The traditional methods (ie holistic approaches, local-feature-based methods, shallow learning) were the approaches used before the boom of deep learning based techniques [1]. They could achieve an accuracy of 95%, while the human accuracy was 9753% [29]. The rapid progress of deep learning methods quickly equaled human performance (DeepFace 9735%) and later on surpassed them with 998% [3].

input: Why did the authors choose MIDAP as the target NPU to experiment on?

However, the excerpt does provide details about MIDAP that suggest why it might be a suitable target for their methodology:

Therefore, while the *reason* for the choice is unstated, the *characteristics* of MIDAP that make it useful for their work are its fully-pipelined design and the resulting ability to perform accurate, static latency estimations [1].

ctxs.title: S3NAS: Fast NPU-aware Neural Architecture Search Methodology
ctxs.text: Even though the proposed methodology can be applied to any type of NPU, the current implementation is made for an adder-tree type NPU, called MIDAP (Kang et al., 2019). It has a fully-pipelined micro-architecture that consists of separate hardware modules and memory modules for convolution, activation function, and various reduction operations. Since it enables us to make a fully static schedule of operations without resource contention in the data path, we can estimate the end-to-end latency of a CNN quite accurately analytically. Unexpected delay may incur from off-chip DRAM delay that is not fully hidden by double buffering.

ctxs.title: Residual Attention Network for Image Classification
ctxs.text: However, recent advances of image classification focus on training feedforward convolutional neural networks using “very deep” structure (27, 33, 10). The feedforward convolutional network mimics the bottom-up paths of human cortex. Various approaches have been proposed to further improve the discriminative ability of deep convolutional neural network. VGG , Inception  and residual learning  are proposed to train very deep neural networks. Stochastic depth , Batch Normalization  and Dropout  exploit regularization for convergence and avoiding overfitting and degradation.

answer: The bottom-up top-down feedforward structure is a combination of a bottom-up fast feedforward process that creates low resolution features maps to quickly collect global information, and a top-down attention feedback process that uses the global information along with the original feature maps to create features for inference [14].

input: What is the main difference between the "maximum frequency" and "exclusive frequency" baselines?
question: What is the main difference between the "maximum frequency" and "exclusive frequency" baselines?
output:
The main difference between the "maximum frequency" and "exclusive frequency" baselines lies in how they select the entity when making a prediction.

*   **Maximum Frequency (Majority Baseline):** This baseline selects the entity that is **most frequently observed** in the context document [1].
*   **Exclusive Frequency (Exclusive Majority Baseline):** This baseline chooses the entity that is **most frequently observed in the context but is *not* observed in the query** [1].

The rationale behind the exclusion in the exclusive frequency baseline is that the placeholder is unlikely to be mentioned twice within a single Cloze form query [1].

ctxs.title: Teaching Machines to Read and Comprehend
ctxs.text: So far we have motivated the need for better datasets and tasks to evaluate the capabilities of machine reading models. We proceed by describing a number of baselines, benchmarks and new models to evaluate against this paradigm. We define two simple baselines, the majority baseline (maximum frequency) picks the entity most frequently observed in the context document, whereas the exclusive majority (exclusive frequency) chooses the entity most frequently observed in the context but not observed in the query. The idea behind this exclusion is that the placeholder is unlikely to be mentioned twice in a single Cloze form query.

answer: The main difference between the “maximum frequency” and “exclusive frequency” baselines is that the latter eliminates all entities found in the query as potential answers [11].

input: What is the meaning of "using graph structures explicitly"?
question: What is the meaning of "using graph structures explicitly"?
output:
"Using graph structures explicitly" means that the proposed Structure-Aware Transformer (SAT) architecture is designed to directly incorporate and consider the underlying structure of the graph when performing its self-attention mechanism, unlike many existing models [1].

Here is a detailed breakdown based on the provided excerpts:

*   **Mechanism of Incorporation:** The SAT achieves this by reformulating the standard self-attention mechanism as a kernel smoother. This mechanism extends the original exponential kernel on node features to also account for the local structures within the graph [1].
*   **Subgraph Representation:** This structural consideration is achieved by extracting a subgraph representation centered around each node [1]. The SAT uses methods to automatically generate these subgraph representations, allowing the kernel smoother to capture both structural and attributed similarities between nodes simultaneously [1].
*   **Contrast with Other Models:** This explicit consideration of structure distinguishes SAT from most existing position-aware Transformers for graph-structured data [1].
*   **Quantifying Structure:** The degree of structural information incorporated is controlled by a parameter, $k$. When $k=0$, the model reverts to a vanilla Transformer that only uses absolute positional encoding and does not utilize the graph structure [2]. Incorporating structural information (i.e., setting $k>0$) leads to substantial performance improvements [2].

In essence, "using graph structures explicitly" means the model doesn't just rely on node features or abstract positions; it actively processes information about the local neighborhood and connectivity of each node within the graph structure itself [1, 2].

ctxs.title: Structure-Aware Transformer for Graph Representation Learning
ctxs.text: In this work, we address the critical question of how to encode structural information into a Transformer architecture. Our principal contribution is to introduce a flexible structure-aware self-attention mechanism that explicitly considers the graph structure and thus captures structural interaction between nodes. The resulting class of Transformers, which we call the Structure-Aware Transformer (SAT), can provide structure-aware representations of graphs, in contrast to most existing position-aware Transformers for graph-structured data. Specifically:   •  We reformulate the self-attention mechanism in Vaswani et al. (2017) as a kernel smoother and extend the original exponential kernel on node features to also account for local structures, by extracting a subgraph representation centered around each node.    •  We propose several methods for automatically generating the subgraph representations, enabling the resulting kernel smoother to simultaneously capture structural and attributed similarities between nodes. The resulting representations are theoretically guaranteed to be at least as expressive as the subgraph representations.    •  We demonstrate the effectiveness of SAT models on five graph and node property prediction benchmarks by showing it achieves better performance than state-of-the-art GNNs and Transformers. Furthermore, we show how SAT can easily leverage any GNN to compute the node representations which incorporate subgraph information and outperform the base GNN, making it an effortless enhancer of any existing GNN.    •  Finally, we show that we can attribute the performance gains to the structure-aware aspect of our architecture, and showcase how SAT is more interpretable than the classic Transformer with an absolute encoding.

ctxs.title: Structure-Aware Transformer for Graph Representation Learning
ctxs.text: The key contribution of SAT is its ability to explicitly incorporate structural information in the self-attention. Here, we seek to demonstrate that this information provides crucial predictive information, and study how the choice of k𝑘k affects the results. Figure 3(a) shows how the test MAE is impacted by varying k𝑘k for k𝑘k-subtree and k𝑘k-subgraph extractors using PNA on the ZINC dataset. All models use the RWPE. k=0𝑘0k=0 corresponds to the vanilla Transformer only using absolute positional encoding, i.e. not using structure. We find that incorporating structural information leads to substantial improvement in performance, with optimal performance around k=3𝑘3k=3 for both k𝑘k-subtree and k𝑘k-subgraph extractors. As k𝑘k increases beyond k=4𝑘4k=4, the performance in k𝑘k-subtree extractors deteriorated, which is consistent with the observed phenomenon that GNNs work best in shallower networks (Kipf & Welling, 2017). We observe that k𝑘k-subgraph does not suffer as much from this issue, underscoring a new aspect of its usefulness. On the other hand, k𝑘k-subtree extractors are more computationally efficient and scalable to larger OGB datasets.

answer: The meaning of using graph structures explicitly is to explicity incorporate structural information into the self-attention [3]. The reason is that both P3 and P7 state the main contribution of SAT with paraphrasing [39].

input: How to create rare token identifier?
question: How to create rare token identifier?
output:
To create a rare token identifier, the process involves finding tokens within the model's vocabulary that have a low prior probability, thereby minimizing the chance of the identifier having a strong prior in either the language or diffusion model [1].

The specific steps described in the excerpt are:

1. **Identify Rare Tokens:** Perform a rare-token lookup within the model's vocabulary ($\hat{\mathbf{V}}$) [1].
2. **Token Sequence Generation:** Obtain a sequence of these rare token identifiers, denoted as $f(\hat{\mathbf{V}})$, where $f$ is the tokenizer function that maps character sequences to tokens [1].
3. **Determine Sequence Length:** The resulting sequence can have a variable length, $k$. The authors found that relatively short sequences, specifically $k=\{1, \dots, 3\}$, work well [1].
4. **Inversion (De-tokenization):** The sequence of tokens is then inverted back into text space using the de-tokenizer on $f(\hat{\mathbf{V}})$ to obtain the unique identifier, $\hat{\mathbf{V}}$ [1].

**Specific Guidance for Imagen:**
For the Imagen model, the authors found success by using a specific method:
*   Uniformly random sampling of tokens that correspond to three or fewer Unicode characters (and do not contain spaces) [1].
*   Using tokens that fall within the T5-XXL tokenizer range of $\{5000, \dots, 10000\}$ [1].

ctxs.title: DreamBooth: Fine Tuning Text-to-Image Diffusion Models for Subject-Driven Generation
ctxs.text: We generally find existing English words (e.g. “unique”, “special”) suboptimal since the model has to learn to disentangle them from their original meaning and to re-entangle them to reference our subject. This motivates the need for an identifier that has a weak prior in both the language model and the diffusion model. A hazardous way of doing this is to select random characters in the English language and concatenate them to generate a rare identifier (e.g. “xxy5syt00”). In reality, the tokenizer might tokenize each letter separately, and the prior for the diffusion model is strong for these letters. We often find that these tokens incur the similar weaknesses as using common English words. Our approach is to find rare tokens in the vocabulary, and then invert these tokens into text space, in order to minimize the probability of the identifier having a strong prior. We perform a rare-token lookup in the vocabulary and obtain a sequence of rare token identifiers f​(𝐕^)𝑓^𝐕f(\hat{\mathbf{V}}), where f𝑓f is a tokenizer; a function that maps character sequences to tokens and 𝐕^^𝐕\hat{\mathbf{V}} is the decoded text stemming from the tokens f​(𝐕^)𝑓^𝐕f(\hat{\mathbf{V}}). The sequence can be of variable length k𝑘k, and find that relatively short sequences of k={1,…,3}𝑘1…3k=\{1,...,3\} work well. Then, by inverting the vocabulary using the de-tokenizer on f​(𝐕^)𝑓^𝐕f(\hat{\mathbf{V}}) we obtain a sequence of characters that define our unique identifier 𝐕^^𝐕\hat{\mathbf{V}}. For Imagen, we find that using uniform random sampling of tokens that correspond to 3 or fewer Unicode characters (without spaces) and using tokens in the T5-XXL tokenizer range of {5000,…,10000}5000…10000\{5000,...,10000\} works well.

answer: In order to get a list of rare token IDs, we first search for rare tokens in the vocabulary [18].

input: What are identity shortcuts and projection shortcuts, and how are they used in the experiments?
question: What are identity shortcuts and projection shortcuts, and how are they used in the experiments?
output:
Based on the provided excerpts, here is a detailed answer regarding identity and projection shortcuts and their use in the experiments:

**What are Identity Shortcuts and Projection Shortcuts?**

The excerpts discuss these shortcuts in the context of "Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition" [1, 2]. While the text does not provide a formal definition of what constitutes an "identity shortcut" versus a "projection shortcut," it describes them as different mechanisms used within the architecture to help with training [1].

**How are they used in the experiments?**

The experiments compare three specific options (A, B, and C) involving these shortcuts [1]:

*   **Option (A):** This configuration uses **zero-padding shortcuts** to increase dimensions, and *all* shortcuts are **parameter-free** (which is equivalent to the setup in Table 2 and Figure 4 right) [1].
*   **Option (B):** This configuration uses **projection shortcuts** specifically for increasing dimensions, while the *other* shortcuts used are **identity** [1].
*   **Option (C):** This configuration uses **projection shortcuts** for *all* shortcuts [1].

**Experimental Findings and Conclusions:**

The comparison of these options yielded several key findings:

*   **General Improvement:** All three options (A, B, and C) were found to be "considerably better than the plain counterpart" [2].
*   **Comparison between A and B:** Option B was found to be "slightly better than A" [2]. The authors attribute this difference to the fact that the zero-padded dimensions in Option A "indeed have no residual learning" [2].
*   **Comparison between B and C:** Option C was found to be "marginally better than B," and the authors attribute this minor advantage to the "extra parameters introduced by many (thirteen) projection shortcuts" [2].
*   **Conclusion on Necessity:** Despite the small differences among options A, B, and C, the authors concluded that **projection shortcuts are not essential** for solving the degradation problem [2].
*   **Practical Choice:** Due to the computational overhead, the authors chose **not to use Option C** in the remainder of the paper, opting instead for a configuration that reduces "memory/time complexity and model sizes" [2].
*   **Importance of Identity Shortcuts:** The authors specifically noted that **identity shortcuts are particularly important** because they help avoid increasing the complexity of

ctxs.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
ctxs.text: Identity vs. Projection Shortcuts. We have shown that parameter-free, identity shortcuts help with training. Next we investigate projection shortcuts (Eqn.(2)). In Table 3 we compare three options: (A) zero-padding shortcuts are used for increasing dimensions, and all shortcuts are parameter-free (the same as Table 2 and Fig. 4 right); (B) projection shortcuts are used for increasing dimensions, and other shortcuts are identity; and (C) all shortcuts are projections.

ctxs.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
ctxs.text: Table 3 shows that all three options are considerably better than the plain counterpart. B is slightly better than A. We argue that this is because the zero-padded dimensions in A indeed have no residual learning. C is marginally better than B, and we attribute this to the extra parameters introduced by many (thirteen) projection shortcuts. But the small differences among A/B/C indicate that projection shortcuts are not essential for addressing the degradation problem. So we do not use option C in the rest of this paper, to reduce memory/time complexity and model sizes. Identity shortcuts are particularly important for not increasing the complexity of the bottleneck architectures that are introduced below.

answer: The identity and projection shortcut are not defined in this section [35]. Identity shortcuts help in training, do not increase the complexity of the bottleneck architectures and solve degradation problem [36].

input: What is the loss used during training of Faster R-CNN ?
question: What is the loss used during training of Faster R-CNN ?
output:
The provided excerpt describes the loss function for **Mask R-CNN**, not Faster R-CNN.

The loss function defined for Mask R-CNN during training is a multi-task loss applied to each sampled Region of Interest (RoI):
$$L = L_{cls} + L_{box} + L_{mask}$$ [1]

This total loss is composed of three parts:
1. **Classification Loss ($L_{cls}$):** This loss is identical to the classification loss defined in the referenced work [1].
2. **Bounding-Box Loss ($L_{box}$):** This loss is also identical to the bounding-box loss defined in the referenced work [1].
3. **Mask Loss ($L_{mask}$):** The mask branch outputs a $K^{m^2}$-dimensional vector for each RoI, encoding $K$ binary masks of resolution $m \times m$, one for each class. A per-pixel sigmoid is applied to these outputs, and $L_{mask}$ is defined as the average binary cross-entropy loss. Importantly, for an RoI matched to ground-truth class $k$, $L_{mask}$ is only calculated based on the $k$-th mask output; the other mask outputs do not contribute to the loss [1].

**Since the excerpt only details the loss for Mask R-CNN, it does not contain the specific information required to answer what the loss used during training of Faster R-CNN is.**

The classification loss Lc​l​ssubscript𝐿𝑐𝑙𝑠L_{cls} and bounding-box loss Lb​o​xsubscript𝐿𝑏𝑜𝑥L_{box} are identical as those defined in . The mask branch has a K​m2𝐾superscript𝑚2Km^{2}-dimensional output for each RoI, which encodes K𝐾K binary masks of resolution m×m𝑚𝑚m\times m, one for each of the K𝐾K classes. To this we apply a per-pixel sigmoid, and define Lm​a​s​ksubscript𝐿𝑚𝑎𝑠𝑘L_{mask} as the average binary cross-entropy loss. For an RoI associated with ground-truth class k𝑘k, Lm​a​s​ksubscript𝐿𝑚𝑎𝑠𝑘L_{mask} is only defined on the k𝑘k-th mask (other mask outputs do not contribute to the loss).

answer: For an RoI associated with ground-truth class k, L_{mask} is only defined on the k-th mask, where L is the average binary cross-entropy loss [15].

input: In what way can SBM-Transformer be considered better than Reformer?

output:
SBM-Transformer can be considered better than Reformer in the following ways, according to the excerpt:

* **Handling Hierarchical Contexts:** SBM-Transformer benefits from the flexibility of the Stochastic Block Model (SBM), which is beneficial because Reformer struggles in ListOps. This struggle is attributed to Reformer's inability to model hierarchical contexts, which is a limitation of its block-diagonal masks [1].

ctxs.title: Transformers meet Stochastic Block Models: Attention with Data-Adaptive Sparsity and Cost
ctxs.text: Table 8 shows the test accuracies of each method. Our SBM-Transformer achieves the best overall performance, ranking first in two tasks, and second in one other. SBM-Transformer also outperforms full attention in all five tasks while computing 30% or less attention scores on average, which supports our claim that masked attention with partial attention score computations can be preferred over full attention depending on the task. With respect to the attention mask structure, we find that flexibility of SBM is indeed beneficial, as Reformer struggles in ListOps, most likely due to the inability of block-diagonal masks to model hierarchical contexts.

answer: SBM-Transformer allows more flexible attention mask structures between linear to full attention with respective computational costs, while Reformer can only use block-diagonal masks that cannot model hierarchical contexts [31].

input: What do you mean by model pruning?

output:

ctxs.title: ShuffleNet: An Extremely Efficient Convolutional Neural Network for Mobile Devices
ctxs.text: Building deeper and larger convolutional neural networks (CNNs) is a primary trend for solving major visual recognition tasks (21, 9, 33, 5, 28, 24). The most accurate CNNs usually have hundreds of layers and thousands of channels (9, 34, 32, 40), thus requiring computation at billions of FLOPs. This report examines the opposite extreme: pursuing the best accuracy in very limited computational budgets at tens or hundreds of MFLOPs, focusing on common mobile platforms such as drones, robots, and smartphones. Note that many existing works (16, 22, 43, 42, 38, 27) focus on pruning, compressing, or low-bit representing a “basic” network architecture. Here we aim to explore a highly efficient basic architecture specially designed for our desired computing ranges.

answer: Although model pruning is a specific concept in deep learning, it refers to reducing the model size by removing redundant network connections or channels [0].

input: What are the FID values achieved by authors using Diffusion Model on ImageNet?
question: What are the FID values achieved by authors using Diffusion Model on ImageNet?
output:
The provided excerpts do not state specific numerical FID values achieved by the authors using Diffusion Models on ImageNet.

However, the excerpts do provide qualitative comparisons regarding FID:

*   **General Performance:** The diffusion models generally obtain the best FID on each task [3].
*   **Comparison to GANs:** For higher resolution ImageNet, the use of classifier guidance allows the diffusion models to "substantially outperform the best GANs" [3].
*   **FID Dependence:** FID scores depend on both diversity and fidelity, and the best FID values are obtained at an intermediate point when tuning the gradient scale [2].

To find the specific FID values, one would need to refer to Table 1 or Table 5 mentioned in the text, which are not included in the excerpt.

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: For all comparisons in this section, we train models on ImageNet 128×\times128 with batch size 256, and sample using 250 sampling steps. We train models with the above architecture changes and compare them on FID, evaluated at two different points of training, in Table 1. Aside from rescaling residual connections, all of the other modifications improve performance and have a positive compounding effect. We observe in Figure 2 that while increased depth helps performance, it increases training time and takes longer to reach the same performance as a wider model, so we opt not to use this change in further experiments.

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: Table 4 also shows that classifier guidance improves precision at the cost of recall, thus introducing a trade-off in sample fidelity versus diversity. We explicitly evaluate how this trade-off varies with the gradient scale in Figure 4. We see that scaling the gradients beyond 1.0 smoothly trades off recall (a measure of diversity) for higher precision and IS (measures of fidelity). Since FID and sFID depend on both diversity and fidelity, their best values are obtained at an intermediate point. We also compare our guidance with the truncation trick from BigGAN in Figure 5. We find that classifier guidance is strictly better than BigGAN-deep when trading off FID for Inception Score. Less clear cut is the precision/recall trade-off, which shows that classifier guidance is only a better choice up until a certain precision threshold, after which point it cannot achieve better precision.

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: Table 5 summarizes our results. Our diffusion models can obtain the best FID on each task, and the best sFID on all but one task. With the improved architecture, we already obtain state-of-the-art image generation on LSUN and ImageNet 64×\times64. For higher resolution ImageNet, we observe that classifier guidance allows our models to substantially outperform the best GANs. These models obtain perceptual quality similar to GANs, while maintaining a higher coverage of the distribution as measured by recall, and can even do so using only 25 diffusion steps.

answer: They obtain state-of-the-art image generation on ImageNet 64×64 [22]. For higher resolution ImageNet [44]. Table 5 shows the performance of ADM Metrics include FID, sFID, Prec, Rec [46].

input: What is a BLEU score?
question: What is a BLEU score?
output:
A BLEU score is a metric commonly used in the image description literature [1]. Specifically, it is described as "a form of precision of word n-grams between generated and reference sentences" [1].

The excerpt also clarifies that:
*   BLEU-n is a geometric average of precision over 1- to n-grams [1].
*   In much of the previous work in this literature, only BLEU-1 (which computes precision at the unigram level) was reported [1].

ctxs.title: Show and tell: A neural image caption generator
ctxs.text: The rest of the metrics can be computed automatically assuming one has access to groundtruth, i.e. human generated descriptions. The most commonly used metric so far in the image description literature has been the BLEU score , which is a form of precision of word n-grams between generated and reference sentences 222In this literature, most previous work report BLEU-1, i.e., they only compute precision at the unigram level, whereas BLEU-n is a geometric average of precision over 1- to n-grams.. Even though this metric has some obvious drawbacks, it has been shown to correlate well with human evaluations. In this work, we corroborate this as well, as we show in Section 4.3. An extensive evaluation protocol, as well as the generated outputs of our system, can be found at \urlhttp://nic.droppages.com/.

answer: BLUE score measures the precision of n-grams between generated sentences and reference sentences, which has been shown to correlate well with human evaluation [23].

input: The paper's model implies that the discriminative parameters also contain significant “generative” structure from the training dataset. What is meant by "generative" structure?
question: The paper's model implies that the discriminative parameters also contain significant “generative” structure from the training dataset. What is meant by "generative" structure?
output:
The paper implies that the discriminative parameters contain a "generative" structure because these parameters encode not only specific features of the training data, such as the jaguar's spots, but also broader structural elements of the subjects, such as its four legs [1].

In essence, the discriminative parameters have learned enough information from the training dataset to contribute to the generation of realistic images, suggesting they possess a generative quality beyond just classification or discrimination [1].

ctxs.title: Understanding Neural Networks Through Deep Visualization
ctxs.text: However, the results presented here suggest an alternate possibility: the previously used priors may simply have been too weak (see Section S1 for one hypothesis of why a strong p​(x)𝑝𝑥p(x) model is needed). With the careful design or learning of a p​(x)𝑝𝑥p(x) model that biases toward realism, one may be able to harness the large number of parameters present in a discriminately learned p​(y|x)𝑝conditional𝑦𝑥p(y|x) model to generate realistic images by enforcing probability under both models simultaneously. Even with the simple, hand-coded p​(x)𝑝𝑥p(x) models we use in this paper as regularizers, complex dependencies between distant pixels already arise (cf. the beetles with structure spanning over 100 pixels in Figure 4). This implies that the discriminative parameters also contain significant “generative” structure from the training dataset; that is, the parameters encode not only the jaguar’s spots, but to some extent also its four legs. With better, learned probabilistic models over the input and activations of higher layers, much more structure may be apparent. Work by Dai et al. (2015) shows some interesting results in this direction. While the images generated in this paper are far from being photo-realistic, they do suggest that transferring discriminatively trained parameters to generative models — opposite the direction of the usual unsupervised pretraining approach — may be a fruitful area for further investigation.

answer: Generative structure is how the data is distributed inside the space where it lives, for example when learning to detect jaguar class, parameters encode not only the jaguar’s spots(Only to distinguish it through a rare property), but to some extent also its four legs(to learn the pattern with which the whole creature can be found) [33].
