*   The method described in the paper is significantly better than ORB-SLAM (short) when the side-rotation (turning magnitude) is small (i.e., the car is mostly driving forward) [2].
*   The performance of the proposed method is comparable to ORB-SLAM (full) across the entire spectrum of side-rotation [2].
*   The large performance gap between the proposed method and ORB-SLAM (short) suggests that the learned ego-motion could potentially replace the local estimation modules in monocular SLAM systems [2].

However, the excerpts do not offer any technical details or analysis regarding the limitations of ORB-SLAM (short) itself in low-turning scenarios.

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Depth and Ego-Motion from Video
ctxs.text: In this work, we mimic this approach by training a model that observes sequences of images and aims to explain its observations by predicting likely camera motion and the scene structure (as shown in Fig. 1). We take an end-to-end approach in allowing the model to map directly from input pixels to an estimate of ego-motion (parameterized as 6-DoF transformation matrices) and the underlying scene structure (parameterized as per-pixel depth maps under a reference view). We are particularly inspired by prior work that has suggested view synthesis as a metric  and recent work that tackles the calibrated, multi-view 3D case in an end-to-end framework . Our method is unsupervised, and can be trained simply using sequences of images with no manual labeling or even camera motion information.

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Depth and Ego-Motion from Video
ctxs.text: For better understanding of our pose estimation results, we show in Figure 9 the ATE curve with varying amount of side-rotation by the car between the beginning and the end of a sequence. Figure 9 suggests that our method is significantly better than ORB-SLAM (short) when the side-rotation is small (i.e. car mostly driving forward), and comparable to ORB-SLAM (full) across the entire spectrum. The large performance gap between ours and ORB-SLAM (short) suggests that our learned ego-motion could potentially be used as an alternative to the local estimation modules in monocular SLAM systems.

answer: Proposed model has good performance as compared with  ORB-SLAM (short) when the turning magnitude is low [1]. ORB-SLAM (short) perform so poorly because it could not learn ego-motion [32].

input: What characteristics of large-scale pre-trained language models made it remarkable successful for passage re-ranking task?
question: What characteristics of large-scale pre-trained language models made it remarkable successful for passage re-ranking task?
output:
Large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) have achieved remarkable success in passage re-ranking due to several key characteristics:

* **Expressive Transformer Structure:** The underlying architecture of these models, such as BERT, ERNIE, and RoBERTa, is based on the transformer structure, which provides a high degree of expressiveness [1].
* **Pretrain-then-finetune Paradigm:** The success is also attributed to the pretrain-then-finetune paradigm [1]. This process allows the model to learn useful implicit knowledge from massive textual corpora [1].
* **Implicit Knowledge Acquisition:** Through this training process, the models are capable of learning semantic relevance within their latent space [1].

These PLM-based re-rankers typically operate by taking the concatenation of the query and passage pair as input and then applying multi-layer full-attention mechanisms to model their semantic relevance [1].

ctxs.title: Incorporating Explicit Knowledge in Pre-trained Language Models for Passage Re-ranking
ctxs.text: Passage Re-ranking is a crucial stage in modern information retrieval systems, which aims to reorder a small set of candidate passages to be presented to users. To put the most relevant passages on top of a ranking list, a re-ranker is usually designed with powerful capacity in modeling semantic relevance, which attracted a wealth of research studies in the past decade (Guo et al., 2020). Recently, large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs), e.g. BERT (Devlin et al., 2018), ERNIE (Sun et al., 2019) and RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), have dominated many natural language processing tasks, and have also achieved remarkable success on passage re-ranking. For example, PLM based re-rankers (MacAvaney et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020; Dong and Niu, 2021; Dong et al., 2022) have achieved state-of-the-art performance, which takes the concatenation of query-passage pair as input, and applies multi-layer full-attention to model their semantic relevance. Their superiority can be attributed to the expressive transformer structure and the pretrain-then-finetune paradigm, which allow the model to learn useful implicit knowledge (i.e., semantic relevance in the latent space) from massive textual corpus (Fan et al., 2021).

answer: Large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) have been found to be successful for passage re-ranking due to their ability to learn semantic relevance in the latent space from massive textual corpus [0].

input: How can the mask extracted directly from the attention maps mitigate the limitation of inversion process?
question: How can the mask extracted directly from the attention maps mitigate the limitation of inversion process?
output:
The provided excerpt suggests that the mask, which is directly extracted from the attention maps, helps to mitigate a limitation related to the inversion process by allowing the restoration of the unedited regions of the original image [1].

Specifically, the authors propose this method "to restore the unedited regions of the original image using a mask, directly extracted from the attention maps" [1]. This approach is noted to work effectively even when using the "naïve DDPM inversion scheme (adding noise followed by denoising)" [1].

In essence, the mask derived from the attention maps guides the process to selectively restore the parts of the image that were not intended to be changed during the editing operation, thereby improving the robustness of the editing process despite the limitations of the inversion technique used [1].

ctxs.title: Prompt-to-Prompt Image Editing with Cross Attention Control
ctxs.text: To alleviate this limitation, we propose to restore the unedited regions of the original image using a mask, directly extracted from the attention maps. Note that here the mask is generated with no guidance from the user. As presented in fig. 12, this approach works well even using the naïve DDPM inversion scheme (adding noise followed by denoising). Note that the cat’s identity is well-preserved under various editing operations, while the mask is produced only from the prompt itself.

answer: Extracted masks directly from the attention maps can restore the unedited regions of the original image [36].

input: What kinds of RNN architectures were used for the decoder in various prior research?
question: What kinds of RNN architectures were used for the decoder in various prior research?
output:
Various RNN architectures were employed for the decoder in prior research, depending on the specific model being referenced:

*   **Standard Hidden Unit:** One approach used an RNN with the standard hidden unit for the decoder [2].
*   **LSTM Hidden Unit:** Another approach involved using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) hidden unit for the decoder, particularly when the encoder was also using an LSTM [2].
*   **GRU Unit:** Some works utilized the Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) for the decoder [2].

Furthermore, the function $f$ that computes the current hidden state ($\mathbf{h}_j$) can be based on several unit types, including a vanilla RNN unit, a GRU, or an LSTM unit [3].

ctxs.title: Effective Approaches to Attention-based Neural Machine Translation
ctxs.text: Common to these two types of models is the fact that at each time step t𝑡t in the decoding phase, both approaches first take as input the hidden state 𝒉tsubscript𝒉𝑡\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{t} at the top layer of a stacking LSTM. The goal is then to derive a context vector 𝒄tsubscript𝒄𝑡\mbox{\boldmath{$c$}}_{t} that captures relevant source-side information to help predict the current target word ytsubscript𝑦𝑡y_{t}. While these models differ in how the context vector 𝒄tsubscript𝒄𝑡\mbox{\boldmath{$c$}}_{t} is derived, they share the same subsequent steps.

ctxs.title: Effective Approaches to Attention-based Neural Machine Translation
ctxs.text: ?) used an RNN with the standard hidden unit for the decoder and a convolutional neural network for encoding the source sentence representation. On the other hand, both ?) and ?) stacked multiple layers of an RNN with a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) hidden unit for both the encoder and the decoder. ?), ?), and ?) all adopted a different version of the RNN with an LSTM-inspired hidden unit, the gated recurrent unit (GRU), for both components.444They all used a single RNN layer except for the latter two works which utilized a bidirectional RNN for the encoder.

ctxs.title: Effective Approaches to Attention-based Neural Machine Translation
ctxs.text: In more detail, one can parameterize the probability of decoding each word yjsubscript𝑦𝑗y_{j} as:    p​(yj|y<j,𝒔)=softmax⁡(g​(𝒉j))𝑝conditionalsubscript𝑦𝑗subscript𝑦absent𝑗𝒔softmax𝑔subscript𝒉𝑗p\left(y_{j}|y_{<j},\mbox{\boldmath{$s$}}\right)=\operatorname{softmax}\left(g\left(\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{j}\right)\right)  (2)   with g𝑔g being the transformation function that outputs a vocabulary-sized vector.555One can provide g𝑔g with other inputs such as the currently predicted word yjsubscript𝑦𝑗y_{j} as in (Bahdanau et al., 2015). Here, 𝒉jsubscript𝒉𝑗\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{j} is the RNN hidden unit, abstractly computed as:    𝒉j=f​(𝒉j−1,𝒔),subscript𝒉𝑗𝑓subscript𝒉𝑗1𝒔\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{j}=f(\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{j-1},\mbox{\boldmath{$s$}}),  (3)   where f𝑓f computes the current hidden state given the previous hidden state and can be either a vanilla RNN unit, a GRU, or an LSTM unit. In (Kalchbrenner and Blunsom, 2013, Sutskever et al., 2014, Cho et al., 2014, Luong et al., 2015), the source representation 𝒔𝒔s is only used once to initialize the decoder hidden state. On the other hand, in (Bahdanau et al., 2015, Jean et al., 2015) and this work, 𝒔𝒔s, in fact, implies a set of source hidden states which are consulted throughout the entire course of the translation process. Such an approach is referred to as an attention mechanism, which we will discuss next.

answer: Previous work included vanilla RNN, LSTM and GRU in the decoder architecture [10]. as Sutskever and Luon stacked multiple layers of RNN with LSTM hidden unit for the decoder and encoder [6]. and Cho, Bahdanau and Jeal all adopted GRU [7].

input: How is this x converted to y using which network?
question: How is this x converted to y using which network?
output:
The input text ($\text{x}$) is converted into the generated video ($\hat{y}_t$) through a sequence of transformations involving several networks.

Here is a breakdown of the process based on the provided excerpts:

1. **Text Encoding:** The input text ($\text{x}$) is first processed by the **CLIP text encoder** ($\text{C}_x$) to produce an embedding ($\text{C}_x(\text{x})$) [1].
2. **Prior Network ($\text{P}$):** This embedding, along with the **BPE-encoded text** ($\hat{x}$), is fed into the **Prior network ($\text{P}$)**. The Prior network generates image embeddings ($\text{y}_e$) given the text embeddings ($\text{x}_e$) and the BPE encoded text tokens ($\hat{x}$) [2].
3. **Decoder Network ($\text{D}^t$):** The image embeddings ($\text{y}_e$) are then passed to the **Decoder network ($\text{D}^t$)**. This network generates a low-resolution RGB image ($\hat{y}_l$) at a size of $64 \times 64$ [2].
4. **Super-Resolution Networks ($\text{SR}_l$ and $\text{SR}_h$):** The low-resolution image ($\hat{y}_l$) is subsequently processed by two super-resolution networks:
    * $\text{SR}_l$ increases the resolution to $256 \times 256$ pixels.
    * $\text{SR}_h$ increases the resolution further to $768 \times 768$ pixels [2].
5. **Frame Interpolation ($\uparrow_F$):** The resulting image sequence undergoes frame interpolation ($\uparrow_F$) [1].
6. **Final Output:** The entire sequence is then processed by the spatiotemporal decoder ($\text{D}^t$) and the final output is the generated video ($\hat{y}_t$) [1].

In summary, the conversion involves the **CLIP text encoder** ($\text{C}_x$) followed by the **Prior network ($\text{P}$)**, which feeds into the **Decoder network ($\text{D}$)**, which is then enhanced by the **$\text{SR}_l$ and $\text{SR}_h$ networks** before final refinement [1, 2].

ctxs.title: Make-A-Video: Text-to-Video Generation without Text-Video Data-VIDEO DATA
ctxs.text: Make-A-Video’s final T2V inference scheme (depicted in Fig. 2) can be formulated as:    yt^=SRh∘SRlt∘↑F∘Dt∘P∘(x^,Cx(x)),\hat{y_{t}}=\operatorname{SR}_{h}\circ\operatorname{SR}_{l}^{t}\circ\uparrow_{F}\circ\operatorname{D}^{t}\circ\operatorname{P}\circ(\hat{x},\operatorname{C}_{x}(x)),  (1)   where yt^^subscript𝑦𝑡\hat{y_{t}} is the generated video, SRh,SRlsubscriptSRℎsubscriptSR𝑙\operatorname{SR}_{h},\operatorname{SR}_{l} are the spatial and spatiotemporal super-resolution networks (Sec. 3.2), ↑Fsubscript↑𝐹\uparrow_{F} is a frame interpolation network (Sec. 3.3), DtsuperscriptD𝑡\operatorname{D}^{t} is the spatiotemporal decoder (Sec. 3.2), PP\operatorname{P} is the prior (Sec. 3.1), x^^𝑥\hat{x} is the BPE-encoded text, CxsubscriptC𝑥\operatorname{C}_{x} is the CLIP text encoder (Radford et al., 2021), and x𝑥x is the input text. The three main components are described in detail in the following sections.

Maintaining a clean aesthetic for high definition videos is part of future work. generated image y^^𝑦\hat{y}.

answer: First, a prior network \operatorname{\textbf{P}}, that during inference generates image embeddings y_{e} given text embeddings x_{e} and BPE encoded text tokens \hat{x} [11]. Second, a decoder network \operatorname{\textbf{D}} that generates a low-resolution 64\times 64 RGB image \hat{y}_{l}, conditioned on the image embeddings y_{e} [13].

input: What type of defense strategies are evaded in this paper
question: What type of defense strategies are evaded in this paper
output:
The paper excerpt indicates that the black-box attack described overcomes **gradient masking defenses** [1].

Specifically, the authors state:

* They show a "more general flaw in the category of gradient masking" [1].
* They demonstrate that even if the defender tries to prevent attacks by "not publishing the directions in which the model is sensitive," the attack can still succeed because these directions can be discovered through other means [1].
* The attack based on "transfer from a substitute model overcomes gradient masking defenses" [1].

While the paper mentions that adversarial training and defensive distillation are two defenses with the greatest empirical success so far, the specific attack discussed in the context of overcoming defenses is shown to bypass gradient masking [1].

ctxs.title: Practical Black-Box Attacks against Machine Learning
ctxs.text: We show a more general flaw in the category of gradient masking. Even if the defender attempts to prevent attacks by not publishing the directions in which the model is sensitive, these directions can be discovered by other means, in which case the same attack can still succeed. We show that the black-box attack based on transfer from a substitute model overcomes gradient masking defenses. No fully effective defense mechanism is known, but we study the two with the greatest empirical success so far: adversarial training (4, 14), and defensive distillation for DNNs .

answer: Two types of defense are evaded, Adversarial training and Defensive distillation [70].

input: Author said that they achieved to make SOTA RE models. Give an evidences for this statement.
question: Author said that they achieved to make SOTA RE models. Give an evidences for this statement.
output:
The provided excerpts contain several instances where the authors claim to achieve or contribute to State-of-the-Art (SOTA) results in Sentence-level Relation Extraction (RE). Here are the evidences supporting this claim:

**Evidence of Achieving SOTA Performance:**

* **Outperforming Previous SOTA:** In one instance, the authors state that their result using the typed entity marker with the RoBERTa model achieved an F1 score of **74.6%**, which is "significantly higher than the SOTA result of 72.7% by LUKE Yamada et al. (2020)" [1].
* **Achieving New SOTA:** Another excerpt claims that the paper presents a baseline that "offers **new SOTA performance**" [2].
* **Significantly Outperforming Various SOTA Models:** Using RoBERTa as the backbone, the improved baseline model achieved an F1 of **74.6% on TACRED** and **83.2% on TACREV**, which is described as "significantly outperforming various SOTA RE models" [3].
* **Demonstrating Superior Performance on Re-TACRED:** The baseline model achieved an F1 of **91.1% on Re-TACRED**, "demonstrating that PLMs can achieve much better results on RE than shown in previous work" [3].

In summary, the authors claim SOTA status through direct comparisons against previous state-of-the-art results, achieving higher scores on benchmark datasets, and presenting a baseline that offers "new SOTA performance" [2, 3].

ctxs.title: An Improved Baseline for Sentence-level Relation Extraction
ctxs.text: We first provide an analysis on different entity representation techniques. In this analysis, we use the base and large versions of BERT Devlin et al. (2019) and the large version of RoBERTa Liu et al. (2019) as the encoder. Table 1 shows the performance of the PLMs incorporated with different entity representation techniques. For each technique, we also provide an example of the processed text. We have several observations from the results. First, the typed entity marker and its variants outperform untyped entity representation techniques by a notable margin. Especially, the RoBERTa model achieves an F1subscript𝐹1F_{1} score of 74.6%percent74.674.6\% using the typed entity marker (punct), which is significantly higher than the SOTA result of 72.7%percent72.772.7\% by LUKE Yamada et al. (2020). This shows that representing all categories of entity information is helpful to the RE task. It also shows that keeping entity names in the input improves the performance of RE models. Second, symbols used in entity markers have an obvious impact on the performance of RE models. Although the original and punct versions of entity representation techniques represent the same categories of entity information, they do lead to a difference in model performance. Particularly, introducing new special tokens hinders the model performance drastically on RoBERTa. On RoBERTaLARGELARGE{}_{\text{LARGE}}, the entity marker underperforms the entity marker (punct) by 0.7%percent0.70.7\%, the typed entity marker underperforms the typed entity marker (punct) by 3.6%percent3.63.6\%, while the entity mask gets a much worse result of 60.9%percent60.960.9\%.

ctxs.title: An Improved Baseline for Sentence-level Relation Extraction
ctxs.text: In this paper, we present a simple yet strong RE baseline that offers new SOTA performance, along with a comprehensive study to understand its prediction generalizability and robustness. Specifically, we revisit two technical problems in sentence-level RE, namely entity representation and noisy or ill-defined labels. We propose an improved entity representation technique, which significantly outperforms existing sentence-level RE models. Especially, our improved RE baseline achieves an F1subscript𝐹1F_{1} score of 91.1%percent91.191.1\% on the Re-TACRED dataset, showing that PLMs already achieve satisfactory performance on this task. We hope the proposed techniques and analyses can benefit future research on RE.

ctxs.title: An Improved Baseline for Sentence-level Relation Extraction
ctxs.text: We evaluate our model on TACRED Zhang et al. (2017), TACREV Alt et al. (2020), and Re-TACRED Stoica et al. (2021). Using RoBERTa Liu et al. (2019) as the backbone, our improved baseline model achieves an F1subscript𝐹1F_{1} of 74.6%percent74.674.6\% and 83.2%percent83.283.2\% on TACRED and TACREV, respectively, significantly outperforming various SOTA RE models. Particularly, our baseline model achieves an F1subscript𝐹1F_{1} of 91.1%percent91.191.1\% on Re-TACRED, demonstrating that PLMs can achieve much better results on RE than shown in previous work.222This work first appeared as a technical report on arXiv in Feb 2021 Zhou and Chen (2021). Since then, the proposed techniques have been incorporated into several follow-up works Chen et al. (2022); Wang et al. (2022b, a); Lu et al. (2022); Han et al. (2021); Kulkarni et al. (2022) that are published before this version of the paper.

answer: Their improved RE baseline achieved SOTA performance on the RE-TACRED dataset with f1 score of 911% [14]. Moreover, Using RoBERTa Liu et al [18]. (2019) as the backbone, they improved baseline model on TACRED and TACREV with f1 score 746% and 832%, respectively [3].

input: How can attention patterns from larger models be applied to smaller models if the models might differ in the size and number of attention layers?
question: How can attention patterns from larger models be applied to smaller models if the models might differ in the size and number of attention layers?
output:
The provided paper excerpt does not directly address how attention patterns from larger models can be applied to smaller models when the models differ in size or the number of attention layers.

However, the excerpt does discuss the **generalizability** of discovered attention patterns across different NLP tasks and the **consistency** of these patterns after fine-tuning:

*   **Generalizability:** The authors found "three patterns consistently existing in both tasks (over 50% of important heads)," suggesting that "important patterns are generalizable across multiple NLP tasks" [1]. This implies that certain structural or functional patterns observed in the attention heads might be transferable across related tasks.
*   **Consistency:** The attention patterns remained stable after fine-tuning, as evidenced by an "average Jensen-Shannon Divergence of 0.010.010.01 between the attention distributions of BERTSum across 3 random seeds" [1].

While these points suggest that the *types* of patterns found are robust, the text does not offer a methodology for *transferring* these patterns from a large model to a smaller one that has a different architecture.

ctxs.title: Human Guided Exploitation of Interpretable Attention Patterns in Summarization and Topic Segmentation
ctxs.text: After verifying on the validation set, we discover three patterns consistently existing in both tasks (over 50% of important heads). This suggests that important patterns are generalizable across multiple NLP tasks, which is consistent with the findings in Bian et al. (2021). Further analysis also shows that the attention patterns are consistent after fine-tuning, where we report an average Jensen-Shannon Divergence of 0.010.010.01 between the attention distributions of BERTSum across 3 random seeds. We hope our findings provide motivation for the in-depth study of pattern importance in different NLP tasks. Lastly, while it may be argued that this step of the pipeline can be automated by directly evaluating the importance and relevance of predefined patterns (e.g. syntax, discourse) based on intuitions, as indicated below, our interactive approach allows the discovery of interpretable patterns which otherwise would be hard to define due to the infinite search space of possible patterns. Next, we describe the three discovered patterns in detail.

answer: BERTSum is the summarization model, and Cross-Segment BERT is the topic segmentation model [31].

input: How can it actually benefit that GAT can learn regardless of the graph structure?
question: How can it actually benefit that GAT can learn regardless of the graph structure?
output:
The ability of Graph Attention Networks (GATs) to learn regardless of the graph structure offers several significant benefits, as detailed in the excerpt:

1. **Addressing Limitations of Previous Methods:** GATs overcome "many of the theoretical issues with previous spectral-based approaches" [1]. Spectral methods often require extensive knowledge of the entire graph structure beforehand, which GATs do not necessitate [1].
2. **Handling Unknown or Evolving Structures:** Because GATs do not depend on knowing the entire graph structure upfront, they are well-suited for scenarios where the graph structure might be incomplete, dynamic, or unknown during the modeling phase [1].
3. **Inductive Capability:** This structural independence allows the models to perform well on **inductive** tasks. Specifically, the models have shown success when tested on "completely unseen graphs" [1], meaning they can generalize their learned patterns to entirely new graph structures they have never encountered during training.

In summary, the benefit is that GATs are robust to structural assumptions, allowing them to generalize effectively to new, unseen graphs, which is a major advantage over methods that require a complete, predefined graph structure [1].

ctxs.title: Graph Attention Networks
ctxs.text: We have presented graph attention networks (GATs), novel convolution-style neural networks that operate on graph-structured data, leveraging masked self-attentional layers. The graph attentional layer utilized throughout these networks is computationally efficient (does not require costly matrix operations, and is parallelizable across all nodes in the graph), allows for (implicitly) assigning different importances to different nodes within a neighborhood while dealing with different sized neighborhoods, and does not depend on knowing the entire graph structure upfront—thus addressing many of the theoretical issues with previous spectral-based approaches. Our models leveraging attention have successfully achieved or matched state-of-the-art performance across four well-established node classification benchmarks, both transductive and inductive (especially, with completely unseen graphs used for testing).

answer: There are no clue to find what graph can GAT process by its regardless of the graph structure [32].

input: What's the effect of expanding channel size?
question: What's the effect of expanding channel size?
output:
The effect of expanding the channel size depends on the scale of the dataset being used:

*   **For the large-scale dataset (JFT-300M subset):** Expanding the model capacity from 64 base channels to **128 base channels resulted in significant additional improvements** [1].
*   **For ImageNet GANs:** Expanding the capacity beyond the baseline was **not beneficial** [1].

ctxs.title: LARGE SCALE GAN TRAINING FOR HIGH FIDELITY NATURAL IMAGE SYNTHESIS
ctxs.text: To confirm that our design choices are effective for even larger and more complex and diverse datasets, we also present results of our system on a subset of JFT-300M (Sun et al., 2017). The full JFT-300M dataset contains 300M real-world images labeled with 18K categories. Since the category distribution is heavily long-tailed, we subsample the dataset to keep only images with the 8.5K most common labels. The resulting dataset contains 292M images – two orders of magnitude larger than ImageNet. For images with multiple labels, we sample a single label randomly and independently whenever an image is sampled. To compute IS and FID for the GANs trained on this dataset, we use an Inception v2 classifier (Szegedy et al., 2016) trained on this dataset. Quantitative results are presented in Table 3. All models are trained with batch size 2048. We compare an ablated version of our model – comparable to SA-GAN (Zhang et al., 2018) but with the larger batch size – against a “full” BigGAN model that makes uses of all of the techniques applied to obtain the best results on ImageNet (shared embedding, skip-z𝑧z, and orthogonal regularization). Our results show that these techniques substantially improve performance even in the setting of this much larger dataset at the same model capacity (64 base channels). We further show that for a dataset of this scale, we see significant additional improvements from expanding the capacity of our models to 128 base channels, while for ImageNet GANs that additional capacity was not beneficial.

answer: expanding channel size substantially improves the performance of the model [34].

input: How did the authors optimize alpha and beta for the activation function of the prediction layers?
question: How did the authors optimize alpha and beta for the activation function of the prediction layers?
output:
The authors optimized $\alpha$ and $\beta$ for the activation function of the prediction layers by setting them to specific values: $\alpha=10$ and $\beta=0.01$ [1].

The activation function used was $\frac{1}{(\alpha \cdot \text{sigmoid}(x) + \beta)}$ [1].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Depth and Ego-Motion from Video
ctxs.text: For single-view depth prediction, we adopt the DispNet architecture proposed in  that is mainly based on an encoder-decoder design with skip connections and multi-scale side predictions (see Figure 4). All conv layers are followed by ReLU activation except for the prediction layers, where we use 1/(α∗s​i​g​m​o​i​d​(x)+β)1𝛼𝑠𝑖𝑔𝑚𝑜𝑖𝑑𝑥𝛽1/(\alpha*sigmoid(x)+\beta) with α=10𝛼10\alpha=10 and β=0.01𝛽0.01\beta=0.01 to constrain the predicted depth to be always positive within a reasonable range. We also experimented with using multiple views as input to the depth network, but did not find this to improve the results. This is in line with the observations in , where optical flow constraints need to be enforced to utilize multiple views effectively.

answer: fixed values for alpha=10 and beta=001 are use to constrain the predicted depth to be always positive within a reasonable range [19].

input: How the proposed loss function is different from that of original Single-Path NAS?
question: How the proposed loss function is different from that of original Single-Path NAS?
output:
The proposed loss function in S3NAS differs from that of the original Single-Path NAS by **directly including the target latency** estimated by a cycle-accurate simulator of the target NPU [2].

In contrast, existing hardware-aware differentiable NAS methods, such as SinglePath NAS, only define certain hyperparameters to balance accuracy and latency, and their loss function (like Eq. (6) mentioned in [1]) **lacks information on the target latency** [1]. Because of this limitation, if a strict latency constraint exists, these older methods require additional search costs for hyperparameters to ensure the final architecture meets the latency constraint. Furthermore, this process must be repeated every time the target latency is changed [1].

S3NAS overcomes this by modifying the loss function to incorporate the target latency directly, which allows it to find a baseline architecture that is shorter in latency than the constraint when using a tight latency constraint, thereby speeding up the search process [2].

ctxs.title: S3NAS: Fast NPU-aware Neural Architecture Search Methodology
ctxs.text: The existing hardware-aware differentiable NAS methods mostly define some hyperparameters to balance between accuracy and latency, including SinglePath NAS, whose loss function is defined as Eq. (6). Since there is no information on the target latency in the loss function, in case there is a strict latency constraint, they have to pay additional search costs for the hyperparameters to let the final architecture have no larger latency than the constraint. In addition, this process needs to be repeated whenever the target latency is changed.

ctxs.title: S3NAS: Fast NPU-aware Neural Architecture Search Methodology
ctxs.text: In this work, we propose a fast NPU-aware NAS methodology extending the Single-Path NAS technique (Stamoulis et al., 2019). We modify the supernet architecture by varying the number of blocks in stages and adding mixed depthwise convolution (Tan and Le, 2019b) to the search space. By modifying the loss function to directly include the target latency estimated by a cycle-accurate simulator of the target NPU, we could find a better baseline architecture with a shorter latency than the latency constraint. Using a tight latency constraint, we can reduce the search space to find the baseline network fast. Afterward, we apply compound scaling to find a larger network than the baseline network, and add SE blocks and h-swish activation functions in the post-processing step. Through the proposed NAS methodology, we could obtain a network with 82.72% accuracy with 11.66ms latency on our target NPU, without special data augmentation in training. It dominates the existing network models on the target NPU. It confirms the importance of supernet architecture design for a given NPU and effectiveness of the three-step approach in the proposed NAS methodology: supernet design, SinglePath NAS with a tighter latency constraint, and compound scaling and post-processing.

answer: Previous method needs additional search cost for hyperparameter, since they have no information for target latency [49]. The author's method directly includes the target latency, resulting in ease of search process [77].

input: What is the best performing CNN model in the abdominal LN detection?
question: What is the best performing CNN model in the abdominal LN detection?
output: The best performing CNN model for abdominal LN detection is **GoogLeNet-TL (256x256)**, which achieved an AUC of **0.92** and a TPR/3FP of **0.70** [1].

ctxs.title: Deep Convolutional Neural Networks for Computer-Aided Detection: CNN Architectures, Dataset Characteristics and Transfer Learning
ctxs.text: Many of our CNN models achieve notably better (FROC-AUC and TPR/3FP) results than the previous state-of-the-art models  for mediastinal LN detection: GoogLeNet-RI-L obtains an AUC=0.95 and 0.85 TPR/3FP, versus AUC=0.92 and 0.70 TPR/3FP  and 0.78 TPR/3FP  which uses stacked shallow learning. This difference lies in the fact that annotated lymph node segmentation masks are required to learn a mid-level semantic boundary detector , whereas CNN approaches only need LN locations for training . In abdominal LN detection,  obtains the best trade-off between its CNN model complexity and sampled data configuration. Our best performing CNN model is GoogLeNet-TL (256x256) which obtains an AUC=0.92 and 0.70 TPR/3FP.

answer: The best performing model is GoogLeNet-TL [37].

input: In terms of image synthesis, do the GANs perform better than VQ-VAE or not?
question: In terms of image synthesis, do the GANs perform better than VQ-VAE or not?
output:
Based on the provided excerpts, it is not possible to give a definitive answer comparing GANs directly against VQ-VAEs in terms of image synthesis performance.

Here is what the excerpts state regarding GANs and related models:

*   **GAN Performance:** GANs were previously considered the state-of-the-art on most image generation tasks, as measured by metrics like FID and Inception Score [1].
*   **Limitations of GANs:** GANs are noted to be difficult to train, often collapsing without careful hyperparameter selection [1].
*   **Comparison to Likelihood Models:** Some likelihood-based models (which are distinct from VQ-VAEs, though both are mentioned in the context of comparison) have been shown to capture more diversity than GANs [1].
*   **Comparison to Diffusion Models:** Diffusion models are presented as an advancement over GANs, suggesting they surpass GANs in certain aspects, though the excerpts do not detail a direct comparison between GANs and VQ-VAEs.

The excerpts mention VAEs specifically in the context of diffusion models: "Furthermore, except for VAEs, sampling from these models is slower than GANs in terms of wall-clock time" [2]. This suggests that VAEs are mentioned as an exception regarding sampling speed when comparing diffusion models to GANs, but it does not quantify the image synthesis quality between GANs and VQ-VAEs.

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: GANs Goodfellow et al. (2014) currently hold the state-of-the-art on most image generation tasks Brock et al. (2018); Wu et al. (2019); Karras et al. (2019b) as measured by sample quality metrics such as FID Heusel et al. (2017), Inception Score Salimans et al. (2016) and Precision Kynkäänniemi et al. (2019). However, some of these metrics do not fully capture diversity, and it has been shown that GANs capture less diversity than state-of-the-art likelihood-based models Razavi et al. (2019); Nichol and Dhariwal (2021); Nash et al. (2021). Furthermore, GANs are often difficult to train, collapsing without carefully selected hyperparameters and regularizers Brock et al. (2018); Miyato et al. (2018); Brock et al. (2016).

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: While GANs hold the state-of-the-art, their drawbacks make them difficult to scale and apply to new domains. As a result, much work has been done to achieve GAN-like sample quality with likelihood-based models Razavi et al. (2019); Ho et al. (2020); Nash et al. (2021); Child (2021). While these models capture more diversity and are typically easier to scale and train than GANs, they still fall short in terms of visual sample quality. Furthermore, except for VAEs, sampling from these models is slower than GANs in terms of wall-clock time.

answer: Fidelity can be higher, but GANs are not always better in terms of low diversity [1]. In table 5 ImageNet256x256 experiment, BigGAN-deep beats VA-VAE2 about FID, sFID, Precision but lose about Recall [2].

input: What is the major one structural difference between ELMO model and others (BERT small, BERT large, DeBERTa)
question: What is the major one structural difference between ELMO model and others (BERT small, BERT large, DeBERTa)
output:
The major structural difference between the ELMO model and the other models (BERT small, BERT large, and DeBERTa) is its underlying architecture.

*   **ELMO** is described as an **LSTM-based language model** [1].
*   In contrast, **BERT small**, **BERT large**, and **DeBERTa** are all **transformer-based language models** [1].

Furthermore, the excerpt provides specific structural details for each:

*   **BERT small** has **12 transformer encoder layers** [1].
*   **BERT large** has **24 transformer encoders** [1].
*   **DeBERTa** has **12 transformer encoder layers** [1].
*   **ELMO** has **3 layers** [1].

ctxs.title: What do Large Language Models Learn beyond Language?
ctxs.text: Next, we describe the LMs and their variants used in NILM. We experiment with four language models, based on both Transformer and RNN architectures. BERT small. This is the bert-base-uncased model with 12 transformer encoder layers and the dimension of the representations is 768. BERT tokenizer is based on the WordPiece model Wu et al. (2016).  BERT large. This is the bert-large-uncased model which has 24 transformer encoders and representations have 1024 dimensions.  DeBERTa. This is a transformer based language model and its tokenizer is built using Byte Pair Encoding Sennrich et al. (2016). We consider the DeBERTa base model. It has 12 transformer encoder layers and representations have 768 dimensions.  ELMO. This is an LSTM based language model Peters et al. (2018). It has 3 layers and the output representations have 1024 dimensions.

answer: ELMO is LSTM based language model, but BERT and DeBERTa is transformer based language model [12].

input: A reparameterization of the variational lower bound yields a lower bound estimator that can be straightforwardly optimized using standard stochastic gradient methods. Is this estimator differentiable?
question: A reparameterization of the variational lower bound yields a lower bound estimator that can be straightforwardly optimized using standard stochastic gradient methods. Is this estimator differentiable?
output:
Yes, the estimator derived from the reparameterization of the variational lower bound is differentiable.

The core idea is that by using a differentiable transformation $g_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}(\boldsymbol{\epsilon},\mathbf{x})$ of an auxiliary noise variable $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$, the expectation with respect to the approximate posterior $q_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})$ can be rewritten as an expectation with respect to the noise variable $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ [2].

Specifically, the process allows for the construction of a **differentiable estimator** of the expectation:
$$\int q_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})f(\mathbf{z})\,d\mathbf{z} = \int p(\boldsymbol{\epsilon})f(g_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}(\boldsymbol{\epsilon},\mathbf{x}))\,d\boldsymbol{\epsilon} \simeq \frac{1}{L}\sum_{l=1}^{L}f(g_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol{\epsilon}^{(l)}))$$
where $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}^{(l)} \sim p(\boldsymbol{\epsilon})$ [3].

When this technique is applied to the variational lower bound, it yields the **Stochastic Gradient Variational Bayes (SGVB) estimator** ($\widetilde{\mathcal{L}}^{A}$ or $\widetilde{\mathcal{L}}^{B}$) [1, 2]. This estimator is explicitly designed to be differentiable with respect to the variational parameters $\boldsymbol{\phi}$ [3].

The resulting objective function, $\widetilde{\mathcal{L}}(\boldsymbol{\theta},\boldsymbol{\phi};\mathbf{x}^{(i)})$, allows for the computation of gradients ($\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta},\boldsymbol{\phi}}\widetilde{\mathcal{L}}$), which can then be used with stochastic optimization methods like SGD or Adagrad [2, 8].

ctxs.title: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
ctxs.text: How can we perform efficient approximate inference and learning with directed probabilistic models whose continuous latent variables and/or parameters have intractable posterior distributions? The variational Bayesian (VB) approach involves the optimization of an approximation to the intractable posterior. Unfortunately, the common mean-field approach requires analytical solutions of expectations w.r.t. the approximate posterior, which are also intractable in the general case. We show how a reparameterization of the variational lower bound yields a simple differentiable unbiased estimator of the lower bound; this SGVB (Stochastic Gradient Variational Bayes) estimator can be used for efficient approximate posterior inference in almost any model with continuous latent variables and/or parameters, and is straightforward to optimize using standard stochastic gradient ascent techniques.

ctxs.title: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
ctxs.text: The strategy in this section can be used to derive a lower bound estimator (a stochastic objective function) for a variety of directed graphical models with continuous latent variables. We will restrict ourselves here to the common case where we have an i.i.d. dataset with latent variables per datapoint, and where we like to perform maximum likelihood (ML) or maximum a posteriori (MAP) inference on the (global) parameters, and variational inference on the latent variables. It is, for example, straightforward to extend this scenario to the case where we also perform variational inference on the global parameters; that algorithm is put in the appendix, but experiments with that case are left to future work. Note that our method can be applied to online, non-stationary settings, e.g. streaming data, but here we assume a fixed dataset for simplicity.

answer: Yes, the reparameterization trick is useful and it can be constructed a differentiable estimator [0]. This trick can be used to obtain a differentiable estimator of the variational lower bound [10]. Authours show how a reparameterization of the variational lower bound yields a simple differentiable unbiased estimator of the lower bound [13]. The proposed estimator can be straightforwardly differentiated and optimized using standard stochastic gradient methods [2].

input: Is there any specific reason that Bw and Bh uses an exponential function for the location prediction?
question: Is there any specific reason that Bw and Bh uses an exponential function for the location prediction?
output:
Based on the provided excerpt, there is **no mention** of Bw or Bh using an exponential function for location prediction.

The excerpt describes the prediction method as follows:

*   The approach follows YOLO by predicting **location coordinates relative to the location of the grid cell** [1].
*   This method constrains the ground truth to fall between **0 and 1** [1].
*   A **logistic activation** function is used to constrain the network's predictions to fall within this range [1].

Since the text only mentions using a logistic activation function for bounding the predictions between 0 and 1, it does not provide any information regarding the use of an exponential function for Bw or Bh.

ctxs.title: YOLO9000: Better, Faster, Stronger
ctxs.text: Instead of predicting offsets we follow the approach of YOLO and predict location coordinates relative to the location of the grid cell. This bounds the ground truth to fall between 00 and 111. We use a logistic activation to constrain the network’s predictions to fall in this range.

answer: Exponential function for the location prediction is used to bound the network’s predictions to fall in ground bounding boxes range of 0 to 1 [25].

input: What are the metrics used for monocular depth and camera motion estimation?
question: What are the metrics used for monocular depth and camera motion estimation?
output:
The provided excerpts describe different evaluation contexts for depth and ego-motion estimation, and thus different metrics are mentioned for each task.

**For Monocular Depth Estimation:**

*   **Scale Matching:** Since the depth predicted by the method is only defined up to a scale factor, a scaling factor ($\hat{s}$) is determined by matching the median of the predicted depth ($D_{pred}$) to the median of the ground truth depth ($D_{gt}$):
    $$\hat{s} = \frac{\text{median}(D_{gt})}{\text{median}(D_{pred})}$$
    This scaling factor is then used to evaluate the depth maps [1].

**For Ego-Motion (Pose) Estimation:**

*   **Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE):** When evaluating the pose estimation network, the Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) is used as the primary metric [2].
*   **Scale Optimization:** Before computing ATE, the scaling factor for the predictions from each method is optimized to best align with the ground truth [2].
*   **Evaluation Context:** This evaluation is performed on 555-frame snippets, and the ATE is averaged over the full sequence [2].

In summary, the metrics depend on the specific task: **median matching** is used to handle scale ambiguity in **depth estimation** [1], while **Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE)** is used for **ego-motion estimation** [2].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Depth and Ego-Motion from Video
ctxs.text: To the best of our knowledge, no previous systems exist that learn single-view depth estimation in an unsupervised manner from monocular videos. Nonetheless, here we provide comparison with prior methods with depth supervision  and recent methods that use calibrated stereo images (i.e. with pose supervision) for training (14, 16). Since the depth predicted by our method is defined up to a scale factor, for evaluation we multiply the predicted depth maps by a scalar s^^𝑠\hat{s} that matches the median with the ground-truth, i.e. s^=m​e​d​i​a​n​(Dg​t)/m​e​d​i​a​n​(Dp​r​e​d)^𝑠𝑚𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑎𝑛subscript𝐷𝑔𝑡𝑚𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑎𝑛subscript𝐷𝑝𝑟𝑒𝑑\hat{s}=median(D_{gt})/median(D_{pred}).

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Depth and Ego-Motion from Video
ctxs.text: To evaluate the performance of our pose estimation network, we applied our system to the official KITTI odometry split (containing 111111 driving sequences with ground truth odometry obtained through the IMU/GPS readings, which we use for evaluation purpose only), and used sequences 000000-080808 for training and 090909-101010 for testing. In this experiment, we fix the length of input image sequences to our system to 555 frames. We compare our ego-motion estimation with two variants of monocular ORB-SLAM  (a well-established SLAM system): 1) ORB-SLAM (full), which recovers odometry using all frames of the driving sequence (i.e. allowing loop closure and re-localization), and 2) ORB-SLAM (short), which runs on 555-frame snippets (same as our input setting). Another baseline we compare with is the dataset mean of car motion (using ground-truth odometry) for 555-frame snippets. To resolve scale ambiguity during evaluation, we first optimize the scaling factor for the predictions made by each method to best align with the ground truth, and then measure the Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE)  as the metric. ATE is computed on 555-frame snippets and averaged over the full sequence.333For evaluating ORB-SLAM (full) we break down the trajectory of the full sequence into 555-frame snippets with the reference coordinate frame adjusted to the central frame of each snippet. As shown in Table 3 and Fig. 9, our method outperforms both baselines (mean odometry and ORB-SLAM (short)) that share the same input setting as ours, but falls short of ORB-SLAM (full), which leverages whole sequences (159115911591 for seq. 090909 and 120112011201 for seq. 101010) for loop closure and re-localization.

answer: Depth map are computed and matched across different scales for monocular depth metric [25]. ATE metric is used for camera motion estimation [31].

input: How does IS and NCE compare in terms of model performance?
question: How does IS and NCE compare in terms of model performance?
output:
The provided excerpt focuses on the mathematical formulation and conceptual differences between two techniques, IS (Improved Sequence) and NCE (Noise Contrastive Estimation), rather than directly comparing their resulting model performance in terms of metrics like accuracy or perplexity.

However, the excerpt does offer a comparative observation regarding the potential advantages of IS:

*   **Multiclass vs. Binary Classification:** The core difference lies in the classification task they frame. NCE frames the task as a **binary classification** between true or noise words using a logistic loss. In contrast, IS frames the task as a **multiclass classification problem** using a Softmax and cross-entropy loss [1].
*   **Potential Advantage of IS:** The authors suggest that because IS optimizes a multiclass classification task (instead of a binary one), **IS "may be a better choice"** [1].
*   **Logit Updates:** Furthermore, the excerpt notes a structural difference in the updates: the logits in IS are **tied**, whereas they are **independent** in NCE [1].

In summary, while the text does not provide empirical performance data, it posits that IS has theoretical advantages stemming from its multiclass formulation compared to NCE's binary formulation [1].

Note that the only difference between NCE and IS is that, in NCE, we define a binary classification task between true or noise words with a logistic loss, whereas in IS we define a multiclass classification problem with a Softmax and cross entropy loss. We hope that our derivation helps clarify the similarities and differences between the two. In particular, we observe that IS, as it optimizes a multiclass classification task (in contrast to solving a binary task), may be a better choice. Indeed, the updates to the logits with IS are tied whereas in NCE they are independent.

answer: IS performs better [27].

input: In the model architecture described in this paper, how many residual connections are used?
question: In the model architecture described in this paper, how many residual connections are used?
output:
The provided excerpts state that the system uses residual connections to encourage gradient flow [1]. Specifically, the LSTM RNNs have 8 layers, and there are residual connections *between* these layers [1]. Furthermore, the text mentions that residual connections greatly improve gradient flow, allowing for the training of very deep encoder and decoder networks, and that they use 8 LSTM layers for both the encoder and decoder [2].

However, the excerpts **do not specify the exact number** of residual connections used, only that they are present between the 8 layers of the LSTM networks [1, 2].

ctxs.title: Google's Neural Machine Translation System: Bridging the Gap between Human and Machine Translation
ctxs.text: This work presents the design and implementation of GNMT, a production NMT system at Google, that aims to provide solutions to the above problems. In our implementation, the recurrent networks are Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) RNNs (23, 17). Our LSTM RNNs have 8 layers, with residual connections between layers to encourage gradient flow . For parallelism, we connect the attention from the bottom layer of the decoder network to the top layer of the encoder network. To improve inference time, we employ low-precision arithmetic for inference, which is further accelerated by special hardware (Google’s Tensor Processing Unit, or TPU). To effectively deal with rare words, we use sub-word units (also known as “wordpieces”)  for inputs and outputs in our system. Using wordpieces gives a good balance between the flexibility of single characters and the efficiency of full words for decoding, and also sidesteps the need for special treatment of unknown words. Our beam search technique includes a length normalization procedure to deal efficiently with the problem of comparing hypotheses of different lengths during decoding, and a coverage penalty to encourage the model to translate all of the provided input.

input: They claim that the attention mechanism bring more discriminative feature representation, is that true ?
question: They claim that the attention mechanism bring more discriminative feature representation, is that true ?
output:
Based on the provided excerpt, the paper claims that the attention mechanism contributes to a more discriminative feature representation, although it describes *how* this is achieved rather than explicitly stating the general principle of discriminative feature learning.

Here is a detailed breakdown:

*   **Specialized Feature Attention:** The Residual Attention Network utilizes an Attention Module where "each trunk branch has its own mask branch to learn attention that is specialized for its features" [1]. This specialization suggests that the network is not applying a single, generalized attention mechanism, but rather one tailored to the specific features extracted at different levels of the network.
*   **Targeted Refinement and Elimination:** The mechanism demonstrates this specialization through examples. For instance, in hot air balloon images, the network uses a "sky mask to eliminate background" using blue color features from the bottom layer, while simultaneously using a "balloon instance mask" to "refine" features from the top layer [1]. This targeted masking and refinement process inherently leads to a more focused and discriminative representation of the object of interest (the balloon) against the background.
*   **Gradual Refinement:** Furthermore, the structure allows for improvement over successive layers: "the incremental nature of stacked network structure can gradually refine attention for complex images" [1]. This gradual refinement implies that the network is iteratively improving its ability to distinguish relevant features from noise or irrelevant parts of the image.

**Conclusion:**

While the excerpt does not contain a direct sentence stating, "The attention mechanism brings more discriminative feature representation," the described functionality—where specialized masks eliminate irrelevant backgrounds and refine object instances across multiple layers—strongly implies that the attention mechanism is designed to achieve a **more discriminative feature representation** by focusing computational resources on the most salient parts of the image.

ctxs.title: Residual Attention Network for Image Classification
ctxs.text: The Residual Attention Network alleviates above problems. In Attention Module, each trunk branch has its own mask branch to learn attention that is specialized for its features. As shown in Fig.1, in hot air balloon images, blue color features from bottom layer have corresponding sky mask to eliminate background, while part features from top layer are refined by balloon instance mask. Besides, the incremental nature of stacked network structure can gradually refine attention for complex images.

answer: The attention masks successfully learn meaningful information from the dataset and their usage resulted in state-of-the-art results, which indicates that the attention mechanism does learn more discriminative features [20].

input: Why does a deeper network with smaller kernel size have better performances ?
question: Why does a deeper network with smaller kernel size have better performances ?
output:
The provided excerpts discuss the challenges of building deep 3D Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and propose a solution using smaller kernels, but they do not explicitly state that a deeper network *with smaller kernel size* inherently leads to better performance. Instead, they explain *why* this design choice is beneficial for network construction and efficiency.

Here is a detailed breakdown based on the text:

**1. The Challenge of Deep 3D Networks:**
*   Deeper networks generally have greater discriminative power due to additional non-linearities and better local optima [1].
*   However, standard 3D convolutions are computationally expensive compared to 2D variants [1].
*   The large number of trainable parameters in 3D architectures makes them increasingly prone to overfitting [1].

**2. The Benefit of Using Smaller Kernels (e.g., $3^3$ instead of $5^3$):**
*   To enable the construction of a deeper 3D architecture, the authors adopt the strategy of using only small $3^3$ kernels [2].
*   This design choice is beneficial because smaller kernels are faster to convolve with and contain fewer weights [2].
*   Specifically, replacing common kernels (like $5^3$) with $3^3$ kernels reduces the element-wise multiplications by a factor of approximately $5^3/3^3 \approx 4.6$ and reduces the number of trainable parameters by the same factor [2].

**Conclusion on Performance:**
The text suggests that by using smaller kernels, it becomes possible to design **deeper network variants** that are **more efficient** and are **implicitly regularized** [2]. This increased efficiency and implicit regularization allow the network to be deeper without immediately succumbing to the computational burden or overfitting issues associated with very large, deep 3D models [1, 2].

**In summary, the benefit is not that the smaller kernel *causes* better performance, but rather that the smaller kernel *enables* the construction of a deeper, more efficient, and implicitly regularized network architecture, which in turn allows the network to leverage the discriminative power of depth.**

ctxs.title: Efficient multi‐scale 3D CNN with fully connected CRF for accurate brain lesion segmentation
ctxs.text: Deeper networks have greater discriminative power due to the additional non-linearities and better quality of local optima (Choromanska et al. (2015)). However, convolutions with 3D kernels are computationally expensive in comparison to the 2D variants, which hampers the addition of more layers. Additionally, 3D architectures have a larger number of trainable parameters, with each layer adding Cl​Cl−1​∏i={x,y,z}𝜿l(i)subscript𝐶𝑙subscript𝐶𝑙1subscriptproduct𝑖𝑥𝑦𝑧superscriptsubscript𝜿𝑙𝑖C_{l}C_{l-1}\prod_{i=\{x,y,z\}}{\bm{\kappa}_{l}^{(i)}} weights to the model. Clsubscript𝐶𝑙C_{l} is the number of FMs in layer l𝑙l and 𝜿l{x,y,z}superscriptsubscript𝜿𝑙𝑥𝑦𝑧\bm{\kappa}_{l}^{\{x,y,z\}} the size of its kernel in the respective spatial dimension. Overall this makes the network increasingly prone to over-fitting.

ctxs.title: Efficient multi‐scale 3D CNN with fully connected CRF for accurate brain lesion segmentation
ctxs.text: In order to build a deeper 3D architecture, we adopt the sole use of small 33superscript333^{3} kernels that are faster to convolve with and contain less weights. This design approach was previously found beneficial for classification of natural images (Simonyan and Zisserman (2014)) but its effect is even more drastic on 3D networks. When compared to common kernel choices of 53superscript535^{3} (Zikic et al. (2014); Urban et al. (2014); Prasoon et al. (2013)) and in our baseline CNN, the smaller 33superscript333^{3} kernels reduce the element-wise multiplications by a factor of approximately 53/33≈4.6superscript53superscript334.65^{3}/3^{3}\approx 4.6 while reducing the number of trainable parameters by the same factor. Thus deeper network variants that are implicitly regularised and more efficient can be designed by simply replacing each layer of common architectures with more layers that use smaller kernels (Fig. 4).

answer: Deeper networks exhibit better performance as they introduce more non-linearities and converge towards better local optima [26]. But, adding more layers increase both computation time and the number of parameters [27].

input: Why not just use membership inference attack recall [1,2] and exposure metric [3], which are commonly used and established metrics? These two basically do what the currently proposed metrics do.
question: Why not just use membership inference attack recall [1,2] and exposure metric [3], which are commonly used and established metrics? These two basically do what the currently proposed metrics do.
output:
The provided excerpt does not contain a direct answer to the question: "Why not just use membership inference attack recall [1,2] and exposure metric [3], which are commonly used and established metrics? These two basically do what the currently proposed metrics do."

However, the excerpt does offer context regarding the existing approaches and how the authors' work differs:

* **Existing Viewpoints:** Some previous work views the memorization of Language Models (LMs) primarily as a **threat to individual privacy** [1].
* **Existing Metrics:** These earlier works utilize metrics that quantify how susceptible the LMs are to **adversarial attacks** [1].
* **Specific Examples:** These metrics are largely dependent on specific attack types, such as the **membership inference attack** (Shokri et al., 2017) [1].
* **The Authors' Focus:** In contrast to these approaches, the authors of the paper state that **"we instead focus on more targeted extraction attacks"** [1].

Therefore, based on the text, the authors' decision to move beyond membership inference attacks suggests that their proposed metrics are designed to address a different, more specific threat vector—**targeted extraction attacks**—rather than solely quantifying susceptibility to membership inference attacks.

ctxs.title: Knowledge Unlearning for Mitigating Privacy Risks in Language Models
ctxs.text: Previous work that explores to which extent LMs have memorized their training data approach the phenomenon with two different viewpoints. Some work view memorization of LMs simply as a threat to individual privacy (Carlini et al., 2021; 2022a; Jagielski et al., 2022) and utilize metrics that quantify how much the LMs are susceptible to adversarial attacks. These metrics are mostly dependent on the specific types of attacks such as the membership inference attack (Shokri et al., 2017) and measure the privacy risks of LMs by quantifying the success rate of these attacks. In our work, we instead focus on more targeted extraction attacks.

answer: These metrics are dependent on the specific attacks, while ours is agnostic of the type of attack [12].

input: Do the authors perform hyperparameter tuning for each dataset independently?
question: Do the authors perform hyperparameter tuning for each dataset independently?
output:
The authors do not perform hyperparameter tuning for each dataset independently; rather, they establish a set of hyperparameters and tune them on a specific validation set.

Here are the details from the text:

* **General Approach:** The authors state that if a specific hyperparameter is not mentioned otherwise, they use the same set of hyperparameters across different tasks [1].
* **Initial Tuning:** They tune their hyperparameters on the **IMDb validation set** [1].
* **Task-Specific Tuning (Epochs):** While the core architecture and learning rates are set, the number of epochs is tuned per task using the validation set of that task: "We tune the number of epochs on the validation set of each task" [1].
* **Dataset Size Consideration:** They note that the fine-tuning duration depends on the dataset size; for small datasets like TREC-6, they fine-tune the LM for 15 epochs to avoid overfitting, whereas they can fine-tune longer on larger datasets [1].

In summary, the core model architecture and most hyperparameters are fixed and tuned on IMDb, but the number of epochs is adjusted based on the specific task's validation set.

ctxs.title: Universal Language Model Fine-tuning for Text Classification
ctxs.text: We are interested in a model that performs robustly across a diverse set of tasks. To this end, if not mentioned otherwise, we use the same set of hyperparameters across tasks, which we tune on the IMDb validation set. We use the AWD-LSTM language model Merity et al. (2017a) with an embedding size of 400400400, 333 layers, 115011501150 hidden activations per layer, and a BPTT batch size of 707070. We apply dropout of 0.40.40.4 to layers, 0.30.30.3 to RNN layers, 0.40.40.4 to input embedding layers, 0.050.050.05 to embedding layers, and weight dropout of 0.50.50.5 to the RNN hidden-to-hidden matrix. The classifier has a hidden layer of size 505050. We use Adam with β1=0.7subscript𝛽10.7\beta_{1}=0.7 instead of the default β1=0.9subscript𝛽10.9\beta_{1}=0.9 and β2=0.99subscript𝛽20.99\beta_{2}=0.99, similar to Dozat and Manning (2017). We use a batch size of 646464, a base learning rate of 0.0040.0040.004 and 0.010.010.01 for fine-tuning the LM and the classifier respectively, and tune the number of epochs on the validation set of each task888On small datasets such as TREC-6, we fine-tune the LM only for 151515 epochs without overfitting, while we can fine-tune longer on larger datasets. We found 505050 epochs to be a good default for fine-tuning the classifier.. We otherwise use the same practices used in Merity et al. (2017a).

If any of these assumptions are violated in a training sequence, the gradients could be corrupted and potentially inhibit training. To improve the robustness of our learning pipeline to these factors, we additionally train a explainability prediction network (jointly and simultaneously with the depth and pose networks) that outputs a per-pixel soft mask E^ssubscript^𝐸𝑠\hat{E}_{s} for each target-source pair, indicating the network’s belief in where direct view synthesis will be successfully modeled for each target pixel. Based on the predicted E^ssubscript^𝐸𝑠\hat{E}_{s}, the view synthesis objective is weighted correspondingly by    ℒv​s=∑<I1,…,IN⁣>⁣∈𝒮∑pE^s​(p)​|It​(p)−I^s​(p)|.subscriptℒ𝑣𝑠subscriptabsentsubscript𝐼1…subscript𝐼𝑁absent𝒮subscript𝑝subscript^𝐸𝑠𝑝subscript𝐼𝑡𝑝subscript^𝐼𝑠𝑝\mathcal{L}_{vs}=\sum_{<I_{1},\ldots,I_{N}>\in\mathcal{S}}\sum_{p}\hat{E}_{s}(p)|I_{t}(p)-\hat{I}_{s}(p)|~{}. To resolve this, we add a regularization term ℒr​e​g​(E^s)subscriptℒ𝑟𝑒𝑔subscript^𝐸𝑠\mathcal{L}_{reg}(\hat{E}_{s}) that encourages nonzero predictions by minimizing the cross-entropy loss with constant label 111 at each pixel location. input: Give one example Relation Extraction question and its answer.

output:
**Question:** Given the sentence "Bill Gates founded Microsoft together with his friend Paul Allen in 1975" and an entity pair ("Bill Gates", "Microsoft"), what is the expected relation prediction for a Relation Extraction (RE) model?

**Answer:** The expected relation prediction is **ORG:FOUNDED\_BY** [1].

ctxs.title: An Improved Baseline for Sentence-level Relation Extraction
ctxs.text: As one of the fundamental information extraction (IE) tasks, relation extraction (RE) aims at identifying the relationship(s) between two entities in a given piece of text from a pre-defined set of relationships of interest. For example, given the sentence “Bill Gates founded Microsoft together with his friend Paul Allen in 1975” and an entity pair (“Bill Gates”, “Microsoft”), the RE model is expected to predict the relation ORG:FOUNDED_BY. On this task, SOTA models based on PLMs Devlin et al. (2019); Joshi et al. (2020) have gained significant success.

input: If there are few examples for learn, we called them few-shot learning. Guess the meaning of zero-shot learning.
question: If there are few examples for learn, we called them few-shot learning. Guess the meaning of zero-shot learning.
output:
Based on the provided excerpt, the text describes **few-shot learning** as a task where the model must learn from a small number of examples [1].

While the excerpt does not explicitly define "zero-shot learning," it establishes the context of few-shot learning as a paradigm shift from traditional supervised learning. Few-shot learning involves leveraging a distribution of similar tasks, where the input-output pairs are collections of images and their associated label similarity, rather than standard independent and identically distributed (iid) samples [1].

Given that few-shot learning deals with learning from a *few* examples, it is reasonable to infer that **zero-shot learning** would likely refer to a scenario where the model must perform a task with **zero** examples of the target class or task during the training/adaptation phase.

ctxs.title: FEW-SHOT LEARNING WITH GRAPH NEURAL NET- WORKS
ctxs.text: One such instance is the ability to learn from few examples, in the so-called few-shot learning tasks. Rather than relying on regularization to compensate for the lack of data, researchers have explored ways to leverage a distribution of similar tasks, inspired by human learning Lake et al. (2015). This defines a new supervised learning setup (also called ‘meta-learning’) in which the input-output pairs are no longer given by iid samples of images and their associated labels, but by iid samples of collections of images and their associated label similarity.

answer: Few-shot learning is learning from few examples [1].

input: What is the difference between Mask R-CNN and Faster R-CNN ?
question: What is the difference between Mask R-CNN and Faster R-CNN ?
output:
The difference between Mask R-CNN and Faster R-CNN lies in the additional output provided by Mask R-CNN.

Mask R-CNN builds upon Faster R-CNN by adding a third output branch to its architecture [1]. While Faster R-CNN produces two outputs for each candidate object—a class label and a bounding-box offset—Mask R-CNN adds a branch that outputs the **object mask** [1].

This addition of the mask output means that Mask R-CNN requires the extraction of a much finer spatial layout of the object compared to Faster R-CNN [1]. Furthermore, Mask R-CNN incorporates the concept of **pixel-to-pixel alignment**, which the excerpt identifies as the main missing piece in Fast/Faster R-CNN [1].

ctxs.title: Mask R-CNN
ctxs.text: Mask R-CNN is conceptually simple: Faster R-CNN has two outputs for each candidate object, a class label and a bounding-box offset; to this we add a third branch that outputs the object mask. Mask R-CNN is thus a natural and intuitive idea. But the additional mask output is distinct from the class and box outputs, requiring extraction of much finer spatial layout of an object. Next, we introduce the key elements of Mask R-CNN, including pixel-to-pixel alignment, which is the main missing piece of Fast/Faster R-CNN.

answer: Mask R-CNN has pixel-to-pixel alignment whereas Faster R-CNN doesn'tMask R-CNN, extends Faster R-CNN by adding a branch for predicting segmentation masks on each Region of Interest (RoI), in parallel with the existing branch for classification and bounding box regression [12].

input: The authors say, "a very simple way to improve the performance of almost any machine learning algorithm is to train many different models on the same data and then to average their predictions". What is this technique called?
question: The authors say, "a very simple way to improve the performance of almost any machine learning algorithm is to train many different models on the same data and then to average their predictions". What is this technique called?
output:
The technique described by the authors, where multiple different models are trained and their predictions are averaged to significantly outperform individual models, is called **ensemble prediction** [1].

The authors specifically detail this process in the context of training 10 separate models to predict $P(h_t|\mathbf{s}_t;\boldsymbol{\theta})$ [1]. They found that initializing these models with different initial parameter values creates enough diversity among the trained models such that averaging their predictions leads to significant performance gains over any single model [1].

ctxs.title: Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network
ctxs.text: We trained 10 separate models to predict P​(ht|𝐬t;𝜽)𝑃conditionalsubscriptℎ𝑡subscript𝐬𝑡𝜽P(h_{t}|\mathbf{s}_{t};\boldsymbol{\theta}), using exactly the same architecture and training procedure as the baseline. The models are randomly initialized with different initial parameter values and we find that this creates sufficient diversity in the trained models to allow the averaged predictions of the ensemble to significantly outperform the individual models. We have explored adding diversity to the models by varying the sets of data that each model sees, but we found this to not significantly change our results, so we opted for the simpler approach. For the distillation we tried temperatures of (1,𝟐,5,10)12510(1,{\bf 2},5,10) and used a relative weight of 0.5 on the cross-entropy for the hard targets, where bold font indicates the best value that was used for table 1 .

answer: The authors define an “ensemble of models” as a set of separate models with the same architecture and training procedure, but different randomly initialized parameters whose predictions are then averaged to increase performance [19].

output:

ctxs.title: Make-A-Video: Text-to-Video Generation without Text-Video Data-VIDEO DATA
ctxs.text: As with all large-scale models trained on data from the web, our models have learnt and likely exaggerated social biases, including harmful ones. Our T2I generation model was trained on data that removed NSFW content and toxic words. All our data (image as well as videos) is publicly available, adding a layer of transparency to our models, and making it possible for the community to reproduce our work.

ctxs.title: Make-A-Video: Text-to-Video Generation without Text-Video Data-VIDEO DATA
ctxs.text: Text-to-Video Generation. While there is remarkable progress in T2I generation, the progress of T2V generation lags behind largely due to two main reasons: the lack of large-scale datasets with high-quality text-video pairs, and the complexity of modeling higher-dimensional video data. Early works (Mittal et al., 2017; Pan et al., 2017; Marwah et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018; Gupta et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019b) are mainly focused on video generation in simple domains, such as moving digits or specific human actions. To our knowledge, Sync-DRAW (Mittal et al., 2017) is the first T2V generation approach that leverages a VAE with recurrent attention. (Pan et al., 2017) and (Li et al., 2018) extend GANs from image generation to T2V generation.

ctxs.title: Make-A-Video: Text-to-Video Generation without Text-Video Data-VIDEO DATA
ctxs.text: Leveraging Image Priors for Video Generation. Due to the complexity of modeling videos and the challenges in high-quality video data collection, it is natural to consider leveraging image priors for videos to simplifying the learning process. After all, an image is a video with a single frame (Bain et al., 2021). In unconditional video generation, MoCoGAN-HD (Tian et al., 2021) formulates video generation as the task of finding a trajectory in the latent space of a pre-trained and fixed image generation model. In T2V generation, NÜWA (Wu et al., 2021b) combines image and video datasets in a multitask pre-training stage to improve model generalization for fine-tuning. CogVideo (Hong et al., 2022) uses a pre-trained and fixed T2I model for T2V generation with only a small number of trainable parameters to reduce memory usage during training. But the fixed autoencoder and T2I models can be restrictive for T2V generation. The architecture of VDM (Ho et al., 2022) can enable joint image and video generation. However, they sample random independent images from random videos as their source of images, and do not leverage the massive text-image datasets.

answer: Modeling videos require expensive computational complexity that it is challenging in high-quality video data collection [36]. Thus, large-scale paired text-video is expensive as well [6]. Because of the limitations, the progress of T2V generation lags behind [8].

input: How author create embeddings for each post in Reddit data?
question: How author create embeddings for each post in Reddit data?
output:
The authors created embeddings for each post in the Reddit data by concatenating four distinct types of information for every post [1]:

1. **Average embedding of the post title:** They used the average of the GloVe CommonCrawl word vectors for the post's title [1].
2. **Average embedding of all the post's comments:** They calculated the average embedding across all the comments associated with the post [1].
3. **The post's score:** The numerical score of the post was included as a feature [1].
4. **The number of comments made on the post:** The total count of comments on the post was also included as a feature [1].

These components were combined into a single feature vector for each post [1].

ctxs.title: Inductive Representation Learning on Large Graphs
ctxs.text: Reddit data. In our second task, we predict which community different Reddit posts belong to. Reddit is a large online discussion forum where users post and comment on content in different topical communities. We constructed a graph dataset from Reddit posts made in the month of September, 2014. The node label in this case is the community, or “subreddit”, that a post belongs to. We sampled 50 large communities and built a post-to-post graph, connecting posts if the same user comments on both. In total this dataset contains 232,965 posts with an average degree of 492. We use the first 20 days for training and the remaining days for testing (with 30% used for validation). For features, we use off-the-shelf 300-dimensional GloVe CommonCrawl word vectors ; for each post, we concatenated (i) the average embedding of the post title, (ii) the average embedding of all the post’s comments (iii) the post’s score, and (iv) the number of comments made on the post.

answer: For Reddit data, authors encode Glove word vectors with GraphSAGE [30].
