input: Could the model just choose the embedding function to be zero just to overcome the very small distance that might occur in case of choosing the hardest negatives during triplet selection?
question: Could the model just choose the embedding function to be zero just to overcome the very small distance that might occur in case of choosing the hardest negatives during triplet selection?
output:
Based on the provided excerpt, the authors are actively trying to *prevent* the model from collapsing to the zero function, which is a known problem when selecting the hardest negatives during training.

The excerpt states that selecting the hardest negatives "can in practice lead to bad local minima early on in training, specifically it can result in a collapsed model (i.e. $f(x)=0$)" [1].

To mitigate this specific problem, the authors propose a selection criterion for negative exemplars ($x_i^n$) such that:
$$\left\|f(x_i^a)-f(x_i^p)\right\|_{2}^{2}<\left\|f(x_i^a)-f(x_i^n)\right\|_{2}^{2}\text{.} \quad (4)$$

This criterion suggests that the proposed method is a *solution* to the problem of the model collapsing to $f(x)=0$, not a description of a scenario where the model *could* choose that function to overcome a small distance. The entire context of the passage is dedicated to describing a mechanism (selecting semi-hard negatives) designed to keep the training process stable and prevent this collapse [1].

input: Is the Walker task notable for having hard-to-escape local optima?
question: Is the Walker task notable for having hard-to-escape local optima?

ctxs.title: Benchmarking Deep Reinforcement Learning for Continuous Control
ctxs.text: In this category, we implement six locomotion tasks of varying dynamics and difficulty: Swimmer (Purcell, 1977; Coulom, 2002; Levine & Koltun, 2013; Schulman et al., 2015a), Hopper (Murthy & Raibert, 1984; Erez et al., 2011; Levine & Koltun, 2013; Schulman et al., 2015a), Walker (Raibert & Hodgins, 1991; Erez et al., 2011; Levine & Koltun, 2013; Schulman et al., 2015a), Half-Cheetah (Wawrzyński, 2007; Heess et al., 2015b), Ant (Schulman et al., 2015b), Simple Humanoid (Tassa et al., 2012; Schulman et al., 2015b), and Full Humanoid (Tassa et al., 2012). The goal for all the tasks is to move forward as quickly as possible. These tasks are more challenging than the basic tasks due to high degrees of freedom. In addition, a great amount of exploration is needed to learn to move forward without getting stuck at local optima. Since we penalize for excessive controls as well as falling over, during the initial stage of learning, when the robot is not yet able to move forward for a sufficient distance without falling, apparent local optima exist including staying at the origin or diving forward slowly.

answer: In this category, we implement six locomotion tasks of varying dynamics and difficulty: Swimmer (Purcell, 1977; Coulom, 2002; Levine & Koltun, 2013; Schulman et al, 2015a), Hopper (Murthy & Raibert, 1984; Erez et al, 2011; Levine & Koltun, 2013; Schulman et al, 2015a), Walker (Raibert & Hodgins, 1991; Erez et al, 2011; Levine & Koltun, 2013; Schulman et al, 2015a), Half-Cheetah (Wawrzyński, 2007; Heess et al, 2015b), Ant (Schulman et al, 2015b), Simple Humanoid (Tassa et al, 2012; Schulman et al, 2015b), and Full Humanoid (Tassa et al, 2012)The goal for all the tasks is to move forward as quickly as possible [11].

input: What are the stage the Mask R-CNN consists of ??
question: What are the stage the Mask R-CNN consists of ??
output:
Mask R-CNN consists of a two-stage procedure [1].

The first stage is identical to the Region Proposal Network (RPN) [1].

In the second stage, Mask R-CNN performs the following tasks in parallel:
*   Predicting the class [1].
*   Predicting the box offset [1].
*   Outputting a binary mask for each Region of Interest (RoI) [1].

ctxs.title: Mask R-CNN
ctxs.text: Mask R-CNN adopts the same two-stage procedure, with an identical first stage (which is RPN). In the second stage, in parallel to predicting the class and box offset, Mask R-CNN also outputs a binary mask for each RoI. This is in contrast to most recent systems, where classification depends on mask predictions (e.g. (33, 10, 26)). Our approach follows the spirit of Fast R-CNN  that applies bounding-box classification and regression in parallel (which turned out to largely simplify the multi-stage pipeline of original R-CNN ).

answer: Two stages are RPN and  In the second stage, in addition to predicting the class and box offset, Mask R-CNN also produces a binary mask for each RoI [14].

input: Why did the authors choose a greedy approach for general classifier?
question: Why did the authors choose a greedy approach for general classifier?
output:
The authors chose a greedy approach for the general multiclass differentiable classifier because, for general non-linear classifiers, the set $P$ that describes the region where the classifier outputs a specific label $\hat{k}(\bm{x}_{0})$ is no longer a polyhedron [1].

To handle this, they approximate the set $P$ at each iteration $i$ with a polyhedron $\tilde{P}_i$ [1]. The algorithm then proceeds by computing the perturbation vector that reaches the boundary of this polyhedron $\tilde{P}_i$ at each iteration and updating the current estimate [1].

The authors explicitly state that this proposed algorithm operates in a greedy way and is **not guaranteed to converge to the optimal perturbation** in (1) [1]. However, they note that in practice, their algorithm yields very small perturbations, which they believe are good approximations of the minimal perturbation [1].

ctxs.title: DeepFool: A Simple and Accurate Method to Fool Deep Neural Networks
ctxs.text: We now extend the DeepFool algorithm to the general case of multiclass differentiable classifiers. For general non-linear classifiers, the set P𝑃P in Eq. (7) that describes the region of the space where the classifier outputs label k^​(𝒙0)^𝑘subscript𝒙0\hat{k}(\bm{x}_{0}) is no longer a polyhedron. Following the explained iterative linearization procedure in the binary case, we approximate the set P𝑃P at iteration i𝑖i by a polyhedron P~isubscript~𝑃𝑖\tilde{P}_{i}     P~i=⋂k=1c{\displaystyle\tilde{P}_{i}=\bigcap_{k=1}^{c}\Big{\{} 𝒙:fk​(𝒙i)−fk^​(𝒙0)​(𝒙i):𝒙subscript𝑓𝑘subscript𝒙𝑖subscript𝑓^𝑘subscript𝒙0subscript𝒙𝑖\displaystyle\bm{x}:f_{k}(\bm{x}_{i})-f_{\hat{k}(\bm{x}_{0})}(\bm{x}_{i})  (10)     +∇fk(𝒙i)⊤𝒙−∇fk^​(𝒙0)(𝒙i)⊤𝒙≤0}.\displaystyle+\nabla f_{k}(\bm{x}_{i})^{\top}\bm{x}-\nabla f_{\hat{k}(\bm{x}_{0})}(\bm{x}_{i})^{\top}\bm{x}\leq 0\Big{\}}.    We then approximate, at iteration i𝑖i, the distance between 𝒙isubscript𝒙𝑖\bm{x}_{i} and the complement of P𝑃P, dist​(𝒙i,Pc)distsubscript𝒙𝑖superscript𝑃𝑐\text{{dist}}(\bm{x}_{i},P^{c}), by dist​(𝒙i,P~ic)distsubscript𝒙𝑖superscriptsubscript~𝑃𝑖𝑐\text{{dist}}(\bm{x}_{i},\tilde{P}_{i}^{c}). Specifically, at each iteration of the algorithm, the perturbation vector that reaches the boundary of the polyhedron P~isubscript~𝑃𝑖\tilde{P}_{i} is computed, and the current estimate updated. The method is given in Algorithm 2. It should be noted that the proposed algorithm operates in a greedy way and is not guaranteed to converge to the optimal perturbation in (1). However, we have observed in practice that our algorithm yields very small perturbations which are believed to be good approximations of the minimal perturbation.

ctxs.title: DeepFool: A Simple and Accurate Method to Fool Deep Neural Networks
ctxs.text: It should be noted that the optimization strategy of DeepFool is strongly tied to existing optimization techniques. In the binary case, it can be seen as Newton’s iterative algorithm for finding roots of a nonlinear system of equations in the underdetermined case . This algorithm is known as the normal flow method. The convergence analysis of this optimization technique can be found for example in . Our algorithm in the binary case can alternatively be seen as a gradient descent algorithm with an adaptive step size that is automatically chosen at each iteration. The linearization in Algorithm 2 is also similar to a sequential convex programming where the constraints are linearized at each step.

answer: The DeepFool method is designed iteratively starting from very simple binary classifiers to more general non-linear differentiable classifiers [11]. The effectiveness of the greedy algorithm is justified by previous work and the results show very small perturbations, thus the authors claim that it is a viable method [12].

input: Is random search (RS) more efficient that reinforcement learning (RL) for learning neural architectures?
question: Is random search (RS) more efficient that reinforcement learning (RL) for learning neural architectures?
output:
Based on the provided excerpt, it cannot be definitively concluded that random search (RS) is more *efficient* than reinforcement learning (RL) for learning neural architectures, although the evidence suggests RL finds better architectures.

Here is a detailed breakdown based on the text:

*   **RL Finds Superior Architectures:** The excerpt strongly suggests that RL is better at finding high-quality architectures than RS. Specifically, "the best model identified with RL is significantly better than the best model found by RS by over 1% as measured by on CIFAR-10" [1]. Furthermore, RL finds a broader set of high-performing models, as evidenced by the "mean performance of the top-5 and top-25 models identified in RL versus RS" [1].
*   **RS is a Viable Strategy:** Despite RL's superior performance in finding better models, the authors acknowledge that RS is not necessarily a poor strategy, stating, "We take these results to indicate that although RS may provide a viable search strategy, RL finds better architectures in the NASNet search space" [1].

**Conclusion on Efficiency:**

The excerpt focuses on the *quality* of the architectures found, not the computational *efficiency* (e.g., time or computational cost per search iteration) of the two methods. While RL finds better results, the text does not provide enough information to compare the efficiency of the two methods.

ctxs.title: Learning Transferable Architectures for Scalable Image Recognition
ctxs.text: Figure 6 shows the performance of reinforcement learning (RL) and random search (RS) as more model architectures are sampled. Note that the best model identified with RL is significantly better than the best model found by RS by over 1% as measured by on CIFAR-10. Additionally, RL finds an entire range of models that are of superior quality to random search. We observe this in the mean performance of the top-5 and top-25 models identified in RL versus RS. We take these results to indicate that although RS may provide a viable search strategy, RL finds better architectures in the NASNet search space.

answer: No, Reninforcement Learning is more efficient than Random Search for learning neural architectures [33].

input: The paper's algorithm yields very small perturbations which are believed to be good approximations of the minimal perturbation. Quantitatively, how far is the paper's approximation from the minimal perturbation?
question: The paper's algorithm yields very small perturbations which are believed to be good approximations of the minimal perturbation. Quantitatively, how far is the paper's approximation from the minimal perturbation?
output:
The paper suggests that the DeepFool algorithm yields perturbations that are very close to the minimal perturbation, but it does not provide a single, universal quantitative measure of how far the approximation is from the absolute minimum across all scenarios. However, it offers several comparative quantitative results:

*   **Comparison to Competitive Approaches:** DeepFool estimates smaller perturbations compared to those computed using competitive approaches [1].
*   **Comparison to Fast Gradient Method:** On the ILSVRC2012 challenge dataset, the average perturbation found using DeepFool was **one order of magnitude smaller** than the perturbation estimated by the fast gradient method [1].
*   **Comparison to Other Methods:** The proposed approach yields slightly smaller perturbation vectors than the method in [3] [1].
*   **$\ell_\infty$ Norm Robustness:** When perturbations are measured using the $\ell_\infty$ norm, DeepFool still yields adversarial perturbations that are **smaller (hence closer to the optimum)** compared to other methods for computing adversarial examples [2].

In summary, while the paper emphasizes that its method is highly accurate and yields perturbations closer to the minimum than existing methods, the quantitative evidence provided is comparative (e.g., "one order of magnitude smaller," "slightly smaller") rather than a direct, absolute error bound against the true minimum perturbation.

ctxs.title: DeepFool: A Simple and Accurate Method to Fool Deep Neural Networks
ctxs.text: It can be seen that DeepFool estimates smaller perturbations (hence closer to minimal perturbation defined in (1)) than the ones computed using the competitive approaches. For example, the average perturbation obtained using DeepFool is 555 times lower than the one estimated with . On the ILSVRC2012 challenge dataset, the average perturbation is one order of magnitude smaller compared to the fast gradient method. It should be noted moreover that the proposed approach also yields slightly smaller perturbation vectors than the method in . The proposed approach is hence more accurate in detecting directions that can potentially fool neural networks. As a result, DeepFool can be used as a valuable tool to accurately assess the robustness of classifiers. On the complexity aspect, the proposed approach is substantially faster than the standard method proposed in . In fact, while the approach  involves a costly minimization of a series of objective functions, we observed empirically that DeepFool converges in a few iterations (i.e., less than 333) to a perturbation vector that fools the classifier. Hence, the proposed approach reaches a more accurate perturbation vector compared to state-of-the-art methods, while being computationally efficient. This makes it readily suitable to be used as a baseline method to estimate the robustness of very deep neural networks on large-scale datasets. In that context, we provide the first quantitative evaluation of the robustness of state-of-the-art classifiers on the large-scale ImageNet dataset. It can be seen that despite their very good test accuracy, these methods are extremely unstable to adversarial perturbations: a perturbation that is 100010001000 smaller in magnitude than the original image is sufficient to fool state-of-the-art deep neural networks.

ctxs.title: DeepFool: A Simple and Accurate Method to Fool Deep Neural Networks
ctxs.text: It should be noted that, when perturbations are measured using the ℓ∞subscriptℓ\ell_{\infty} norm, the above conclusions remain unchanged: DeepFool yields adversarial perturbations that are smaller (hence closer to the optimum) compared to other methods for computing adversarial examples. Table 2 reports the ℓ∞subscriptℓ\ell_{\infty} robustness to adversarial perturbations measured by ρ^adv∞​(f)=1|𝒟|​∑𝒙∈𝒟‖𝒓^​(𝒙)‖∞‖𝒙‖∞superscriptsubscript^𝜌adv𝑓1𝒟subscript𝒙𝒟subscriptnorm^𝒓𝒙subscriptnorm𝒙\hat{\rho}_{\text{adv}}^{\infty}(f)=\frac{1}{|\mathscr{D}|}\sum_{\bm{x}\in\mathscr{D}}\frac{\|\hat{\bm{r}}(\bm{x})\|_{\infty}}{\|\bm{x}\|_{\infty}}, where 𝒓^​(𝒙)^𝒓𝒙\hat{\bm{r}}(\bm{x}) is computed respectively using DeepFool (with p=∞𝑝p=\infty, see Section 3.3), and the Fast gradient sign method for MNIST and CIFAR-10 tasks.

ctxs.title: DeepFool: A Simple and Accurate Method to Fool Deep Neural Networks
ctxs.text: Our main contributions are the following:   •  We propose a simple yet accurate method for computing and comparing the robustness of different classifiers to adversarial perturbations.    •  We perform an extensive experimental comparison, and show that 1) our method computes adversarial perturbations more reliably and efficiently than existing methods 2) augmenting training data with adversarial examples significantly increases the robustness to adversarial perturbations.    •  We show that using imprecise approaches for the computation of adversarial perturbations could lead to different and sometimes misleading conclusions about the robustness. Hence, our method provides a better understanding of this intriguing phenomenon and of its influence factors.    We now review some of the relevant work. The phenomenon of adversarial instability was first introduced and studied in . The authors estimated adversarial examples by solving penalized optimization problems and presented an analysis showing that the high complexity of neural networks might be a reason explaining the presence of adversarial examples. Unfortunately, the optimization method employed in  is time-consuming and therefore does not scale to large datasets. In , the authors showed that convolutional networks are not invariant to some sort of transformations based on the experiments done on Pascal3D+ annotations. Recently, Tsai et al.  provided a software to misclassify a given image in a specified class, without necessarily finding the smallest perturbation. Nguyen et al.  generated synthetic unrecognizable images, which are classified with high confidence. The authors of  also studied a related problem of finding the minimal geometric transformation that fools image classifiers, and provided quantitative measure of the robustness of classifiers to geometric transformations. Closer to our work, the authors of  introduced the “fast gradient sign” method, which computes the adversarial perturbations for a given classifier very efficiently. Despite its efficiency, this method provides only a coarse approximation of the optimal perturbation vectors. In fact, it performs a unique gradient step, which often leads to sub-optimal solutions. Then in an attempt to build more robust classifiers to adversarial perturbations,  introduced a smoothness penalty in the training procedure that allows to boost the robustness of the classifier. Notably, the method in  was applied in order to generate adversarial perturbations. We should finally mention that the phenomenon of adversarial instability also led to theoretical work in  that studied the problem of adversarial perturbations on some families of classifiers, and provided upper bounds on the robustness of these classifiers. A deeper understanding of the phenomenon of adversarial instability for more complex classifiers is however needed; the method proposed in this work can be seen as a baseline to efficiently and accurately generate adversarial perturbations in order to better understand this phenomenon.

ctxs.title: DeepFool: A Simple and Accurate Method to Fool Deep Neural Networks
ctxs.text: In practice, the above algorithm can often converge to a point on the zero level set ℱℱ\mathscr{F}. In order to reach the other side of the classification boundary, the final perturbation vector 𝒓^^𝒓\hat{\bm{r}} is multiplied by a constant 1+η1𝜂1+\eta, with η≪1much-less-than𝜂1\eta\ll 1. In our experiments, we have used η=0.02𝜂0.02\eta=0.02.

answer: The authors only claim that the DeepFool can be used as a baseline for adversarial perturbation calculation and that it heavily depends on existing optimization methods [18]. In the paper, its effectiveness is proven relative to other state-of-the-art methods [20]. Although the analysis of how far the estimated perturbation from the actual minimal perturbation can be found in referenced papers, the more sophisticated analysis is not mentioned in the paper [3]. Thus, it is difficult to answer the question entirely [8].

input: According to the paper, does BERT is overfitted?
question: According to the paper, does BERT is overfitted?
output:
Based on the provided excerpt, there is **no direct statement** that BERT is overfitted.

However, the excerpt presents evidence suggesting that the performance gains of the models are **not solely due to them having seen specific concepts during pretraining**, which addresses a potential concern about overfitting to the pretraining data.

Here is a detailed breakdown of what the text implies:

* **Generalization Ability:** The key finding is that the models pretrained on all three domains "outperformed the non-pretrained model (NP)" [1].
* **Independence from Specific Topics:** The authors state that this suggests the results "do not rely on having access to text on specific topics during pretraining" [1].
* **Challenging the Overfitting Hypothesis:** They explicitly note that the higher performance on tasks like palindrome or anagram classification is "not due to the pretrained models having seen information about such concepts during pretraining" [1]. This is supported by the fact that the results generalize even to "ROC stories, which contain no information on such technical concepts" [1].

In summary, the paper argues that the models generalize well to new, unrelated corpora, suggesting their learned representations are robust rather than being narrowly overfitted to the pretraining text.

ctxs.title: What do Large Language Models Learn beyond Language?
ctxs.text: Tables 2 and 3 shows the average accuracy of six non-linguistic tasks (palindrome classification, isogram classification, tautonym classification, odd even classification, decimal operation and median) fine-tuned using different BERT and DeBERTA representations respectively. We note that the models pretrained on all three domains outperformed the non-pretrained model (NP). This suggests that the results of experiments in Section 5 generalize to new text corpora for pretraining, and do not rely on having access to text on specific topics during pretraining. This is a non-trivial result, since it suggests for example, that the higher performance of pretrained models on tasks such as palindrome and anagram classification is not due to the pretrained models having seen information about such concepts during pretraining. This is especially so since the results even generalize to ROC stories, which contain no information on such technical concepts.

answer: They say that the reason of good performance of fine-tuned model is not caused by task specific knowledge [27].

input: Why author said that they adopt a much simpler approach for SQuAD compared to past work?
question: Why author said that they adopt a much simpler approach for SQuAD compared to past work?
output:
The authors state that they adopted a much simpler approach for SQuAD compared to past work because, unlike BERT Devlin et al. (2019) and XLNet Yang et al. (2019), they **only finetuned RoBERTa using the provided SQuAD training data** [1].

This contrasts with the previous works, which **augmented their training data with additional QA datasets** [1]. Furthermore, while Yang et al. (2019) used a custom layer-wise learning rate schedule when finetuning XLNet, the authors chose to **use the same learning rate for all layers** [1].

ctxs.title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach
ctxs.text: We adopt a much simpler approach for SQuAD compared to past work. In particular, while both BERT Devlin et al. (2019) and XLNet Yang et al. (2019) augment their training data with additional QA datasets, we only finetune RoBERTa using the provided SQuAD training data. Yang et al. (2019) also employed a custom layer-wise learning rate schedule to finetune XLNet, while we use the same learning rate for all layers.

answer: Author said that they adopt a much simpler approach for SQuAD compared to past work to emphasize that they only finetune RoBERTa using the SQuAD training data, and they use the same learning rate for all layers, not like previous works [61].

input: How do they utilize fully-connected layers on dense prediction of image?
question: How do they utilize fully-connected layers on dense prediction of image?
output:
The provided excerpt mentions the use of fully connected prediction in the context of dense prediction, specifically referencing the work of Eigen et al. [1].

The text states:

*   "We also discuss in-network upsampling 3.3, of which the **fully connected prediction by Eigen et al. is a special case**." [1]

This indicates that the method discussed by Eigen et al. [1] utilized a fully connected prediction mechanism as a specific instance of in-network upsampling within the framework of Fully Convolutional Networks (FCNs) for dense prediction.

ctxs.title: Fully convolutional networks for semantic segmentation
ctxs.text: Dense prediction with convnets  Several recent works have applied convnets to dense prediction problems, including semantic segmentation by Ning et al. , Farabet et al. , and Pinheiro and Collobert ; boundary prediction for electron microscopy by Ciresan et al.  and for natural images by a hybrid neural net/nearest neighbor model by Ganin and Lempitsky ; and image restoration and depth estimation by Eigen et al. (5, 6). Common elements of these approaches include   •  small models restricting capacity and receptive fields;    •  patchwise training (27, 2, 8, 28, 11);    •  post-processing by superpixel projection, random field regularization, filtering, or local classification (8, 2, 11);    •  input shifting and output interlacing for dense output (28, 11) as introduced by OverFeat ;    •  multi-scale pyramid processing (8, 28, 11);    •  saturating tanh\tanh nonlinearities (8, 5, 28); and    •  ensembles (2, 11),    whereas our method does without this machinery. However, we do study patchwise training 3.4 and “shift-and-stitch” dense output 3.2 from the perspective of FCNs. We also discuss in-network upsampling 3.3, of which the fully connected prediction by Eigen et al.  is a special case.

answer: they used in-network upsampling [10].

input: What are transformation modules? Is this related to transformers?
question: What are transformation modules? Is this related to transformers?
output:
Based on the provided excerpt, here is a detailed answer to your question:

**What are transformation modules?**

Transformation modules are a type of approach used to adapt large-scale models when they are introduced to new concepts, without requiring the expensive process of re-training the entire model on an expanded dataset [1].

The excerpt describes these approaches as follows:
*   They involve **freezing the main model** [1].
*   They then **train these transformation modules** to modify or adapt the model's output when the model encounters these new concepts [1].

**Is this related to transformers?**

The excerpt does not explicitly state whether "transformation modules" are related to the neural network architecture known as "transformers." However, the context strongly suggests that the underlying large-scale model being discussed is likely a transformer-based model, as the entire field of text-to-image generation and large-scale generative models is heavily dominated by transformer architectures.

The text discusses methods for personalizing text-to-image generation using "Textual Inversion" [1], and the problem described—introducing new concepts without catastrophic forgetting—is a common challenge in large generative models, which are often based on transformer architectures.

In summary, while the term "transformation modules" is used generically to describe the adaptation mechanism, the context of "large scale models" and "text-to-image generation" implies a relationship with transformer-based generative models.

ctxs.title: An Image is Worth One Word: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation using Textual Inversion
ctxs.text: Introducing new concepts into large scale models is often difficult. Re-training a model with an expanded dataset for each new concept is prohibitively expensive, and fine-tuning on few examples typically leads to catastrophic forgetting (Ding et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022). More measured approaches freeze the model and train transformation modules to adapt its output when faced with new concepts (Zhou et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Skantze & Willemsen, 2022). However, these approaches are still prone to forgetting prior knowledge, or face difficulties in accessing it concurrently with newly learned concepts (Kumar et al., 2022; Cohen et al., 2022).

answer: The authors explain how trainable “transformation modules” attached to a frozen (non-trainable) base model might allow for existing models to be used for new concepts, instead of finetuning or retraining (both of which have their associated challenges) [2].

input: Why the focal loss strategy did not worked for the authors?
question: Why the focal loss strategy did not worked for the authors?
output:
The focal loss strategy did not work for the authors because it caused a decrease in their mean Average Precision (mAP) by about 2 points [1].

The authors speculate that this might be because YOLOv3 is already robust to the problem that focal loss is designed to address, given that YOLOv3 utilizes separate objectness predictions and conditional class predictions [1]. They are uncertain about the exact reason, but they hypothesize that for most examples, there might be no loss contribution from the class predictions [1].

ctxs.title: YOLOv3: An Incremental Improvement
ctxs.text: Focal loss. We tried using focal loss. It dropped our mAP about 2 points. YOLOv3 may already be robust to the problem focal loss is trying to solve because it has separate objectness predictions and conditional class predictions. Thus for most examples there is no loss from the class predictions? Or something? We aren’t totally sure.

answer: The authors hypothesize that YOLOv3 may already be robust to the problem which the focal loss is tryin to solve because it has spearate objectness predictions and conditional class predictions [26].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Visual Representation Learning via Mutual Information Regularized Assignment
ctxs.text: There has been a growing interest in using a large-scale dataset to build powerful machine learning models . Self-supervised learning (SSL), which aims to learn a useful representation without labels, is suitable for this trend; it is actively studied in the fields of natural language processing (19, 20) and computer vision (10, 30). input: What kind of output variations are possible with dreambooth?
question: What kind of output variations are possible with dreambooth?
output:
With DreamBooth, it is possible to generate new images of a specific subject while applying various types of variations guided by text prompts [1]. These variations include:

*   **Changing the subject's location** [1].
*   **Modifying the subject's properties**, such as their color or shape [1].
*   **Altering the subject's pose** [1].
*   **Changing the viewpoint** of the subject [1].
*   **Implementing other semantic modifications** [1].

ctxs.title: DreamBooth: Fine Tuning Text-to-Image Diffusion Models for Subject-Driven Generation
ctxs.text: Given only a few (typically 3-5) casually captured images of a specific subject, without any textual description, our objective is to generate new images of the subject with high detail fidelity and with variations guided by text prompts. Example variations include changing the subject location, changing subject properties such as color or shape, modifying the subject’s pose, viewpoint, and other semantic modifications. We do not impose any restrictions on input image capture settings and the subject image can have varying contexts. We next provide some background on text-to-image diffusion models (Sec. 3.1), then present our fine-tuning technique to bind a unique identifier with a subject described in a few images (Sec. 3.2), and finally propose a class-specific prior-preservation loss that enables us to overcome language drift in our fine-tuned model (Sec. 3.3).

answer: Output variants include changing the subject's location, species, color, shape, pose, expression, material, and semantics [14].

input: Why did the authors finetune the VAE on language understanding tasks?
question: Why did the authors finetune the VAE on language understanding tasks?
output:
The provided excerpt does not state that the authors finetuned a VAE on language understanding tasks. Instead, it describes how the "Optimus" model, which uses a latent space and regularization, organizes sentences and compares its setup to the BERT paper [1].

The excerpt details two schemes for applying the model:
1. **Fine-tuning ($\text{i}$):** Where "both the pre-trained model and the classifier are updated" [1].
2. **Feature-based ($\text{ii}$):** Where "pre-trained model weights are frozen to provide embeddings for the classifier update" [1].

The text discusses using the hidden feature of (CLS) as the sentence-level representation, similar to the BERT paper, and how the KL regularization impacts this feature $\boldsymbol{h}_{\texttt{(CLS)}}$ [1]. However, it does not explain *why* the authors chose to finetune the VAE specifically for language understanding tasks.

ctxs.title: Optimus: Organizing Sentences via Pre-trained Modeling of a Latent Space
ctxs.text: Due to the regularization term ℒRsubscriptℒ𝑅\mathcal{L}_{R}, Optimus can organize sentences in the way specified by the prior distribution. For basic VAEs, a smooth feature space is learned, which is specifically beneficial for better generalization when the number of task-specific labeled data is low. To have a fair comparison, we follow the BERT paper, where the hidden feature of (CLS) is used as the sentence-level representation. In this way, the linear classifiers for both models have the same number of trainable parameters. Though the latent vector 𝒛𝒛\boldsymbol{z} is typically used as sentence-level representation in VAE literature, we argue that the KL regularization applied on 𝒛𝒛\boldsymbol{z} has a large impact on the preceding layer feature 𝒉(CLS)subscript𝒉(CLS){\boldsymbol{h}}_{\texttt{(CLS)}}. Specifically, 𝒉(CLS)subscript𝒉(CLS){\boldsymbol{h}}_{\texttt{(CLS)}} is fed into an linear classifier 𝐖C∈ℝK×Hsubscript𝐖Csuperscriptℝ𝐾𝐻{{\bf W}}_{\text{C}}\in\mathbb{R}^{K\times H}, where K𝐾K is the number of classes, with objective −log⁡(softmax​(𝒉(CLS)​𝐖C⊤))softmaxsubscript𝒉(CLS)superscriptsubscript𝐖Ctop-\log(\text{softmax}({\boldsymbol{h}}_{\texttt{(CLS)}}{{\bf W}}_{\text{C}}^{\top})). Two schemes are used: (i)i(\textup{\it i}) Fine-tuning, where both the pre-trained model and the classifier are updated; (ii)ii(\textup{\it ii}) Feature-based, where pre-trained model weights are frozen to provide embeddings for the classifier update.

answer: The authors might be performing finetuning of the pretrained model and classifier weights to perform better on low resource language understanding tasks [37].

input: Which are the metrics used by authors to compare the performance of the models?
question: Which are the metrics used by authors to compare the performance of the models?
output:
The authors utilize several metrics to compare the performance of the models, depending on the specific aspect of quality they wish to measure:

*   **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID):** This is used as the **default metric for overall sample quality comparisons** because it captures both diversity and fidelity and has been the established standard in state-of-the-art generative modeling [2].
*   **Precision and Recall:** These metrics are used to separately measure aspects of the model's output:
    *   **Precision** is used to measure **fidelity**, specifically as the fraction of model samples that fall into the data manifold [2].
    *   **Recall** is used to measure **diversity** or **distribution coverage**, specifically as the fraction of data samples that fall into the sample manifold [2].
*   **Inception Score (IS):** This metric is used to measure **fidelity** [2].

The authors also mention other related metrics that have been proposed in the literature, such as:

*   **Inception Score (IS):** Proposed to measure how well a model captures the ImageNet class distribution while producing convincing individual samples [1].
*   **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID):** Proposed as a more consistent measure compared to IS, providing a symmetric distance between two image distributions in the Inception-V3 latent space [1].
*   **sFID:** A version of FID that utilizes spatial features to better capture spatial relationships [1].

When comparing against other methods, the authors re-compute Precision, Recall, and IS using public samples or models to ensure consistent comparisons [2].

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: Inception Score (IS) was proposed by Salimans et al. , and it measures how well a model captures the full ImageNet class distribution while still producing individual samples that are convincing examples of a single class. One drawback of this metric is that it does not reward covering the whole distribution or capturing diversity within a class, and models which memorize a small subset of the full dataset will still have high IS Barratt and Sharma (2018). To better capture diversity than IS, Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) was proposed by Heusel et al. , who argued that it is more consistent with human judgement than Inception Score. FID provides a symmetric measure of the distance between two image distributions in the Inception-V3 Szegedy et al. (2015) latent space. Recently, sFID was proposed by Nash et al.  as a version of FID that uses spatial features rather than the standard pooled features. They find that this metric better captures spatial relationships, rewarding image distributions with coherent high-level structure. Finally, Kynkäänniemi et al.  proposed Improved Precision and Recall metrics to separately measure sample fidelity as the fraction of model samples which fall into the data manifold (precision), and diversity as the fraction of data samples which fall into the sample manifold (recall).

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: We use FID as our default metric for overall sample quality comparisons as it captures both diversity and fidelity and has been the de facto standard metric for state-of-the-art generative modeling work Karras et al. (2019a, b); Brock et al. (2018); Ho et al. (2020). We use Precision or IS to measure fidelity, and Recall to measure diversity or distribution coverage. When comparing against other methods, we re-compute these metrics using public samples or models whenever possible. This is for two reasons: first, some papers Karras et al. (2019a, b); Ho et al. (2020) compare against arbitrary subsets of the training set which are not readily available; and second, subtle implementation differences can affect the resulting FID values Parmar et al. (2021). To ensure consistent comparisons, we use the entire training set as the reference batch Heusel et al. (2017); Brock et al. (2018), and evaluate metrics for all models using the same codebase.

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: For all comparisons in this section, we train models on ImageNet 128×\times128 with batch size 256, and sample using 250 sampling steps. We train models with the above architecture changes and compare them on FID, evaluated at two different points of training, in Table 1. Aside from rescaling residual connections, all of the other modifications improve performance and have a positive compounding effect. We observe in Figure 2 that while increased depth helps performance, it increases training time and takes longer to reach the same performance as a wider model, so we opt not to use this change in further experiments.

answer: They use FID as our default metric for overall sample quality comparisons as it captures both diversity and fidelity and has been the de facto standard metric for state-of-the-art generative modeling work [16]. Moreover, they use Precision or IS to measure fidelity, and Recall to measure diversity or distribution coverage [17]. In Table 4, they report FID, sFID, IS, Precision, and Recall as metrics [22].

input: How many hyperparameter combinations were used for the random hyperparameter search?
question: How many hyperparameter combinations were used for the random hyperparameter search?
output: A random hyperparameter search of **300 possible combinations** was conducted [1].

ctxs.title: Understanding Neural Networks Through Deep Visualization
ctxs.text: If the above regularization methods are applied individually, they are somewhat effective at producing more interpretable images; Figure 3 shows the effects of each individual hyperparameter. However, preliminary experiments uncovered that their combined effect produces better visualizations. To pick a reasonable set of hyperparameters for all methods at once, we ran a random hyperparameter search of 300 possible combinations and settled on four that complement each other well. The four selected combinations are listed in Table 1 and optimized images using each are shown for the “Gorilla” class output unit in Figure 4. Of the four, some show high frequency information, others low frequency; some contain dense pixel data, and others contain only sparse outlines of important regions. We found the version in the lower-left quadrant to be the best single set of hyperparameters, but often greater intuition can be gleaned by considering all four at once. Figure 5 shows the optimization results computed for a selection of units on all layers. A single image for every filter of all five convolutional layers is shown in Supplementary Figure S1. Nine images for each filter of all layers, including each of the 1000 ImageNet output classes, can be viewed at http://yosinski.com/deepvis.

answer: 300 sets of possible hyperparameter combinations then choose four of them that complement each other well [29].

input: Did the authors use AlexNet for evaluation of SqueezeNet?

output:

To establish a basis for comparison when evaluating SqueezeNet, the authors used "AlexNet555Our baseline is bvlc\_alexnet from the Caffe codebase Jia et al. (2014). and the associated model compression results as a basis for comparison when evaluating SqueezeNet" [1].

ctxs.title: SqueezeNet: AlexNet-level accuracy with 50x fewer parameters and<0.5MB model size
ctxs.text: We now turn our attention to evaluating SqueezeNet. In each of the CNN model compression papers reviewed in Section 2.1, the goal was to compress an AlexNet Krizhevsky et al. (2012) model that was trained to classify images using the ImageNet Deng et al. (2009) (ILSVRC 2012) dataset. Therefore, we use AlexNet555Our baseline is bvlc_alexnet from the Caffe codebase Jia et al. (2014). and the associated model compression results as a basis for comparison when evaluating SqueezeNet.

input: How does the performance change when a dense retriever is evaluated on out-of-domain queries and documents that are different from the domain on which the retriever was trained?
question: How does the performance change when a dense retriever is evaluated on out-of-domain queries and documents that are different from the domain on which the retriever was trained?
output:
The performance of a dense retriever degrades significantly when evaluated on out-of-domain queries and documents compared to the training domain.

Specifically:

*   **Domain Shift Sensitivity:** Dense retrieval methods are noted to be "extremely sensitive to domain shifts" [1].
*   **Example of Poor Performance:** A model trained on the MS MARCO dataset performs "rather poorly for questions for COVID-19 scientific literature" [1]. This poor performance occurred because the MS MARCO dataset predates COVID-19 and therefore lacks COVID-19 related topics, meaning the model did not learn how to represent these topics well in the vector space [1].
*   **Comparison to Baseline:** Even when evaluated on six selected domain-specific retrieval datasets, a state-of-the-art dense retrieval model that achieved an MRR@10 of 33.2 points on the MS MARCO passage ranking dataset performs poorly when compared to a simple BM25 lexical search [2].

ctxs.title: GPL: Generative Pseudo Labeling for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation of Dense Retrieval
ctxs.text: However, as shown in Thakur et al. (2021b), dense retrieval methods require large amounts of training data to work well.333For reference, the popular MS MARCO dataset (Nguyen et al., 2016) has about 500k training instances; the Natural Questions dataset (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019) has more than 100k training instances.  Most importantly, dense retrieval methods are extremely sensitive to domain shifts: Models trained on MS MARCO perform rather poorly for questions for COVID-19 scientific literature (Wang et al., 2020; Voorhees et al., 2021). The MS MARCO dataset was created before COVID-19, hence, it does not include any COVID-19 related topics and models did not learn how to represent this topic well in a vector space.

ctxs.title: GPL: Generative Pseudo Labeling for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation of Dense Retrieval
ctxs.text: We use the MS MARCO passage ranking dataset Nguyen et al. (2016) as the data from the source domain. It has 8.8M passages and 532.8K query-passage pairs labeled as relevant in the training set. As Table 1 shows, a state-of-the-art dense retrieval model, achieving an MRR@10 of 33.2 points on the MS MARCO passage ranking dataset, performs poorly on the six selected domain-specific retrieval datasets when compared to simple BM25 lexical search.

ctxs.title: GPL: Generative Pseudo Labeling for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation of Dense Retrieval
ctxs.text: So far, ICT and CD have only been studied on in-domain performance, i.e. a large in-domain labeled dataset is available which is used for subsequent supervised fine-tuning. SimCSE, CT, and TSDAE have been only studied for unsupervised sentence embedding learning. As our results show in Appendix E, they do not work at all for purely unsupervised dense retrieval.

ctxs.title: GPL: Generative Pseudo Labeling for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation of Dense Retrieval
ctxs.text: If these pre-training approaches can be used for unsupervised domain adaptation for dense retrieval was so far unclear. In this work, we transfer the setup from Wang et al. (2021) to dense retrieval and first pre-train on the target corpus, followed by supervised training on labeled data from MS MARCO Nguyen et al. (2016). Performance is then measured on the target corpus.

answer: It is said that when evaluating a retriever trained on a source domain in an out-of-domain setting, the performance is obtained lower than BM25 [2]. Also, dense retrievers are said to be sensitive to domain shift and models that perform well on MS MARCO do not perform well on COVID-19 data [23]. There have been many studies on unsupervised sentence embedding learning, but it is said that they do not work well in unsupervised dense retrieval [7]. Therefore, the performance of the retriever in out-of-domain may be worse [8].

input: Explain the motivation of this paper
question: Explain the motivation of this paper
output:
The motivation of this paper is to investigate whether the process of pretraining Large Language Models (LLMs) on text is solely about teaching them language, or if this pretraining process also equips them with abilities related to non-linguistic reasoning and symbolic manipulation [1].

Specifically, the authors aim to determine:

*   **The scope of learning:** Whether pretraining inherently leads to the acquisition of skills beyond mere language understanding [1].
*   **The nature of these skills:** Whether LLMs develop "helpful inductive biases for non-linguistic reasoning" [1].

To explore this, the paper sets out to see if LLMs can perform tasks such as:

*   Quantitative computation (e.g., finding the median of numbers) [1].
*   Recognizing regular expressions [1].
*   Identifying properties of strings, such as whether a string is a palindrome [1].

The study achieves this by creating a comprehensive set of 19 tasks categorized into three paradigms: quantitative computation, recognizing regular expressions, and string reasoning [1]. The paper then compares the performance of transformer and RNN-based LMs on these tasks against non-pretrained neural model variants using metrics like accuracy and sample efficiency [1].

ctxs.title: What do Large Language Models Learn beyond Language?
ctxs.text: In this paper, we explore whether pretraining on text is inherently about learning language, or if pretraining also imbues LMs with skills for symbolic manipulation and non-linguistic reasoning (for example, performing quantitative computation such as finding the median of a set of numbers, recognizing regular expressions, or identifying whether a string is a palindrome, as shown in Figure 1). In other words, we investigate whether and how pretraining develops helpful inductive biases for non-linguistic reasoning. For this analysis, we create a set of 19 tasks from three categories of task paradigms: quantitative computation (§3.1), recognizing regular expressions (§3.2), and string reasoning (§3.3). Figure 1 shows an example for each category, and the full list of tasks is described in the table 1. We experiment with transformer and RNN based LMs (§4) for learning these tasks, and perform a comparative analysis with (non-pretrained) neural model variants from the perspective of learning metrics such as accuracy and sample efficiency.

answer: The motivation of this paper is analyzing whether pretraining on text is inherently about learning language or if pretraining inject non-linguisitc reasoning to LMs [1].

input: How can we solve the chllenges of image segmentation ?
question: How can we solve the chllenges of image segmentation ?
output:
The provided excerpts discuss the challenges inherent in **instance segmentation**, which is a complex task that combines aspects of object detection and semantic segmentation [1].

Here is a detailed breakdown of the challenges and how the mentioned methods attempt to address them:

### 1. The Challenge of Instance Segmentation
Instance segmentation is difficult because it requires two things simultaneously:
*   **Object Detection:** Correctly identifying and localizing every object in an image using bounding boxes [1].
*   **Semantic Segmentation:** Precisely segmenting every instance of an object, meaning each pixel must be classified, but unlike semantic segmentation, it must also differentiate between individual instances [1].

### 2. Approaches and Their Limitations

The excerpts describe two main approaches mentioned in the context of solving this problem:

**A. Fully Convolutional Instance Segmentation (FCIS)**
*   **Method:** This approach combines a segment proposal system with an object detection system, aiming to predict outputs (classes, boxes, and masks) simultaneously using fully convolutional channels [2].
*   **Limitation:** Despite its speed, FCIS suffers from systematic errors when dealing with overlapping instances and tends to create spurious edges, indicating it is challenged by the fundamental difficulties of instance segmentation [2].

**B. Mask R-CNN**
*   **Method:** Mask R-CNN is presented as a system designed to overcome the limitations of previous methods like FCIS [3].
*   **Advantage:** The text suggests that Mask R-CNN achieves good results, even under challenging conditions, and specifically shows no systematic artifacts on overlapping instances, unlike FCIS+++ [3].

**Conclusion on Solving the Challenges:**

While the excerpts do not provide a single, definitive "solution," they illustrate an evolution in solving the problem:

*   The fundamental difficulty lies in simultaneously achieving accurate instance localization (detection) and pixel-level differentiation (segmentation) [1].
*   The development of systems like **Mask R-CNN** appears to be a successful advancement over earlier methods (like FCIS), as it demonstrates robustness against common failure modes such as artifacts on overlapping instances [3].

ctxs.title: Mask R-CNN
ctxs.text: Instance segmentation is challenging because it requires the correct detection of all objects in an image while also precisely segmenting each instance. It therefore combines elements from the classical computer vision tasks of object detection, where the goal is to classify individual objects and localize each using a bounding box, and semantic segmentation, where the goal is to classify each pixel into a fixed set of categories without differentiating object instances.111Following common terminology, we use object detection to denote detection via bounding boxes, not masks, and semantic segmentation to denote per-pixel classification without differentiating instances. Yet we note that instance segmentation is both semantic and a form of detection. Given this, one might expect a complex method is required to achieve good results. However, we show that a surprisingly simple, flexible, and fast system can surpass prior state-of-the-art instance segmentation results.

ctxs.title: Mask R-CNN
ctxs.text: Most recently, Li et al.  combined the segment proposal system in  and object detection system in  for “fully convolutional instance segmentation” (FCIS). The common idea in (8, 11, 26) is to predict a set of position-sensitive output channels fully convolutionally. These channels simultaneously address object classes, boxes, and masks, making the system fast. But FCIS exhibits systematic errors on overlapping instances and creates spurious edges (Figure 6), showing that it is challenged by the fundamental difficulties of segmenting instances.

ctxs.title: Mask R-CNN
ctxs.text: Mask R-CNN outputs are visualized in Figures 2 and 5. Mask R-CNN achieves good results even under challenging conditions. In Figure 6 we compare our Mask R-CNN baseline and FCIS+++ . FCIS+++ exhibits systematic artifacts on overlapping instances, suggesting that it is challenged by the fundamental difficulty of instance segmentation. Mask R-CNN shows no such artifacts.

answer: In this paper, It is shown that a surprisingly simple, flexible, and fast system can surpass prior state-of-the-art instance segmentation results [1]. We use object detection to denote detection via bounding boxes, not masks, and semantic segmentation to denote per-pixel classification without differentiating instances [10]. Given this, one might expect a complex method to be required to achieve good results [36].

input: Why DropPath regularization didn’t work well for NASNets?
question: Why DropPath regularization didn’t work well for NASNets?
output: The provided excerpt states that **DropPath does not work well for NASNets** [1]. However, the excerpt **does not provide a detailed explanation** as to *why* DropPath specifically did not work well for NASNets. It only contrasts its poor performance with the significant performance improvement seen when using the modified version, ScheduledDropPath [1].

ctxs.title: Learning Transferable Architectures for Scalable Image Recognition
ctxs.text: For complete details of of the architecture learning algorithm and the controller system, please refer to Appendix A. Importantly, when training NASNets, we discovered ScheduledDropPath, a modified version of DropPath , to be an effective regularization method for NASNet. In DropPath , each path in the cell is stochastically dropped with some fixed probability during training. In our modified version, ScheduledDropPath, each path in the cell is dropped out with a probability that is linearly increased over the course of training. We find that DropPath does not work well for NASNets, while ScheduledDropPath significantly improves the final performance of NASNets in both CIFAR and ImageNet experiments.

answer: Authors found that ScheduledDropPath, a modified version of DropPath works well for NASNets [22].

input: Why is it a good idea to apply the convolutions across patches of the video instead of whole frames?
question: Why is it a good idea to apply the convolutions across patches of the video instead of whole frames?
output:
The provided excerpt does not explicitly state *why* it is a good idea to apply convolutions across patches of the video instead of whole frames.

However, the text does suggest a benefit of applying this model structure:

*   Applying the model convolutionally across patches of the video and stacking multiple layers of such models is intended to **"further get improvements for supervised tasks"** [1].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Video Representations using LSTMs

Applying this model in the lower layers of a convolutional net could help extract motion information that would otherwise be lost across max-pooling layers. input: With "memory of cases" here, do they simply mean a prompt that contains all of these cases as examples?
question: With "memory of cases" here, do they simply mean a prompt that contains all of these cases as examples?
output:
No, the "memory of cases" in MemPrompt does not simply mean a prompt that contains all of these cases as examples. While the prompt is certainly *edited* to incorporate these cases, the mechanism is more dynamic and structured than just dumping every past interaction into the prompt.

Here is a detailed breakdown based on the provided excerpts:

1. **Dynamic Prompt Editing:** The core idea is that the prompt is not static; it is "edited to adapt to the input" [2]. This suggests a process where the system decides *which* memory elements to include for a specific query, rather than including everything.
2. **Retrieval Mechanism:** The system actively "retrieves a $\mathbf{fb}$ if a question similar to $\mathbf{x}$ has been asked before" [1]. This retrieval step implies a similarity matching process against past interactions, meaning only relevant feedback ($\mathbf{fb}$) is brought into the current prompt context.
3. **Structured Feedback Integration:** The memory is integrated into the prompt using specific formats to teach the model how to react to past feedback. For instance, the prompt includes examples of the form $(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{fb} \rightarrow \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{y})$ [1]. This structure is designed to make the model "react to $\mathbf{fb}$" [1].

In summary, while the memory *is* part of the prompt, the process is active: the system **retrieves** relevant feedback ($\mathbf{fb}$) and **edits** the prompt dynamically to include these specific, relevant examples, rather than just containing a static dump of every past case.

ctxs.title: MemPrompt: Memory-assisted Prompt Editing with User Feedback
ctxs.text: As mentioned, given an input 𝐱𝐱\mathbf{x}, we prompt the model to generate an output 𝐲𝐲\mathbf{y} and a sentence 𝐮𝐮\mathbf{u} expressing its understanding of the task. Thus, the in-context examples for \oursare of the form 𝐱→𝐮,𝐲→𝐱𝐮𝐲\mathbf{x}\rightarrow\mathbf{u},\mathbf{y}. In addition to the input 𝐱𝐱\mathbf{x}, \oursretrieves a 𝐟𝐛𝐟𝐛\mathbf{fb} if a question similar to 𝐱𝐱\mathbf{x} has been asked before. To enable the model to react to such feedback, we also include examples of the form (𝐱,𝐟𝐛→𝐮,𝐲)formulae-sequence→𝐱𝐟𝐛𝐮𝐲(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{fb}\rightarrow\mathbf{u},\mathbf{y}) in the prompt, which are aimed to teach the model to react to 𝐟𝐛𝐟𝐛\mathbf{fb} (Appendix D).

ctxs.title: MemPrompt: Memory-assisted Prompt Editing with User Feedback
ctxs.text: Our approach, \ours, pairs gpt-3 with a growing memory of cases where the model misunderstood user’s intent and was provided with corrective feedback. This feedback is question dependent, and thus the prompt for each sample is edited to adapt to the input. In this sense, our work can be seen as an instance of prompt engineering Liu et al. (2021b) which involves editing the prompts. Our work adds interactivity to prompt engineering as it involves dynamically updating the prompt for every instance.

answer: Yes, "memory of cases" in this context does mean a prompt with all of these relevant cases as examples listed out [18]. However, the MemPrompt model's input size is limited to 2048-tokens, so adding all possible matches to the prompt would not be possible, which is why the authors' proposed approach specifically focuses on selecting which prompts to include [2].

input: There will be differences in basic units depending on the language. As a result, what should be changed in the model, and did the author consider that part?
question: There will be differences in basic units depending on the language. As a result, what should be changed in the model, and did the author consider that part?
output:
The model needs to accommodate differences in basic units depending on the language, and the authors have addressed this in the description of the process.

Here is a detailed breakdown:

**What should be changed in the model?**

The fundamental change required in the model's input processing is that the definition of the "basic language unit" must be language-specific.

*   **For English:** The basic language unit is defined as the **word** [1].
*   **For Chinese:** The basic language unit is defined as the **Chinese Character** [1].

**Did the author consider that part?**

Yes, the authors explicitly considered this language dependency in the description of the first learning stage:

*   They state that for English, the basic unit is the word, and for Chinese, it is the Chinese Character [1].

Furthermore, the authors considered this dependency when describing the second, phrase-level masking stage, which also requires language-specific tools:

*   For English, they use **lexical analysis and chunking tools** to determine phrase boundaries [2].
*   For other languages, such as Chinese, they use **language-dependent segmentation tools** to obtain the word/phrase information [2].

ctxs.title: ERNIE: Enhanced Representation through Knowledge Integration
ctxs.text: The first learning stage is to use basic level masking, It treat a sentence as a sequence of basic Language unit, for English, the basic language unit is word, and for Chinese, the basic language unit is Chinese Character. In the training process, We randomly mask 15 percents of basic language units, and using other basic units in the sentence as inputs, and train a transformer to predict the mask units. Based on basic level mask, we can obtain a basic word representation. Because it is trained on a random mask of basic semantic units, high level semantic knowledge is hard to be fully modeled.

ctxs.title: ERNIE: Enhanced Representation through Knowledge Integration
ctxs.text: The second stage is to employ phrase-level masking. Phrase is a small group of words or characters together acting as a conceptual unit. For English, we use lexical analysis and chunking tools to get the boundary of phrases in the sentences, and use some language dependent segmentation tools to get the word/phrase information in other language such as Chinese. In phrase-level mask stage, we also use basic language units as training input, unlike random basic units mask, this time we randomly select a few phrases in the sentence, mask and predict all the basic units in the same phrase. At this stage, phrase information is encoded into the word embedding.

answer: To consider differences in basic language unit between english and chinese, authors set the basic units per language differently [18]. Then, they use some language dependent segmentation tools to get the word/phrase information [19].

input: Have the authors experimented with extending to other auxiliary tasks other than meta-path prediction?
question: Have the authors experimented with extending to other auxiliary tasks other than meta-path prediction?
output:
The authors state that while they demonstrate their framework using meta-path predictions as auxiliary tasks, the framework **could be extended to include other auxiliary tasks** [2].

Furthermore, they mention that the framework is designed to be applicable to various auxiliary tasks, as they propose SELAR to handle the challenge of "identifying useful auxiliary tasks" [2].

However, the provided excerpts do not detail any experiments where they have already extended the framework beyond meta-path prediction; they only describe the potential for such extensions [2].

ctxs.title: Self-supervised auxiliary learning with meta-paths for heterogeneous graphs
ctxs.text: We proposed meta-path prediction as self-supervised auxiliary tasks on heterogeneous graphs. Our experiments show that the representation learning on heterogeneous graphs can benefit from meta-path prediction which encourages to capture rich semantic information. The auxiliary tasks can be further improved by our proposed method SELAR, which automatically balances auxiliary tasks to assist the primary task via a form of meta-learning. The learnt weighting function identifies more beneficial meta-paths for the primary tasks. Within a task, the weighting function can adjust the cross entropy like the focal loss, which focuses on hard examples by decreasing weights for easy samples. Moreover, when it comes to challenging and remotely relevant auxiliary tasks, our HintNet helps the learner by correcting the learner’s answer dynamically and further improves the gain from auxiliary tasks. Our framework based on meta-learning provides learning strategies to balance primary task and auxiliary tasks, and easy/hard (and positive/negative) samples. Interesting future directions include applying our framework to other domains and various auxiliary tasks. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/mlvlab/SELAR.

ctxs.title: Self-supervised auxiliary learning with meta-paths for heterogeneous graphs
ctxs.text: The goal of our framework is to learn with multiple auxiliary tasks to improve the performance of the primary task. In this work, we demonstrate our framework with meta-path predictions as auxiliary tasks. But our framework could be extended to include other auxiliary tasks. The meta-paths capture diverse and meaningful relations between nodes on heterogeneous graphs . However, learning with auxiliary tasks has multiple challenges: identifying useful auxiliary tasks, balancing the auxiliary tasks with the primary task, and converting challenging auxiliary tasks into solvable (and relevant) tasks. To address the challenges, we propose SELf-supervised Auxiliary LeaRning (SELAR). Our framework consists of two main components: 1) learning weight functions to softly select auxiliary tasks and balance them with the primary task via meta-learning, and 2) learning Hint Networks to convert challenging auxiliary tasks into more relevant and solvable tasks to the primary task learner.

answer: In this paper, the authors did not conduct experiments on extending the framework to other auxiliary tasks besides meta-path prediction [28]. However, the authors mention that it is a possible direction for future work [7].

input: What are the limitations of the SRCNN in the SISR task?
question: What are the limitations of the SRCNN in the SISR task?
output:
The limitation of SRCNN in the Single Image Super-Resolution (SISR) task, as described in the excerpt, is that it is **trained for a single scale factor** [1].

Because of this limitation, SRCNN is only supposed to work with the specific scale factor it was trained on [1]. If a different scale factor is required, a completely new model must be trained [1].

To handle scenarios requiring multiple or fractional scale factors, the authors suggest that one must construct an individual single-scale SR system for every scale of interest [1].

ctxs.title: Accurate Image Super-Resolution Using Very Deep Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: Scale As in most existing SR methods, SRCNN is trained for a single scale factor and is supposed to work only with the specified scale. Thus, if a new scale is on demand, a new model has to be trained. To cope with multiple scale SR (possibly including fractional factors), we need to construct individual single scale SR system for each scale of interest.

answer: It requires context from small image regions, it converges too slowly during training, and the network only works for one set scale [15].

ctxs.title: YOLO9000: Better, Faster, Stronger
ctxs.text: This formulation also works for detection. Now, instead of assuming every image has an object, we use YOLOv2’s objectness predictor to give us the value of P​r​(physical object)𝑃𝑟physical objectPr(\text{physical object}). The detector predicts a bounding box and the tree of probabilities. We traverse the tree down, taking the highest confidence path at every split until we reach some threshold and we predict that object class.

input: Did author consider only three user behaviors on mobile usage?
question: Did author consider only three user behaviors on mobile usage?
output:
No, the authors considered more than three user behaviors on mobile usage.

One excerpt mentions that the inputs to the transformer encoder include "behavior type embeddings (retention, installation, and uninstallation)" [2]. This indicates at least three specific behavior types were considered.

However, another excerpt provides a broader context of applications using user behaviors, mentioning:
*   App install advertisements [1]
*   Mobile app recommendations [1]
*   News recommendations [1]

Therefore, the authors considered various aspects of user behavior, including installation and uninstallation status, retention, and usage patterns, not just three behaviors [2].

ctxs.title: general-purpose user embeddings based on mobile app usage
ctxs.text: User behaviors on mobile apps usage contain rich preference information and have been used in a variety of applications (Lu et al., 2014). The most significant of which is app install advertisements (Gogel, 2018; Lee and Shin, 2016) and mobile app recommendations (Zhu et al., 2014). Yahoo posted a large scale prediction engine for app install advertising based on a two-step logistic regression model considering user features generated from behaviors on apps (Bhamidipati et al., 2017). For reducing sparseness, Yahoo also classifies apps into predefined interest taxonomies when understanding app usage patterns (Radosavljevic et al., 2016). Usage patterns of apps are learned for app purchase recommendations with a Deep Memory Network(Gligorijevic et al., 2018). Beyond app install advertising, users’ app-installation behaviors are also used for news recommendations (Liu et al., 2017), where the knowledge of the neighborhood of the cold-start users is transferred from an APP domain to a new domain. A large survey on mobile app user behaviors across main app markets around the world was conducted to instruct cross-country app competitions and analyze the challenges for software engineering (Lim et al., 2014).

ctxs.title: general-purpose user embeddings based on mobile app usage
ctxs.text: The transformer encoder part receives the user retention, shared app embeddings, date embeddings, and behavior type embeddings (retention, installation, and uninstallation) as input. Thus, the inputs altogether include complete information on when and whether users install or uninstall what apps as well as their current status of app usage. The date embeddings make the transformer suitable for modeling user behaviors that are low-frequency and distribute unevenly over time. Besides, we also introduce a masked app prediction task like BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) to help extract information more productively.

answer: Authors only consider three behaviors: retention, installation, and un-installation [69]. The reason is that authors note the three behaviors, and also model behavior type embeddings of those 3 behaviors [7].

input: What are the metrics used to compare the performance of the residual network to the other models ?
question: What are the metrics used to compare the performance of the residual network to the other models ?
output:
The provided excerpts describe various comparisons made in the experiments, but they do not explicitly detail a comprehensive list of *all* the metrics used. However, we can infer the primary performance indicators mentioned:

*   **Image Classification Performance:** The primary goal of the work is image classification, and performance is compared against state-of-the-art results on benchmark datasets [1].
*   **Parameter Efficiency:** In one part of the evaluation, the Residual Attention Network is compared against origin networks (like ResNeXt and Inception) based on both **parameter efficiency** and **final performance** [1].
*   **General Performance:** The effectiveness of the attention residual learning mechanism is demonstrated by comparing the results of the attention residual learning technique against a baseline method, where the networks trained using the attention residual learning technique **consistently outperform** the baseline networks [2].

In summary, the comparisons are made using **image classification performance** on various datasets (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet), and specifically, **parameter efficiency** is also mentioned as a comparative metric [1].

ctxs.title: Residual Attention Network for Image Classification
ctxs.text: In this section, we evaluate the performance of proposed Residual Attention Network on a series of benchmark datasets including CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100 , and ImageNet . Our experiments contain two parts. In the first part, we analyze the effectiveness of each component in the Residual Attention Network including attention residual learning mechanism and different architectures of soft mask branch in the Attention Module. After that, we explore the noise resistance property. Given limited computation resources, we choose CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 dataset to conduct these experiments. Finally, we compare our network with state-of-the-art results in CIFAR dataset. In the second part, we replace the Residual Unit with Inception Module and ResNeXt to demonstrate our Residual Attention Network surpasses origin networks both in parameter efficiency and final performance. We also compare image classification performance with state-of-the-art ResNet and Inception on ImageNet dataset.

ctxs.title: Residual Attention Network for Image Classification
ctxs.text: We train these networks using different mechanisms and summarize the results in the Table 3. As shown in Table 3, the networks trained using attention residual learning technique consistently outperform the networks trained with baseline method which proves the effectiveness of our method. The performance increases with the number of Attention Module when applying attention residual learning. In contrast, the performance of networks trained with “naive attention learning” method suffers obvious degradation with increased number of Attention Module.

answer: The main metrics used to compare different methods were Top-1 and Top-5 error, test error on the CIFAR datasets, the number of parameters and the number of FLOPs [28]. The mean absolute response of output features of each stage was also used to compare their method with ResNet [32].
