They constrain b1=b2=0subscript𝑏1subscript𝑏20b_{1}=b_{2}=0, so the decision boundaries for class 1 and class 2 become ‖x‖​(‖W1‖​c​o​s​(m​θ1)−‖W2‖​c​o​s​(θ2))=0norm𝑥normsubscript𝑊1𝑐𝑜𝑠𝑚subscript𝜃1normsubscript𝑊2𝑐𝑜𝑠subscript𝜃20\left\|x\right\|\left(\left\|W_{1}\right\|cos\left(m\theta_{1}\right)-\left\|W_{2}\right\|cos\left(\theta_{2}\right)\right)=0 and ‖x‖​(‖W1‖​‖W2‖​c​o​s​(θ1)−c​o​s​(m​θ2))=0norm𝑥normsubscript𝑊1normsubscript𝑊2𝑐𝑜𝑠subscript𝜃1𝑐𝑜𝑠𝑚subscript𝜃20\left\|x\right\|\left(\left\|W_{1}\right\|\left\|W_{2}\right\|cos\left(\theta_{1}\right)-cos\left(m\theta_{2}\right)\right)=0, respectively, where m𝑚m is a positive integer introducing an angular margin, and θisubscript𝜃𝑖\theta_{i} is the angle between Wisubscript𝑊𝑖W_{i} and x𝑥x. Due to the non-monotonicity of the cosine function, a piece-wise function is applied in L-softmax to guarantee the monotonicity. The loss function is defined as follows:    ℒi=−l​o​g​(e‖Wy​i‖​‖xi‖​φ​(θy​i)e‖Wy​i‖​‖xi‖​φ​(θy​i)+∑j≠yie‖Wy​i‖​‖xi‖​c​o​s​(θj))subscriptℒ𝑖𝑙𝑜𝑔superscript𝑒normsubscript𝑊𝑦𝑖normsubscript𝑥𝑖𝜑subscript𝜃𝑦𝑖superscript𝑒normsubscript𝑊𝑦𝑖normsubscript𝑥𝑖𝜑subscript𝜃𝑦𝑖subscript𝑗subscript𝑦𝑖superscript𝑒normsubscript𝑊𝑦𝑖normsubscript𝑥𝑖𝑐𝑜𝑠subscript𝜃𝑗\mathcal{L}_{i}=-log\left(\frac{e^{\left\|W_{yi}\right\|\left\|x_{i}\right\|\varphi(\theta_{yi})}}{e^{\left\|W_{yi}\right\|\left\|x_{i}\right\|\varphi(\theta_{yi})+\sum_{j\neq y_{i}}e^{\left\|W_{yi}\right\|\left\|x_{i}\right\|cos(\theta_{j})}}}\right)  (4)   where    φ​(θ)=(−1)k​c​o​s​(m​θ)−2​k,θ∈(k​πm,(k+1)​πm)formulae-sequence𝜑𝜃superscript1𝑘𝑐𝑜𝑠𝑚𝜃2𝑘𝜃𝑘𝜋𝑚𝑘1𝜋𝑚\varphi(\theta)=(-1)^{k}cos(m\theta)-2k,\theta\in\left(\frac{k\pi}{m},\frac{(k+1)\pi}{m}\right)  (5)   Considering that L-Softmax is difficult to converge, it is always combined with softmax loss to facilitate and ensure the convergence. Therefore, the loss function is changed into: fyi=λ​‖Wyi‖​‖xi‖​c​o​s​(θyi)+‖Wyi‖​‖xi‖​φ​(θyi)1+λsubscript𝑓subscript𝑦𝑖𝜆normsubscript𝑊subscript𝑦𝑖normsubscript𝑥𝑖𝑐𝑜𝑠subscript𝜃subscript𝑦𝑖normsubscript𝑊subscript𝑦𝑖normsubscript𝑥𝑖𝜑subscript𝜃subscript𝑦𝑖1𝜆f_{y_{i}}=\frac{\lambda\left\|W_{y_{i}}\right\|\left\|x_{i}\right\|cos(\theta_{y_{i}})+\left\|W_{y_{i}}\right\|\left\|x_{i}\right\|\varphi(\theta_{y_{i}})}{1+\lambda}, where λ𝜆\lambda is a dynamic hyper-parameter. Based on L-Softmax, A-Softmax loss  further normalized the weight W𝑊W by L2 norm (‖W‖=1norm𝑊1\left\|W\right\|=1) such that the normalized vector will lie on a hypersphere, and then the discriminative face features can be learned on a hypersphere manifold with an angular margin (Fig. 6). Liu et al.  introduced a deep hyperspherical convolution network (SphereNet) that adopts hyperspherical convolution as its basic convolution operator and is supervised by angular-margin-based loss. To overcome the optimization difficulty of L-Softmax and A-Softmax, which incorporate the angular margin in a multiplicative manner, ArcFace  and CosFace , AMS loss  respectively introduced an additive angular/cosine margin c​o​s​(θ+m)𝑐𝑜𝑠𝜃𝑚cos(\theta+m) and c​o​s​θ−m𝑐𝑜𝑠𝜃𝑚cos\theta-m. They are extremely easy to implement without tricky hyper-parameters λ𝜆\lambda, and are more clear and able to converge without the softmax supervision. The decision boundaries under the binary classification case are given in Table V. Based on large margin, FairLoss  and AdaptiveFace  further proposed to adjust the margins for different classes adaptively to address the problem of unbalanced data. Compared to Euclidean-distance-based loss, angular/cosine-margin-based loss explicitly adds discriminative constraints on a hypershpere manifold, which intrinsically matches the prior that human face lies on a manifold. However, Wang et al.  showed that angular/cosine-margin-based loss can achieve better results on a clean dataset, but is vulnerable to noise and becomes worse than center loss and softmax in the high-noise region as shown in Fig. 7.

answer: Angular/cosine-margin-based loss allows the separation of learned features with larger angular/cosine distance [24].

input: What is a 128-dim correction?
question: What is a 128-dim correction?
output:
A 128-dim correction refers to the addition of a correction term with a dimension of 128, which is part of the formula for calculating $z_w$ [1].

Specifically, the calculation of $z_w$ is given by:
$$z_{w}=h^{T}CNN(chars_{w})+h^{T}Mcorr_{w}$$
[1]

In this equation, $h^{T}Mcorr_{w}$ represents the correction term, where $M$ is a matrix that projects a low-dimensional embedding vector $corr_{w}$ back up to the dimensionality of the projected LSTM hidden state of $h$ [1]. The text notes that adding this 128-dim correction halves the gap between the regular model and the CNN Softmax result, as evidenced in Table 1 [1].

ctxs.title: Attention-Based Models for Speech Recognition
ctxs.text: As TIMIT is a relatively small dataset, proper regularization is crucial. We used the adaptive weight noise as a main regularizer . We first trained our models with a column norm constraint  with the maximum norm 111 until the lowest development negative log-likelihood is achieved.333 Applying the weight noise from the beginning of training caused severe underfitting.  During this time, ϵitalic-ϵ\epsilon and ρ𝜌\rho are set to 10−8superscript10810^{-8} and 0.950.950.95, respectively. At this point, we began using the adaptive weight noise, and scaled down the model complexity cost LCsubscript𝐿𝐶L_{C} by a factor of 10, while disabling the column norm constraints. Once the new lowest development log-likelihood was reached, we fine-tuned the model with a smaller ϵ=10−10italic-ϵsuperscript1010\epsilon=10^{-10}, until we did not observe the improvement in the development phoneme error rate (PER) for 100K weight updates. Batch size 1 was used throughout the training.

input: Why are both map point matches and visual odometry matches required?
question: Why are both map point matches and visual odometry matches required?
output:
Both map point matches and visual odometry matches are utilized in the Localization Mode of ORB-SLAM2 to provide a robust localization system, each addressing different aspects of localization accuracy and drift.

Here is a detailed breakdown of why both are necessary:

*   **Visual Odometry Matches:** These matches are derived from the comparison of ORB features between the current camera frame and the 3D points that were established in the *previous* frame using stereo or depth information [1]. These matches contribute to making the localization **robust to unmapped regions** [1]. However, the text explicitly notes that relying on visual odometry alone can lead to the **accumulation of drift** [1].
*   **Map Point Matches:** These matches ensure **drift-free localization to the existing map** [1]. By matching features to points already established in the map, the system corrects for any accumulated errors from the visual odometry process, thereby maintaining high accuracy within the known environment.

In summary, visual odometry matches provide the necessary continuous tracking and robustness when venturing into areas not yet fully mapped, while map point matches serve as the crucial mechanism to constrain and correct the drift inherent in the visual odometry process when operating within the established map [1].

ctxs.title: ORB-SLAM2: an Open-Source SLAM System for Monocular, Stereo and RGB-D Cameras
ctxs.text: We incorporate a Localization Mode which can be useful for lightweight long-term localization in well mapped areas, as long as there are not significant changes in the environment. In this mode the local mapping and loop closing threads are deactivated and the camera is continuously localized by the tracking using relocalization if needed. In this mode the tracking leverages visual odometry matches and matches to map points. Visual odometry matches are matches between ORB in the current frame and 3D points created in the previous frame from the stereo/depth information. These matches make the localization robust to unmapped regions, but drift can be accumulated. Map point matches ensure drift-free localization to the existing map. This mode is demonstrated in the accompanying video.

answer: map point matches and visual odometry matches are required for Localization Mode which can be useful for lightweight long-term localization in well mapped areas, as long as there are not significant changes in the environment [30].

input: How does a "network-centric" approach differ from a "dataset-centric approach"?
question: How does a "network-centric" approach differ from a "dataset-centric approach"?
output:
The difference between a "network-centric" approach and a "dataset-centric approach" lies in the type of data required for the investigation.

*   **Dataset-centric approaches** require both a trained Deep Neural Network (DNN) and actual running data (such as images from the training or test set) to be processed through the network [1]. Examples include displaying training/test images that cause high or low activations for specific units, or using the deconvolution method to highlight image portions responsible for a unit's firing [1].
*   **Network-centric approaches**, conversely, investigate the network directly without needing any data from a dataset [2]. These methods synthesize inputs to understand the network's function. For instance, they start with an initial input ($\mathbf{x}_0$), calculate the activation ($a_i(\mathbf{x})$) of a specific unit $i$, and then iteratively adjust the input in the direction of the gradient ($\partial a_i(\mathbf{x})/\partial\mathbf{x}$) to synthesize inputs ($\mathbf{x}^*$) that cause progressively higher activations for that unit [2].

ctxs.title: Understanding Neural Networks Through Deep Visualization
ctxs.text: Another approach is to try to interpret the function computed by each individual neuron. Past studies in this vein roughly divide into two different camps: dataset-centric and network-centric. The former requires both a trained DNN and running data through that network; the latter requires only the trained network itself. One dataset-centric approach is to display images from the training or test set that cause high or low activations for individual units. Another is the deconvolution method of Zeiler & Fergus (2013), which highlights the portions of a particular image that are responsible for the firing of each neural unit.

ctxs.title: Understanding Neural Networks Through Deep Visualization
ctxs.text: Network-centric approaches investigate a network directly without any data from a dataset. For example, Erhan et al. (2009) synthesized images that cause high activations for particular units. Starting with some initial input 𝐱=𝐱𝟎𝐱subscript𝐱0\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{x_{0}}, the activation ai​(𝐱)subscript𝑎𝑖𝐱a_{i}(\mathbf{x}) caused at some unit i𝑖i by this input is computed, and then steps are taken in input space along the gradient ∂ai​(𝐱)/∂𝐱subscript𝑎𝑖𝐱𝐱\partial a_{i}(\mathbf{x})/\partial\mathbf{x} to synthesize inputs that cause higher and higher activations of unit i𝑖i, eventually terminating at some 𝐱∗superscript𝐱\mathbf{x^{*}} which is deemed to be a preferred input stimulus for the unit in question. In the case where the input space is an image, 𝐱∗superscript𝐱\mathbf{x^{*}} can be displayed directly for interpretation. Others have followed suit, using the gradient to find images that cause higher activations (Simonyan et al., 2013; Nguyen et al., 2014) or lower activations (Szegedy et al., 2013) for output units.

answer: "Dataset-centric approach" requires the trained network together with some dataset  to run through the network showing high or low responses of different units while interacting with most significant images of such dataset [6]. This approach can also use deconvolution layers and upsampling to map and highlight the regions of an image that were responsible of the firing of the different units [7].

input: How can author claim that only using absolute positional encoding with Transformer can show the relaxed structural inductive bias?
question: How can author claim that only using absolute positional encoding with Transformer can show the relaxed structural inductive bias?
output:
The authors claim that only using absolute positional encoding with the Transformer exhibits a "too relaxed structural inductive bias" because this type of encoding alone is insufficient to guarantee similar node representations even when two nodes share similar local structures [1].

The core reasons provided for this claim are:

1. **Lack of Structural Similarity Measurement:** Distance or Laplacian-based positional representations, which are often used as positional signatures, do not inherently provide a measure of structural similarity between nodes [1].
2. **Limitation in the Inductive Case:** This limitation is particularly problematic in the inductive setting, where the model must handle nodes originating from different graphs [1].

In contrast, the authors argue that the subgraph representations incorporated into their structure-aware attention mechanism are specifically designed to measure the structural similarity between nodes, which allows them to generate similar node-level representations if the nodes have similar attributes and surrounding structures [1].

ctxs.title: Structure-Aware Transformer for Graph Representation Learning
ctxs.text: We further argue that only using absolute positional encoding with the Transformer would exhibit a too relaxed structural inductive bias which is not guaranteed to generate similar node representations even if two nodes have similar local structures. This is due to the fact that distance or Laplacian-based positional representations generally serve as structural or positional signatures but do not provide a measure of structural similarity between nodes, especially in the inductive case where two nodes are from different graphs. This is also empirically affirmed in Section 5 by their relatively worse performance without using our structural encoding. In contrast, the subgraph representations used in the structure-aware attention can be tailored to measure the structural similarity between nodes, and thus generate similar node-level representations if they possess similar attributes and surrounding structures. We can formally state this in the following theorem:

answer: Author claims that Transformer only using absolute positional encoding often generates dissimilar representations for nodes with similar local structures [26].

input: What is AdaGN?
question: What is AdaGN?
output:
AdaGN, which stands for adaptive group normalization, is a layer introduced in the context of diffusion models [1].

It functions by incorporating both the **timestep** and the **class embedding** into each residual block after a group normalization operation [1].

The mathematical definition of the AdaGN layer is provided as:
$$\text{AdaGN}(h,y) = y_s \text{GroupNorm}(h) + y_b$$
where:
*   $h$ represents the intermediate activations of the residual block following the first convolution [1].
*   $y = (y_s, y_b)$ is a pair obtained from a linear projection of the timestep and class embedding [1].

This mechanism is similar to adaptive instance norm [1] and FiLM [1].

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: We also experiment with a layer Nichol and Dhariwal (2021) that we refer to as adaptive group normalization (AdaGN), which incorporates the timestep and class embedding into each residual block after a group normalization operation Wu and He (2018), similar to adaptive instance norm Karras et al. (2019a) and FiLM Perez et al. (2017). We define this layer as AdaGN​(h,y)=ys​ GroupNorm​(h)+ybAdaGNℎ𝑦subscript𝑦𝑠 GroupNormℎsubscript𝑦𝑏\text{AdaGN}(h,y)=y_{s}\text{ GroupNorm}(h)+y_{b}, where hℎh is the intermediate activations of the residual block following the first convolution, and y=(ys,yb)𝑦subscript𝑦𝑠subscript𝑦𝑏y=(y_{s},y_{b}) is obtained from a linear projection of the timestep and class embedding.

answer: AdaGN incorporates the timestep and class embedding into each residual block after a group normalization operation [69], similar to adaptive instance norm [27] and FiLM [24].

input: What is the definition of 'cycle consistency loss'?
question: What is the definition of 'cycle consistency loss'?
output:
The cycle consistency loss ($\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}$) is a mechanism introduced to regularize the learned mappings, $G$ and $F$, in an unpaired image-to-image translation task, ensuring that the translation process is reversible to a degree.

This loss is defined by two components:

1. **Forward Cycle Consistency:** For any image $x$ from the source domain $X$, applying the translation $G$ followed by $F$ should bring the image back close to the original input:
   $$ \mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}(x)}(\lVert F(G(x))-x\rVert_{1}) $$
   This enforces the cycle $x \rightarrow G(x) \rightarrow F(G(x)) \approx x$ [2].

2. **Backward Cycle Consistency:** Similarly, for any image $y$ from the target domain $Y$, applying the translation $F$ followed by $G$ should bring the image back close to the original input:
   $$ \mathbb{E}_{y\sim p_{\text{data}}(y)}(\lVert G(F(y))-y\rVert_{1}) $$
   This enforces the cycle $y \rightarrow F(y) \rightarrow G(F(y)) \approx y$ [2].

The total cycle consistency loss is the sum of these two expectations:
$$ \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}(G,F)= \mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}(x)}(\lVert F(G(x))-x\rVert_{1}) + \mathbb{E}_{y\sim p_{\text{data}}(y)}(\lVert G(F(y))-y\rVert_{1}) \quad \text{(Equation 2)} $$

This loss is designed because adversarial losses alone cannot guarantee that the learned function maps an individual input $x_i$ to a desired output $y_i$; by enforcing cycle consistency, the learned mappings $G$ and $F$ are encouraged to be consistent with each other [2].

ctxs.title: Unpaired Image-to-Image Translation Using Cycle-Consistent Adversarial Networks
ctxs.text: Cycle Consistency The idea of using transitivity as a way to regularize structured data has a long history. In visual tracking, enforcing simple forward-backward consistency has been a standard trick for decades (24, 48). In the language domain, verifying and improving translations via “back translation and reconciliation” is a technique used by human translators  (including, humorously, by Mark Twain ), as well as by machines . More recently, higher-order cycle consistency has been used in structure from motion , 3D shape matching , co-segmentation , dense semantic alignment (65, 64), and depth estimation . Of these, Zhou et al.  and Godard et al.  are most similar to our work, as they use a cycle consistency loss as a way of using transitivity to supervise CNN training. In this work, we are introducing a similar loss to push G𝐺G and F𝐹F to be consistent with each other. Concurrent with our work, in these same proceedings, Yi et al.  independently use a similar objective for unpaired image-to-image translation, inspired by dual learning in machine translation .

ctxs.title: Unpaired Image-to-Image Translation Using Cycle-Consistent Adversarial Networks
ctxs.text: Adversarial training can, in theory, learn mappings G𝐺G and F𝐹F that produce outputs identically distributed as target domains Y𝑌Y and X𝑋X respectively (strictly speaking, this requires G𝐺G and F𝐹F to be stochastic functions) . However, with large enough capacity, a network can map the same set of input images to any random permutation of images in the target domain, where any of the learned mappings can induce an output distribution that matches the target distribution. Thus, adversarial losses alone cannot guarantee that the learned function can map an individual input xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i} to a desired output yisubscript𝑦𝑖y_{i}. To further reduce the space of possible mapping functions, we argue that the learned mapping functions should be cycle-consistent: as shown in Figure 3 (b), for each image x𝑥x from domain X𝑋X, the image translation cycle should be able to bring x𝑥x back to the original image, i.e., x→G​(x)→F​(G​(x))≈x→𝑥𝐺𝑥→𝐹𝐺𝑥𝑥x\rightarrow G(x)\rightarrow F(G(x))\approx x. We call this forward cycle consistency. Similarly, as illustrated in Figure 3 (c), for each image y𝑦y from domain Y𝑌Y, G𝐺G and F𝐹F should also satisfy backward cycle consistency: y→F​(y)→G​(F​(y))≈y→𝑦𝐹𝑦→𝐺𝐹𝑦𝑦y\rightarrow F(y)\rightarrow G(F(y))\approx y. We incentivize this behavior using a cycle consistency loss:    ℒcyc​(G,F)=subscriptℒcyc𝐺𝐹absent\displaystyle\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}(G,F)= 𝔼x∼pdata​(x)​(∥F​(G​(x))−x∥1)subscript𝔼similar-to𝑥subscript𝑝data𝑥delimited-()subscriptdelimited-∥∥𝐹𝐺𝑥𝑥1\displaystyle\ \mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}(x)}(\lVert F(G(x))-x\rVert_{1})     +\displaystyle+ 𝔼y∼pdata​(y)​(∥G​(F​(y))−y∥1).subscript𝔼similar-to𝑦subscript𝑝data𝑦delimited-()subscriptdelimited-∥∥𝐺𝐹𝑦𝑦1\displaystyle\ \mathbb{E}_{y\sim p_{\text{data}}(y)}(\lVert G(F(y))-y\rVert_{1}).  (2)   In preliminary experiments, we also tried replacing the L1 norm in this loss with an adversarial loss between F​(G​(x))𝐹𝐺𝑥F(G(x)) and x𝑥x, and between G​(F​(y))𝐺𝐹𝑦G(F(y)) and y𝑦y, but did not observe improved performance.

answer: [Cyclic consistency implies that for each image x from domain X, the image translation cycle should be able to bring x back to the original image [12]. The data can be of any nature other than an image as well] [16].

input: What is the cost function of the depthwise convolution?
question: What is the cost function of the depthwise convolution?
output:
The provided excerpts describe the computational cost of a **depthwise separable convolution**, which is composed of a depthwise convolution and a $1\times 1$ pointwise convolution [1].

The computational cost of a depthwise separable convolution with a width multiplier $\alpha$ is given by Equation (6):
$$D_{K}\cdot D_{K}\cdot\alpha M\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F}+\alpha M\cdot\alpha N\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F} \quad \text{(6)}$$

However, the excerpt does not explicitly provide a separate, standalone "cost function" specifically for *only* the depthwise convolution part of the operation. Instead, it presents the total cost of the *separable* operation.

If we look at the structure of the separable convolution described in [1], the total cost is the sum of the depthwise and $1\times 1$ pointwise convolutions:
$$\text{Total Cost} = (\text{Depthwise Cost}) + (\text{Pointwise Cost}) \quad \text{[1]}$$

Equation (5) shows the total cost without a width multiplier ($\alpha=1$):
$$D_{K}\cdot D_{K}\cdot M\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F}+M\cdot N\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F} \quad \text{(5)}$$

Equation (6) shows the total cost *with* a width multiplier $\alpha$:
$$D_{K}\cdot D_{K}\cdot\alpha M\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F}+\alpha M\cdot\alpha N\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F} \quad \text{(6)}$$

Based on the structure of Equation (6), the two components are:
1. **Depthwise Convolution Cost:** $D_{K}\cdot D_{K}\cdot\alpha M\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F}$
2. **Pointwise Convolution Cost:** $\alpha M\cdot\alpha N\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F}$

Therefore, the cost associated with the **depthwise convolution** component of the depthwise separable convolution (when using a width multiplier $\alpha$) is:
$$D_{K}\cdot D_{K}\cdot\alpha M\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F} \quad \text{[6]}$$

ctxs.text: DK⋅DK⋅M⋅DF⋅DF+M⋅N⋅DF⋅DF⋅subscript𝐷𝐾subscript𝐷𝐾𝑀subscript𝐷𝐹subscript𝐷𝐹⋅𝑀𝑁subscript𝐷𝐹subscript𝐷𝐹D_{K}\cdot D_{K}\cdot M\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F}+M\cdot N\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F}  (5)   which is the sum of the depthwise and 1×1111\times 1 pointwise convolutions.

ctxs.title: MobileNets: Efficient Convolutional Neural Networks for Mobile Vision Applications
ctxs.text: The computational cost of a depthwise separable convolution with width multiplier α𝛼\alpha is:    DK⋅DK⋅α​M⋅DF⋅DF+α​M⋅α​N⋅DF⋅DF⋅⋅subscript𝐷𝐾subscript𝐷𝐾𝛼𝑀subscript𝐷𝐹subscript𝐷𝐹⋅⋅𝛼𝑀𝛼𝑁subscript𝐷𝐹subscript𝐷𝐹D_{K}\cdot D_{K}\cdot\alpha M\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F}+\alpha M\cdot\alpha N\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F}  (6)   where α∈(0,1)𝛼01\alpha\in(0,1) with typical settings of 1, 0.75, 0.5 and 0.25. α=1𝛼1\alpha=1 is the baseline MobileNet and α<1𝛼1\alpha<1 are reduced MobileNets. Width multiplier has the effect of reducing computational cost and the number of parameters quadratically by roughly α2superscript𝛼2\alpha^{2}. Width multiplier can be applied to any model structure to define a new smaller model with a reasonable accuracy, latency and size trade off. It is used to define a new reduced structure that needs to be trained from scratch.

answer: D_{K}\cdot D_{K}\cdot M\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F}+M\cdot N\cdot D_{F}\cdot D_{F} is the cost function for depthwise separable convolution [22]. With two hyperparameter settings, the function looks like this - D_{K}\cdot D_{K}\cdot\alpha M\cdot\rho D_{F}\cdot\rho D_{F}+\alpha M\cdot\alpha N\cdot\rho D_{F}\cdot\rho D_{F} [31].

input: Why author build their model jointly?
question: Why author build their model jointly?
output:
The authors built their model jointly to improve performance on a specific, challenging scenario in entity linking and coreference resolution: **mentions that lack the correct entity within their own candidate lists** [1].

Here is a detailed breakdown based on the excerpt:

* **Limitation of Standalone Models:** The authors observed that a "Standalone model cannot link such mentions, as it is limited to the local candidate list" [1]. This indicates that relying only on the immediate context or candidates available to a single mention is insufficient for these difficult cases.
* **Advantage of Joint Approaches:** In contrast, the joint models were designed to overcome this limitation. They can "solve some of these cases by using the correct candidates from other mentions in the cluster" [1].
* **Comparative Performance:** The joint approaches demonstrated superior performance compared to the standalone method, with the "Global model" performing better than the "Local one" among the joint methods [1].

In essence, the joint modeling approach allows the system to leverage information across an entire cluster of mentions, rather than being restricted to the limited candidates available to any single mention, thereby tackling a "corner case" that the standalone models fail at [1].

ctxs.title: Joint Models for Entity Linking and Coreference Resolution
ctxs.text: In order to tackle research question (Q3), we study the accuracy of our models on the important corner case that involves mentions without correct entity in their candidate lists. This is illustrated in Table 4, which focuses on such mentions in clusters where at least one mention contains the correct entity in its candidate list. As expected, the Standalone model cannot link such mentions, as it is limited to the local candidate list. In contrast, both our joint approaches can solve some of these cases by using the correct candidates from other mentions in the cluster, with a superior performance of our Global model compared to the Local one.

answer: They build their model jointly to make model can use the correct candidates from other mentions in the cluster [16].

input: Does it have to be integrated into the network in an end-to-end manner? I guess it could make the network heavier.
question: Does it have to be integrated into the network in an end-to-end manner? I guess it could make the network heavier.
output:
The provided excerpts do not directly answer whether the integration of temporal information "has to be integrated into the network in an end-to-end manner" or if it inherently makes the network heavier. However, they do discuss various architectural choices regarding the integration of temporal and spatial dimensions, some of which address computational load:

*   **Computational Load Concerns:** The text explicitly mentions that using **3D convolutional layers is computationally heavy** [2].
*   **Approximation Strategies:** Because of this computational burden, the authors employ strategies to avoid full spatiotemporal integration. For instance, they extend a dimension decomposition strategy to attention layers, where they stack a **temporal attention layer after each spatial attention layer** to approximate a full spatiotemporal attention layer [2].
*   **Comparison to Other Methods:** The authors contrast their approach with other models:
    *   One method (CogVideo) adds temporal layers to each (frozen) spatial layer, while the current work **trains them jointly** [3].
    *   Another method (VDM) extended its 2D U-Net to 3D using unflattened 1x3x3 convolution filters, while the current work applies an **additional 3x1x1 convolution projection** after each 1x3x3 layer to pass temporal information through the convolution layers [3].

In summary, while the excerpts don't give a definitive "yes" or "no" on the necessity of end-to-end integration, they demonstrate that the authors are actively managing the computational weight by using **factorized or decomposed attention/convolutional layers** rather than relying solely on monolithic 3D structures [2, 3].

ctxs.title: Make-A-Video: Text-to-Video Generation without Text-Video Data-VIDEO DATA
ctxs.text: In order to expand the two-dimensional (2D) conditional network into the temporal dimension, we modify the two key building blocks that now require not just spatial but also temporal dimensions in order to generate videos: (i) Convolutional layers (Sec. 3.2.1), and (ii) attention layers (Sec. 3.2.2), discussed in the following two subsections. Other layers, such as fully-connected layers, do not require specific handling when adding an additional dimension, as they are agnostic to structured spatial and temporal information. Temporal modifications are made in most U-Net-based diffusion networks: the spatiotemporal decoder DtsuperscriptDt\operatorname{D^{t}} now generating 161616 RGB frames, each of size 64×64646464\times 64, the newly added frame interpolation network ↑Fsubscript↑𝐹\uparrow_{F}, increasing the effective frame rate by interpolating between the 161616 generated frames (as depicted in Fig. 2), and the super-resolution networks SRltsuperscriptsubscriptSR𝑙𝑡\operatorname{SR}_{l}^{t}.

ctxs.title: Make-A-Video: Text-to-Video Generation without Text-Video Data-VIDEO DATA
ctxs.text: A crucial component of T2I networks is the attention layer, where in addition to self-attending to extracted features, text information is injected to several network hierarchies, alongside other relevant information, such as the diffusion time-step. While using 3D convolutional layers is computationally heavy, adding the temporal dimension to attention layers is outright infeasible in terms of memory consumption. Inspired by the work of (Ho et al., 2022), we extend our dimension decomposition strategy to attention layers as well. Following each (pre-trained) spatial attention layer, we stack a temporal attention layer, which as with the convolutional layers, approximates a full spatiotemporal attention layer. Specifically, given an input tensor hℎh, we define f​l​a​t​t​e​n𝑓𝑙𝑎𝑡𝑡𝑒𝑛flatten as a matrix operator that flattens the spatial dimension into h′∈RB×C×F×H​Wsuperscriptℎ′superscript𝑅𝐵𝐶𝐹𝐻𝑊h^{\prime}\in R^{B\times C\times F\times HW}. u​n​f​l​a​t​t​e​n𝑢𝑛𝑓𝑙𝑎𝑡𝑡𝑒𝑛unflatten is defined as the inverse matrix operator. The Pseudo-3D attention layer therefore is therefore defined as:

ctxs.title: Make-A-Video: Text-to-Video Generation without Text-Video Data-VIDEO DATA
ctxs.text: Factorized space-time attention layers have also been used in VDM (Ho et al., 2022) and CogVideo (Hong et al., 2022). CogVideo has added temporal layers to each (frozen) spatial layers whereas we train them jointly. In order to force their network to train for images and videos interchangeably, VDM has extended their 2D U-Net to 3D through unflattened 1x3x3 convolution filters, such that the subsequent spatial attention remains 2D, and added 1D temporal attention through relative position embeddings. In contrast, we apply an additional 3x1x1 convolution projection (after each 1x3x3) such that the temporal information will also be passed through each convolution layer.

answer:
They extend the spatial layers at the model initialization stage, to include temporal information, and the extended spatial-temporal network learn significantly accelerates the T2V training process by instantaneously transferring the knowledge
from a previously trained T2I network to a new T2V one [14]. Because of the fact that using 3D convolutional layers is computationally heavy, they followed the work of (Ho et al, 2022) extending dimension decomposition strategy to attention layers [20]. In contrast to VDM, they apply an additional 3x1x1 convolution projection (after each 1x3x3) such that the temporal information will also be passed through each convolution layer [23].

*   **PromptSource** [1]
*   **Super-NaturalInstructions (SuperNI)** [1]

The excerpt notes that these datasets are examples of human-written instruction data used in the development of instruction-tuned models [1].

ctxs.title: Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Model with Self Generated Instructions
ctxs.text: The recent NLP literature has witnessed a tremendous amount of activity in building models that can follow natural language instructions (Mishra et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022; Sanh et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022; Chung et al., 2022, i.a.). These developments are powered by two key components: large pretrained language models (LM) and human-written instruction data (e.g., PromptSource (Bach et al., 2022) and Super-NaturalInstructions (Wang et al., 2022, SuperNI for short)). However, collecting such instruction data is costly and often suffers limited diversity given that most human generations tend to be popular NLP tasks, falling short of covering a true variety of tasks and different ways to describe them. Continuing to improve the quality and coverage of instruction-tuned models necessitates the development of alternative approaches for supervising the instruction tuning process.

answer: PromptSource and Super-NaturalInstructions (also called "super-NI" in short) are two existing public datasets that contain instructions for tuning large language models [0].

input: What is the goal behind using a single model SR approach?
question: What is the goal behind using a single model SR approach?
output:
The goal behind using a single model for Super-Resolution (SR) is to address the inefficiency and impracticality of the traditional approach when dealing with multiple scales [2].

Here is a detailed breakdown based on the provided text:

1. **Problem with Single-Scale Methods:** Existing methods, such as SRCNN, are trained for only one specific scale factor and are designed to work only with that specified scale [1]. If a different scale is required, a completely new model must be trained [1].
2. **Inefficiency of Multi-Scale Adaptation:** To handle multiple scales (including fractional factors), the traditional method requires constructing a separate, individual single-scale SR system for *each* scale of interest [1].
3. **The Solution (Single Model):** Preparing and maintaining many individual machines or models for every possible scenario to cope with multiple scales is described as "inefficient and impractical" [2]. Therefore, the goal of this work is to design and train **a single network** capable of handling the multiple-scale SR problem efficiently [2].

This single-network approach proves highly effective, performing comparably to a single-scale expert for a given sub-task, and offers significant resource savings; for instance, when handling three scale factors ($\times2, 3, 4\times$), the number of parameters can be reduced by three-fold [2].

ctxs.title: Accurate Image Super-Resolution Using Very Deep Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: Scale As in most existing SR methods, SRCNN is trained for a single scale factor and is supposed to work only with the specified scale. Thus, if a new scale is on demand, a new model has to be trained. To cope with multiple scale SR (possibly including fractional factors), we need to construct individual single scale SR system for each scale of interest.

ctxs.title: Accurate Image Super-Resolution Using Very Deep Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: However, preparing many individual machines for all possible scenarios to cope with multiple scales is inefficient and impractical. In this work, we design and train a single network to handle multiple scale SR problem efficiently. This turns out to work very well. Our single machine is compared favorably to a single-scale expert for the given sub-task. For three scales factors (×2,3,4\times 2,3,4), we can reduce the number of parameters by three-fold.

answer: Existing methods are only trained for a single scale, so adapting them to other scales requires retraining [15]. However, this would be impractical, so having a single model that accepts multiple scales would fix the problem [16].

ctxs.title: What do Large Language Models Learn beyond Language?
ctxs.text: This task paradigm focuses on reasoning tasks over individual strings or pairs of strings.  Palindrome classification. A string is a palindrome if it reads the same forward and backward. The task is to classify whether a given string is a palindrome. The string length ranges from 1 to 15. Anagram classification. Two strings are anagrams if one is formed by rearranging letters from the other. The task is to classify if a pair of strings are anagrams. The string length ranges from 2 to 15. Isogram classification. A string is an isogram if it has no repeating characters. The task is to classify whether a given string is an isogram. The string length ranges from 1 to 52. Tautonym classification. A tautonym is a word which can be broken down into two identical parts, with the same spelling. The task is to classify whether a given string is a tautonym. The string length ranges from 1 to 10.  Length of a string. Output the length of a given string. The string length ranges from 1 to 10. Count of unique characters. Given a string, count the number of unique characters in it. The string lengths ranges from 10 to 30. Parity check. Given a binary string, output if the counts of ones and zeros are the same. The maximum length of the binary string is 20. Vowels classification. Given a string, classify if the string contains only vowel characters. The string length ranges from 3 to 10.  Maximum frequent character. Given a string, output the character with the maximum frequency. The string length ranges from 5 to 30.

answer: Calculating length of a string is not a string reasoning task because it does not require character composition within or with another string [11].

ctxs.text: There are two types of metrics to evaluate language VAEs. (i)i(\textup{\it i}) Generation capability: we use perplexity (PPL). Note that NLM and GPT-2 has exactly PPL, while VAEs does not. Following He et al. (2019), we use the importance weighted bound in Burda et al. (2015) to approximate log⁡p​(𝒙)𝑝𝒙\log p(\boldsymbol{x}), and report PPL. We report the full results with ELBO, KL and Reconstruction in Appendix, but note that higher ELBO does not necessarily yield better language modeling.

output:
Based on the provided excerpt, the text does not explicitly state whether fine-tuning dense or sparse retrievers on a target corpus will allow them to outperform lexical models.

However, the excerpt does offer some relevant insights:

*   **Performance vs. Efficiency:** The text notes that there is a trade-off between performance and computational cost, and that "More efficient approaches e.g. based on dense or sparse embeddings can substantially underperform traditional lexical models like BM25" [1].
*   **Generalization:** It also mentions that "the in-domain performance of a model does not correlate well with its generalization capabilities: models fine-tuned with identical training data might generalize differently" [1].

While the excerpt suggests that even when using dense or sparse embeddings, they can underperform lexical models, it does not provide a definitive answer regarding the impact of *fine-tuning* on target corpus awareness.

ctxs.title: BEIR: A Heterogeneous Benchmark for Zero-shot Evaluation of Information Retrieval Models
ctxs.text: We use beir to evaluate ten diverse retrieval methods from five broad architectures: lexical, sparse, dense, late interaction, and re-ranking. From our analysis, we find that no single approach consistently outperforms other approaches on all datasets. Further, we notice that the in-domain performance of a model does not correlate well with its generalization capabilities: models fine-tuned with identical training data might generalize differently. In terms of efficiency, we find a trade-off between the performances and the computational cost: computationally expensive models, like re-ranking models and late interaction model perform the best. More efficient approaches e.g. based on dense or sparse embeddings can substantially underperform traditional lexical models like BM25. Overall, BM25 remains a strong baseline for zero-shot text retrieval.

answer: If dense/sparse retrievers are pre-trained on target corpus to enable the retrievers to be corpus-aware, the fine-tuned retrievers underperform lexical models [4].

input: How has the evolution of network architectures in deep face recognition systems, such as the transition from AlexNet to ResNet and SENet, impacted the performance of these systems?
question: How has the evolution of network architectures in deep face recognition systems, such as the transition from AlexNet to ResNet and SENet, impacted the performance of these systems?
output:
The evolution of network architectures in deep face recognition (FR) systems has been closely tied to advancements in training techniques and has led to a continuous improvement in performance.

Here is a detailed breakdown of the impact based on the provided text:

* **General Trend:** The mainstream architectures in deep FR have generally followed the trajectory of deep object classification architectures, evolving rapidly from models like AlexNet to SENet [1]. The adoption of advanced training techniques, such as batch normalization (BN), allowed networks to become deeper and made the training process more controllable, leading to continual performance improvements in deep FR [2].

* **Key Architectural Milestones and Performance:** Specific architectural advancements led to significant performance boosts:
    * **DeepFace (2014):** This early work utilized a **nine-layer CNN** and achieved an accuracy of **97.35% on LFW** when incorporating 3D alignment for face processing [2]. This performance was noted as approaching human performance on unconstrained conditions for the first time [3].
    * **FaceNet (2015):** This system utilized a **GoogleNet** architecture and achieved a good performance of **99.63%** by employing a triplet loss function based on triplets generated via an online triplet mining method [2].
    * **VGGface (2015):** This approach trained the **VGGNet** on a large-scale dataset and fine-tuned it using a triplet loss function similar to FaceNet, obtaining an accuracy of **98.95%** [2].
    * **SphereFace (2017):** This system employed a **64-layer ResNet architecture** and introduced the angular softmax (A-Softmax) loss to learn discriminative features, boosting accuracy to **99.42% on LFW** [2].
    * **Later Developments (2017):** Following the introduction of the VGGface2 dataset, Cao et al. trained a **SENet** using the MS-celeb-1M dataset and subsequently fine-tuned it with VGGface2, achieving the State-of-the-Art (SOTA) performance on the IJB-A and IJB-B benchmarks [2].

In summary, the progression from foundational architectures (like the 9-layer CNN in DeepFace) through more complex, deeper structures (like ResNet in SphereFace) and specialized architectures (like SEN

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: Mainstream architectures. The commonly used network architectures of deep FR have always followed those of deep object classification and evolved from AlexNet to SENet rapidly. We present the most influential architectures of deep object classification and deep face recognition in chronological order 111The time we present is when the paper was published. in Fig. 8.

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: With the evolved architectures and advanced training techniques, such as batch normalization (BN), the network becomes deeper and the training becomes more controllable. Following these architectures in object classification, the networks in deep FR are also developed step by step, and the performance of deep FR is continually improving. We present these mainstream architectures of deep FR in Fig. 9. In 2014, DeepFace  was the first to use a nine-layer CNN with several locally connected layers. With 3D alignment for face processing, it reaches an accuracy of 97.35% on LFW. In 2015, FaceNet  used a large private dataset to train a GoogleNet. It adopted a triplet loss function based on triplets of roughly aligned matching/nonmatching face patches generated by a novel online triplet mining method and achieved good performance of 99.63%. In the same year, VGGface  designed a procedure to collect a large-scale dataset from the Internet. It trained the VGGNet on this dataset and then fine-tuned the networks via a triplet loss function similar to FaceNet. VGGface obtains an accuracy of 98.95%. In 2017, SphereFace  used a 64-layer ResNet architecture and proposed the angular softmax (A-Softmax) loss to learn discriminative face features with angular margin. It boosts the achieves to 99.42% on LFW. In the end of 2017, a new large-scale face dataset, namely VGGface2 , was introduced, which consists of large variations in pose, age, illumination, ethnicity and profession. Cao et al. first trained a SENet with MS-celeb-1M dataset  and then fine-tuned the model with VGGface2 , and achieved the SOTA performance on the IJB-A  and IJB-B .

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: In 2014, DeepFace  achieved the SOTA accuracy on the famous LFW benchmark , approaching human performance on the unconstrained condition for the first time (DeepFace: 97.35% vs. Human: 97.53%), by training a 9-layer model on 4 million facial images. Inspired by this work, research focus has shifted to deep-learning-based approaches, and the accuracy was dramatically boosted to above 99.80% in just three years. Deep learning technique has reshaped the research landscape of FR in almost all aspects such as algorithm designs, training/test datasets, application scenarios and even the evaluation protocols. Therefore, it is of great significance to review the breakthrough and rapid development process in recent years. There have been several surveys on FR (24, 25, 26, 27, 28) and its subdomains, and they mostly summarized and compared a diverse set of techniques related to a specific FR scene, such as illumination-invariant FR , 3D FR , pose-invariant FR . Unfortunately, due to their earlier publication dates, none of them covered the deep learning methodology that is most successful nowadays. This survey focuses only on recognition problem, and one can refer to Ranjan et al.  for a brief review of a full deep FR pipeline with detection and alignment, or refer to Jin et al.  for a survey of face alignment. Specifically, the major contributions of this survey are as follows:

answer: As deep FR models followed the footsteps of deep object classification network architectures the performance got better, training got more controllable, and models got deeper [27]. It started with DeepFace which was based on AlexNet that achieved 9735% on the LFW benchmark [29]. Then came the FaceNet based on GoogleNet which achieved 9963% [3].

input: What added benefits do the ILSVRC provide over the existing PASCAL-VOC challenge?
question: What added benefits do the ILSVRC provide over the existing PASCAL-VOC challenge?
output:
The ILSVRC (ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge) provides several added benefits over the PASCAL VOC dataset, primarily related to scale and scope:

* **Massive Increase in Scale:** ILSVRC scales up the goal of standardized training and evaluation set by PASCAL VOC by "more than an order of magnitude in number of object classes and images" [2]. For instance, PASCAL VOC 2012 featured 20 object classes and 21,738 images, whereas ILSVRC 2012 included 1000 object classes and 1,431,167 annotated images [2].
* **Broader Scope of Recognition:** While PASCAL VOC provides a standardized test bed for tasks like object detection, image classification, object segmentation, person layout, and action classification [2], ILSVRC builds upon this foundation through its sheer size and complexity [2].
* **Advanced Localization Analysis:** ILSVRC allows for a deeper analysis of object localization difficulty by comparing statistics from its validation sets against the PASCAL VOC benchmark [3].

In summary, ILSVRC's primary advantage is its massive scale—significantly more classes and images than PASCAL VOC—which drives significant algorithmic advances in large-scale image recognition and retrieval [1, 2].

ctxs.title: ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge
ctxs.text: The ILSVRC dataset and the competition has allowed significant algorithmic advances in large-scale image recognition and retrieval.

ctxs.title: ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge
ctxs.text: The closest to ILSVRC is the PASCAL VOC dataset (Everingham et al.,, 2010, 2014), which provides a standardized test bed for object detection, image classification, object segmentation, person layout, and action classification. Much of the design choices in ILSVRC have been inspired by PASCAL VOC and the similarities and differences between the datasets are discussed at length throughout the paper. ILSVRC scales up PASCAL VOC’s goal of standardized training and evaluation of recognition algorithms by more than an order of magnitude in number of object classes and images: PASCAL VOC 2012 has 20 object classes and 21,738 images compared to ILSVRC2012 with 1000 object classes and 1,431,167 annotated images.

We compute statistics on the ILSVRC2012 single-object localization validation set images compared to PASCAL VOC 2012 validation images.

answer: Images from the ILSVRC2012 single-object localization validation set are compared to images from the PASCAL VOC benchmark for object recognition [102]. They have also analyzed the level of difficulty of object localization in these images compared to those of objects from the PASCAL VOC benchmark [13]. The level of difficulty of object localization is also analyzed [62].

input: What is catastrophic forgetting?
question: What is catastrophic forgetting?
output: Catastrophic forgetting occurs when the fine-tuning process is too aggressive [1]. If the fine-tuning is overly aggressive, it can eliminate the benefit of the information that was captured during the language modeling phase [1].

ctxs.title: Universal Language Model Fine-tuning for Text Classification
ctxs.text: Fine-tuning the target classifier is the most critical part of the transfer learning method. Overly aggressive fine-tuning will cause catastrophic forgetting, eliminating the benefit of the information captured through language modeling; too cautious fine-tuning will lead to slow convergence (and resultant overfitting). Besides discriminative fine-tuning and triangular learning rates, we propose gradual unfreezing for fine-tuning the classifier.

answer: Catastrophic forgetting involved increasing error as a model start to overfit and knowledge captured through pretraining is lost [24].

ctxs.title: MobileNets: Efficient Convolutional Neural Networks for Mobile Vision Applications
ctxs.text: Convolutional neural networks have become ubiquitous in computer vision ever since AlexNet  popularized deep convolutional neural networks by winning the ImageNet Challenge: ILSVRC 2012 . The general trend has been to make deeper and more complicated networks in order to achieve higher accuracy (27, 31, 29, 8). However, these advances to improve accuracy are not necessarily making networks more efficient with respect to size and speed. In many real world applications such as robotics, self-driving car and augmented reality, the recognition tasks need to be carried out in a timely fashion on a computationally limited platform.

input: What is an example of a "dataset-centric" approach?
question: What is an example of a "dataset-centric" approach?
output: An example of a "dataset-centric" approach is to display images from the training or test set that cause high or low activations for individual units [1]. Another dataset-centric method mentioned is the deconvolution method of Zeiler & Fergus (2013), which aims to highlight the parts of a specific image that are responsible for the activation of each neural unit [1].

ctxs.title: Understanding Neural Networks Through Deep Visualization
ctxs.text: Another approach is to try to interpret the function computed by each individual neuron. Past studies in this vein roughly divide into two different camps: dataset-centric and network-centric. The former requires both a trained DNN and running data through that network; the latter requires only the trained network itself. One dataset-centric approach is to display images from the training or test set that cause high or low activations for individual units. Another is the deconvolution method of Zeiler & Fergus (2013), which highlights the portions of a particular image that are responsible for the firing of each neural unit.

answer: An example of "dataset-centric" approach can be deconvolution method which is used to highlight certain regions of some image that has the highest effects in the response of different units [6].

input: Can the methods of "one-to-many augmentation" like data augmentation and 3D face reconstruction effectively improve the performance of deep FR algorithms in terms of accuracy and diversity of training data?
question: Can the methods of "one-to-many augmentation" like data augmentation and 3D face reconstruction effectively improve the performance of deep FR algorithms in terms of accuracy and diversity of training data?
output:
Yes, the methods of "one-to-many augmentation," which include data augmentation and 3D model approaches, can effectively improve the performance of deep Face Recognition (FR) algorithms by mitigating the challenges associated with large-scale data collection [1].

Here is a detailed breakdown based on the provided excerpts:

*   **Mitigating Data Collection Challenges:** The core purpose of these augmentation methods is to address the fact that collecting a large database for deep FR is "extremely expensive and time consuming" [1]. By using "one-to-many augmentation," researchers can artificially increase the diversity and quantity of the available data, which benefits both the training set and the gallery of test data [1].

*   **Effectiveness of Data Augmentation:** Data augmentation is a widely adopted technique in deep FR algorithms [2]. Common methods fall into two categories:
    *   **Photometric Transformations:** These involve changes to the image's color or brightness properties [2].
    *   **Geometric Transformations:** These include techniques such as:
        *   **Mirroring:** Flipping the image [2].
        *   **Rotating:** Changing the orientation of the image [2].
        *   **Oversampling:** Obtaining multiple patches by cropping the image at various scales [2].

*   **Examples of Application:** The excerpts provide examples demonstrating how these augmentations are applied to enhance training:
    *   One study involved cropping 400 face patches that varied in position, scale, and color channels, and also mirrored the images [2].
    *   Another example involved generating seven overlapped image patches centered at different facial landmarks and training separate CNNs on each patch [2].

In summary, these augmentation techniques allow researchers to artificially expand the dataset's diversity and volume, thereby helping to improve the robustness and performance of the deep FR algorithms despite limitations in real-world data acquisition [1, 2].

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: Collecting a large database is extremely expensive and time consuming. The methods of “one-to-many augmentation” can mitigate the challenges of data collection, and they can be used to augment not only training data but also the gallery of test data. we categorized them into four classes: data augmentation, 3D model, autoencoder model and GAN model.

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: Data augmentation. Common data augmentation methods consist of photometric transformations (75, 22) and geometric transformations, such as oversampling (multiple patches obtained by cropping at different scales) , mirroring , and rotating  the images. Recently, data augmentation has been widely used in deep FR algorithms (58, 59, 60, 35, 21, 36, 61, 62). for example, Sun et al.  cropped 400 face patches varying in positions, scales, and color channels and mirrored the images. Liu et al.  generated seven overlapped image patches centered at different landmarks on the face region and trained them with seven CNNs with the same structure.

answer: In terms of accuracy, the paper mentions a set of work done on assembled multi-input networks that used "one-to-many augmentation" methods to expand their dataset and achieve better results compared to individual networks [40]. In terms of diversity, all data augmentation, 3D face reconstruction, autoencoders, and especially GANs were found to be effective in generating faces in certain poses, angles, with different expressions, etc [41].

input: How the features are converted to pixel labels in SegNet?
question: How the features are converted to pixel labels in SegNet?
output:
The features are converted to pixel labels in SegNet through a **feed-forward mapping** process using a **deep convolutional encoder-decoder architecture** [1].

Here is a detailed breakdown based on the excerpt:

* **Architecture:** SegNet is a fully trainable deep architecture designed for joint feature learning and mapping an input image to its pixel-wise semantic labels [1].
* **Process:** The mapping occurs in a feed-forward manner [1].
* **Mechanism:** The architecture utilizes an **encoder-decoder** structure [1]. The encoder part generates deep layers of feature encoding that capture a large spatial context for each pixel [1]. The decoder then uses these low-resolution encoder feature maps to produce the semantic labels [1].
* **Key Innovation:** The paper highlights that SegNet is among the first deep learning methods to learn how to map these low-resolution encoder feature maps to the semantic labels [1].

This process results in an output that produces **smooth segment labels**, which is an advantage when compared to methods that rely on local patch-based classifiers [1].

ctxs.title: SegNet: A Deep Convolutional Encoder-Decoder Architecture for Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: We presented SegNet, a fully trainable deep architecture for joint feature learning and mapping an input image in a feed-forward manner to its pixel-wise semantic labels. A highlight of the proposed architecture is its ability to produce smooth segment labels when compared with local patch based classifiers. This is due to deep layers of feature encoding that employ a large spatial context for pixel-wise labelling. To the best of our knowledge this is the first deep learning method to learn to map low resolution encoder feature maps to semantic labels. Both qualitative and numerical accuracy of the SegNet for outdoor and indoor scenes is very competitive, even without use of any CRF post-processing. We have also demonstrated the use of pre-trained SegNet for obtaining good performance on other datasets with a small extra computational effort. The encoder-decoder architecture of the SegNet can also be trained unsupervised and to handle missing data in the input during test time.

answer: SegNet performs feed-forward computation to obtain pixel-wise labelling [13].

input: What are the benefits of using the predictor to calculate user-item interaction score instead of directly encoding into their inner product?
question: What are the benefits of using the predictor to calculate user-item interaction score instead of directly encoding into their inner product?
output:
The benefit of using the predictor to model the user-item interaction, as done in BUIR, instead of directly encoding the interactions into the inner product (or Euclidean distance) as done by existing discriminative OCCF methods, is that it allows the model to **encode the high-level relationship between users and items into their representations** [1].

Furthermore, by utilizing the predictor, BUIR can **accurately compute the user-item interaction scores** and **optimize the representation without explicitly using negative samples** [1].

ctxs.title: Bootstrapping User and Item Representations for One-Class Collaborative Filtering
ctxs.text: Existing discriminative OCCF methods (Rendle et al., 2009; Hsieh et al., 2017) have tried to optimize the latent space where the user-item interactions are directly encoded into their inner product (or Euclidean distance). On the contrary, BUIR additionally uses the predictor to model their interaction, which results in the capability of encoding the high-level relationship between users and items into the representations. In conclusion, with the help of the predictor, BUIR accurately computes the user-item interaction scores as well as optimizes the representation without explicitly using negative samples.

answer: Using predictor can optimize the representation without any negative sample [21].

input: Why the training was unstable without these activations scaled before addition?
question: Why the training was unstable without these activations scaled before addition?
output:
The provided excerpts do not explicitly state *why* the training was unstable without scaling the residuals before addition. However, they do describe the instability and the proposed solutions:

*   **Instability Observation:** Instability was observed in the context of very deep residual networks [2].
*   **Proposed Solutions/Observations:**
    *   One approach suggested by He et al. was a two-phase training process: an initial "warm-up" phase using a very low learning rate, followed by a second phase with a high learning rate [2].
    *   The authors of the paper found that if the number of filters was very high, even an extremely low learning rate (0.00001) was insufficient to manage the instabilities, and the subsequent high learning rate phase risked destroying any positive effects [2].
    *   The authors found that **scaling the residuals** (using factors between 0.1 and 0.3) before adding them to the previous layer activation seemed to stabilize the training [1].

In summary, while the excerpts don't detail the underlying mechanism of the instability, they indicate that the instability was particularly pronounced in **very deep residual networks** and when the **number of filters was very high** [2]. Scaling the residuals was found to be a more reliable method for stabilization compared to the two-phase learning rate approach [2].

ctxs.title: Inception-v4, Inception-ResNet and the Impact of Residual Connections on Learning
ctxs.text: We found that scaling down the residuals before adding them to the previous layer activation seemed to stabilize the training. In general we picked some scaling factors between 0.1 and 0.3 to scale the residuals before their being added to the accumulated layer activations (cf. Figure 20).

ctxs.title: Inception-v4, Inception-ResNet and the Impact of Residual Connections on Learning
ctxs.text: A similar instability was observed by He et al. in  in the case of very deep residual networks and they suggested a two-phase training where the first “warm-up” phase is done with very low learning rate, followed by a second phase with high learning rata. We found that if the number of filters is very high, then even a very low (0.00001) learning rate is not sufficient to cope with the instabilities and the training with high learning rate had a chance to destroy its effects. We found it much more reliable to just scale the residuals.

ctxs.title: Inception-v4, Inception-ResNet and the Impact of Residual Connections on Learning
ctxs.text: Even where the scaling was not strictly necessary, it never seemed to harm the final accuracy, but it helped to stabilize the training.

answer: The training was stabilised after scaling down the residuals before adding them to the previous layer activations [14]. Even when this scaling was not strictly necessary, it helped stabilise the training without affecting the accuracy [15]. But this paper does not answer why the training was unstable without these scaled activations [16].

input: What is the purpose of bounding box regression?
question: What is the purpose of bounding box regression?
output:
The provided excerpt describes a technical implementation detail within the R-FCN framework, specifically detailing how bounding box regression is addressed.

Based on the text, the purpose of bounding box regression is to **parameterize a bounding box** [1].

This is achieved through a specific process:
1. A sibling convolutional layer, which is $k^2 - d$ convolutional layer, is appended to the network structure [1].
2. Position-sensitive RoI pooling is performed on the resulting $k^2 - d$ maps, which produces a $k^2 - d$ vector for each Region of Interest (RoI) [1].
3. This vector is then aggregated into a $4d$ vector via average voting [1].
4. This final $4d$ vector then **parameterizes a bounding box** as $t = (t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$ [1].

The excerpt also notes that the implementation described is for **class-agnostic bounding box regression** for simplicity, though a class-specific version is also possible [1].

ctxs.title: R-FCN: Object Detection via Region-based Fully Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: We further address bounding box regression (7, 6) in a similar way. Aside from the above k2​(C+1)superscript𝑘2𝐶1k^{2}(C+1)-d convolutional layer, we append a sibling 4​k24superscript𝑘24k^{2}-d convolutional layer for bounding box regression. The position-sensitive RoI pooling is performed on this bank of 4​k24superscript𝑘24k^{2} maps, producing a 4​k24superscript𝑘24k^{2}-d vector for each RoI. Then it is aggregated into a 444-d vector by average voting. This 444-d vector parameterizes a bounding box as t=(tx,ty,tw,th)𝑡subscript𝑡𝑥subscript𝑡𝑦subscript𝑡𝑤subscript𝑡ℎt=(t_{x},t_{y},t_{w},t_{h}) following the parameterization in . We note that we perform class-agnostic bounding box regression for simplicity, but the class-specific counterpart (i.e., with a 4​k2​C4superscript𝑘2𝐶4k^{2}C-d output layer) is applicable.

answer: Bounding box regression is needed to find the coordinates of the bounding boxes of the objects within the RoI [11].

input:
What was the size of model obtained by applying Deep Compression 
to SqueezeNet, using 33% sparsity and 8-bit quantization?
question:
What was the size of model obtained by applying Deep Compression 
to SqueezeNet, using 33% sparsity and 8-bit quantization?
output: The size of the model obtained by applying Deep Compression to SqueezeNet, using 33% sparsity and 8-bit quantization, was **0.66 MB** [1].

ctxs.title: SqueezeNet: AlexNet-level accuracy with 50x fewer parameters and<0.5MB model size
ctxs.text: It appears that we have surpassed the state-of-the-art results from the model compression community: even when using uncompressed 32-bit values to represent the model, SqueezeNet has a 1.4×1.4\times smaller model size than the best efforts from the model compression community while maintaining or exceeding the baseline accuracy. Until now, an open question has been: are small models amenable to compression, or do small models “need” all of the representational power afforded by dense floating-point values? To find out, we applied Deep Compression Han et al. (2015a) to SqueezeNet, using 33% sparsity666Note that, due to the storage overhead of storing sparse matrix indices, 33% sparsity leads to somewhat less than a 3×3\times decrease in model size. and 8-bit quantization. This yields a 0.66 MB model (363×363\times smaller than 32-bit AlexNet) with equivalent accuracy to AlexNet. Further, applying Deep Compression with 6-bit quantization and 33% sparsity on SqueezeNet, we produce a 0.47MB model (510×510\times smaller than 32-bit AlexNet) with equivalent accuracy. Our small model is indeed amenable to compression.

answer: size after taking these considerations would be a 066 MB model 363× smaller than 32-bit AlexNet with equivalent accuracy to AlexNet [24].

input: Is there a reason of not realizing pre-processing techniques to the real data to remove noise before the training ?
question: Is there a reason of not realizing pre-processing techniques to the real data to remove noise before the training ?
output:
Based on the provided excerpts, the authors suggest that there is a specific reason why pre-processing techniques might not be strictly necessary or why their proposed methods are robust enough to handle noisy real-world data without extensive pre-processing.

The key points are:

* **Robustness to Real-World Data:** The authors explicitly state that their methods are designed to perform well on real-world scans, which are characterized as potentially being "highly partial, noisy, or oversmoothed" [2].
* **Benefit of Multi-Resolution Filtering:** The introduction of their "multi-resolution 3D filtering" is highlighted as a mechanism that helps achieve this robustness. They note that this filtering is effective on real-world data, and they hypothesize that "the low 3D resolution component filters out spurious and noisy micro-structures" [2].
* **View-Invariance and Regularization:** Furthermore, they mention that using spheres for discretization in their multi-resolution approach helps "regularize out potential noise or irregularities in real-world scanned data (relative to synthetic training data)," enabling "robust performance on real-world scans" [1].

In summary, the authors' design—specifically the **multi-resolution 3D filtering**—is intended to inherently handle the noise and irregularities present in real-world scans, thereby mitigating the absolute necessity of extensive pre-processing before training [1, 2].

ctxs.title: Volumetric and Multi-view CNNs for Object Classification on 3D Data
ctxs.text: Although the multi-view CNN presented by  produces compelling results, we are able to improve its performance through a multi-resolution extension with improved data augmentation. We introduce multi-resolution 3D filtering to capture information at multiple scales. We perform sphere rendering (see Sec 3) at different volume resolutions. Note that we use spheres for this discretization as they are view-invariant. In particular, this helps regularize out potential noise or irregularities in real-world scanned data (relative to synthetic training data), enabling robust performance on real-world scans. Note that our 3D multi-resolution filtering is different from classical 2D multi-resolution approaches, since the 3D filtering respects the distance in 3D.

ctxs.title: Volumetric and Multi-view CNNs for Object Classification on 3D Data
ctxs.text: We further assess the performance of volumetric CNNs and multi-view CNNs on real-world reconstructions in Table 4. All methods are trained on CAD models in ModelNet40 but tested on real data, which may be highly partial, noisy, or oversmoothed (Fig 6). Our networks continue to outperform state-of-the-art results. In particular, our 3D multi-resolution filtering is quite effective on real-world data, possibly because the low 3D resolution component filters out spurious and noisy micro-structures. Example results for object retrieval can be found in supplementary.

answer: The reason is that, in the real data, it will not always be possible to do the pre-processing steps, especially if they require tedious manual noise removal which cannot be completely done automatically [34]. Thus, by using the noisy dataset, the authors demonstrate that their model is robust to real-world noise and occlusions [53].

input: What are the examples of the high level features that separate the anatomical structures for lesions regions identification ?
question: What are the examples of the high level features that separate the anatomical structures for lesions regions identification ?
output:
The provided excerpt indicates that the network learns to identify specific anatomical structures, which serve as high-level features beneficial for lesion segmentation. Specifically, the network learns to differentiate between the **ventricles, CSF, white matter, and gray matter** [1].

The authors state that this ability to differentiate tissue types is beneficial for lesion segmentation, aligning with previous research that showed incorporating tissue priors significantly improved the performance of traditional classifiers [1]. Furthermore, the authors suggest that a rigorous analysis of the spatial cues extracted by the network could potentially reveal correlations that are not yet well defined regarding how different lesion types affect different parts of the brain based on their underlying pathology [1].

ctxs.title: Efficient multi‐scale 3D CNN with fully connected CRF for accurate brain lesion segmentation
ctxs.text: The discriminative power of the learned features is indicated by the success of recent CNN-based systems in matching human performance in domains where it was previously considered too ambitious (He et al. (2015); Silver et al. (2016)). Analysis of the automatically extracted information could potentially provide novel insights and facilitate research on pathologies for which little prior knowledge is currently available. In an attempt to illustrate this, we explore what patterns have been learned automatically for the lesion segmentation tasks. We visualize the activations of DeepMedic’s FMs when processing a subject from our TBI database. Many appearing patterns are difficult to interpret, especially in deeper layers. In Fig. 14 we provide some examples that have an intuitive explanation. One of the most interesting findings is that the network learns to identify the ventricles, CSF, white and gray matter. This reveals that differentiation of tissue type is beneficial for lesion segmentation. This is in line with findings in the literature, where segmentation performance of traditional classifiers was significantly improved by incorporation of tissue priors (Van Leemput et al. (1999); Zikic et al. (2012)). It is intuitive that different types of lesions affect different parts of the brain depending on the underlying mechanisms of the pathology. A rigorous analysis of spatial cues extracted by the network may reveal correlations that are not well defined yet.

answer: Figure 14 shows that the network learns to identify the ventricles, CSF, white and gray matter, with each filter identifying different tissue types, indicating that learning the differences in the features of different tissue types is helpful for lesion segmentation [72].
